[2022-12-31] (x) @rel_zeta_tech To clarify, just in case I misunderstood the question: computing the prover's message in each round of sum-check is embarrassingly parallel. But the prover can't start working on computing its round i message until it knows the round i-1 message.
[2022-12-31] (x) @rel_zeta_tech And again, the same log-round issue arises in FRI and Bulletproofs/IPA and I haven't encountered the suggestion that this is the key bottleneck in those. (6/6)
[2022-12-31] (x) @rel_zeta_tech This will also drop the size of the prover's data structure by factor 16 per round rather than a factor of 2, so the data structure will fit in memory after only a couple of rounds. The (standard) trick is to sum over [16]^{log_{16}(n)} rather than {0, 1}^{log_2(n)}. (5/6)
[2022-12-31] (x) @rel_zeta_tech If it really were a bottleneck, one can drop the round complexity at the cost of more prover work. E.g., one can drop the rounds by a factor of 4, increasing total prover field ops by a factor of about 2^4=16. (4/6)
[2022-12-31] (x) @rel_zeta_tech It wasn't the prover bottleneck in Zebra and Giraffe. Those were actually interactive, but waiting for the next verifier challenge to come from off-chip should only be slower than hashing the prover's (short) message. (3/6)
[2022-12-31] (x) @rel_zeta_tech But I think the focus on this as a possible bottleneck for sum-check is misguided for several reasons. (2/6)
[2022-12-31] (x) @rel_zeta_tech I don't think this should be possible. The whole point of Fiat-Shamir is that the next verifier challenge should be unpredictable until the prover's next message is known, and that verifier challenge is needed to update the sum-check-prover's data structure. (1/6)
[2022-12-31] (x) @bfarmer @weikengchen Even with standard hashes, I think there are entities that are conservative w/ security and won't use this tech given the state of our understanding. All implemented composed SNARKs have heuristic security or at least significant loss in concrete security can't be ruled out (7/7)
[2022-12-31] (x) @bfarmer @weikengchen Third, I wouldn't totally write off security considerations yet.  I hope you're right that the situation with SNARK-friendly hash functions is going to better soon. But while projects are deploying Poseidon with at most 95 bits of security, it's premature to relax on this. (6/7)
[2022-12-31] (x) @bfarmer @weikengchen Second, I'm not convinced all statements people will ever want to prove are easily broken into pieces represented by circuits of size ~2^24, without introducing major overhead. But I agree rollups are amenable--by definition they compress many simple statements/transactions (5/7)
[2022-12-31] (x) @bfarmer @weikengchen These are already getting applied to statements that would be massive if represented as a circuit. I'm not pushing this viewpoint, but think it's helpful in conveying why "special-purpose monolithic SNARKs" are not going to be subsumed by recursion. (4/7)
[2022-12-31] (x) @bfarmer @weikengchen BTW already-pervasive protocols like Bulletproofs/IPA can be viewed as special-purpose monolithic (sum-check-based!) SNARKs if you squint hard enough, see, e.g., Sum-Check Arguments (https://t.co/RhgmcRDc4s, Appendix A). (3/7)
[2022-12-31] (x) @bfarmer @weikengchen One that I think we'll see soon is neural network training/inference, the bottleneck in which is often repeated matrix multiplication of small integers. I'm working on some others that I hope people will find compelling. (2/7)
[2022-12-31] (x) @bfarmer @weikengchen A few things. First, some critical applications are amenable to super-efficient protocols that entirely avoid circuits or other intermediate representations, and this will lead to night-and-day prover performance improvements. (1/7)
[2022-12-31] (x) I’d love to see a @hubermanlab style explanation for why narrative and story are such powerful tools in convincing, connecting, and mobilizing groups of people. 

Anyone have ideas?

[2023-01-01] (x) In a duck-duck-goose game with 2022 + 2023 = 4045 players, the 4045th player wins. 

The next year transition when this happens is hundreds of thousands of years away – New Year's Eve, 590550. https://t.co/Pg06jHJZbh
[2023-01-01] (x) 2022 + 2023 = 4045 is the sum of the 18th prime squared and the 18th perfect square:

61^2 + 18^2 = 4045.
[2023-01-01] (x) And @catherinehyeo points out that 2023 = (1 + 2 + 3 + 4 5 6 - 7) * (8 + 9). 🙌https://t.co/fWfCSRmhdt
[2023-01-01] (x) Bonus: Noam Elkies tells me 2023 is the dimension of a projective space that receives a cubic Veronese embedding. (It's apparently the *only* year that both has both that property and is a sevenfold square 🤯)
[2023-01-01] (x) 2023 is also the number of tilings of a 4x4 board with 1x1 tiles and right trominoes. There are 195 such tilings of a 3x4 board, and 16839 such tilings of a 4x5 board (and 39 for a 3x3 board, and 249651 for a 5x5 one).
[2023-01-01] (x) And 2023 divides the right-concatenation of all numbers less than or equal to 2023 written in base 8. The last year with the analogous property was 1449, and the next one is 7223. https://t.co/LqReB1j1xD
[2023-01-01] (x) And 2023 is the number of binary strings of length 14 with no substrings of the form 0001, 0011, or 1100. https://t.co/b40hCuR3xA
[2023-01-01] (x) Plus 2023 is the smallest number greater than 34 that cannot be scored using 34 darts on a standard dartboard. https://t.co/baKlFhBPVD
[2023-01-01] (x) And 2023 is apparently the number of 10x10 binary arrays, symmetric across both diagonals, with every 1 adjacent to at least one other 1 both bishopwise and rookwise, but with no three 1s in a row bishopwise or rookwise. https://t.co/mlxLqrrw2V
[2023-01-01] (x) Plus 2023 is equal to the alternating sum of the first 14 centered octagonal pyramidal numbers.

(No GIFs of centered octagonal pyramidal numbers either 🤔)
[2023-01-01] (x) The sixth power of 2023 can be written as a sum of seven sixth powers:

2023^6 = 68544923587844151889 = 1136^6 + 345^6 + 1902^6 + 1548^6 + 1320^6 + 240^6 + 30^6. 

2009 had this property as well; prior to that, it hadn't happened since 1757. The next such year is 2471. https://t.co/IJdjbmIUP1
[2023-01-01] (x) And 2023 is also the number of edge covers in the 7-wheel graph. 

The 6-wheel graph has 562 edge covers, and the 8-wheel graph has 7240. https://t.co/kTgCYYRPSQ
[2023-01-01] (x) Moreover, 2023 is a concentric heptagonal number; the last such year was 1905, and the next is 2143.

(Why are there no GIFs of concentric heptagons??) https://t.co/DlObjJg10w
[2023-01-01] (x) The numbers (6 × 2023^11) - 1 and  (6 × 2023^11) + 1 (i.e., 13934967522481641918015914989909151561 and 13934967522481641918015914989909151563, respectively) are twin primes. https://t.co/mZi9gnI1g6
[2023-01-01] (x) Plus the concatenation of 2023's prime factors is a palindrome: 71717.
[2023-01-01] (x) And 2023 is the remainder when 7^7 is divided by 7! (here, the exclamation point denotes the factorial). 

(In case you're curious, dividing 8^8 by 8! leaves remainder 4096, which happens to be a power of 2.) https://t.co/UtVvQpShcG
[2023-01-01] (x) And the last nonprime year whose prime factors contained no digits other than 1 and 7 was 1331 (not quite 1337, unfortunately).
[2023-01-01] (x) 2023 is also the first year since 803 such that the sum of its digits equals its least prime factor and the sum of the squares of its digits equals its largest prime factor. The next such year is 106811. https://t.co/OEOOescAUs
[2023-01-01] (x) Even just the fact that the sum of 2023's digits equals its squarefree part is rare – that last happened in 1815, and occurs next in 2400.
[2023-01-01] (x) ... and there are only a few more years like that ever: the next is 2400, followed by 52215, 615627, 938600, and 1648656.
[2023-01-01] (x) 2023 is equal to the sum of its digits times the square of the sum of its digits squared (try saying that 2023 times fast):

2023 = (2 + 0 + 2 + 3) × (2^2 + 0^2 + 2^2 + 3^2)^2 

This is the first such year since 1 CE... https://t.co/6wA3GKbEO1

[2023-01-03] (x) It's a somewhat bizarre analogy given the context, but this is the same sort of problem that motivates online job boards and dating sites, not to mention other social meetup platforms -- it's really hard to solve a matching problem when you don't know who else is looking.
[2023-01-03] (x) I am extremely Covid-cautious. Over the last three years, I've had similarly cautious friends reach out to me because of my public posts and writing on the pandemic. But in most of those cases I would otherwise have had no way of knowing that these people were being careful too!
[2023-01-03] (x) ... especially since one's level of need to take precaution often depends on information that we typically keep private, like personal health status.
[2023-01-03] (x) This approach really appeals to me from the perspective of a market designer. 

There's a matching problem to solve here -- lots of people need (or at least want) to avoid Covid, but it's hard to know who the like-minded people are...
[2023-01-03] (x) As the set of people still taking precautions against Covid dwindles, if you're being careful -- and especially if you're at high risk -- finding people it's safe to socialize with becomes a hard matching problem.

Enter @covidmeetupscom, a platform trying to solve this:

[2023-01-11] (x) @Orlando_btc @BrianQuintenz Thanks. Upcoming installments will focus on practical application to types of apps (wallets, websites, etc.) as well as proposed regs. [2023-01-11] (x) ++/ Finally, if you’re looking for background on why we should be regulating apps and not protocols (aka businesses, not software), here’s a link to Part 1: https://t.co/era7X1Fx9y
[2023-01-11] (x) +/ Thanks for reading. Feedback is welcomed - the feedback we received from the community (both anons and doxxed) on the first piece and this Part 2 has been enormously helpful. https://t.co/GfvOIkiiAU
[2023-01-11] (x) 22/ So, using the RANP framework as a foundation, we plan to continue to engage with policymakers and regulators to establish clear policy objectives that are calibrated to ensure the societal benefits of web3 technology far exceed its costs.
[2023-01-11] (x) 21/ However, the strength of these arguments remains uncertain.

As a result, it’d be foolish for web3 actors to refuse to engage in policymaking or to reject all regulation on the basis that the Constitution will protect web3, as that protection may not end up materializing.
[2023-01-11] (x) 20/3⃣Constitutional Implications.

The constitutional implications of applying regulation to web3 apps must also be considered as there are valid arguments that the 1st and 4th amendments could ultimately protect web3 from certain types of regulation (eg, KYC reqs in DeFi).
[2023-01-11] (x) 19/🔸Example: From CFTC enforcement actions one might expect differing treatment for a frontend purpose built for trading derivatives vs. a frontend for a general DEX, while a simple block explorer (e.g., Etherscan) should receive the greatest leniency. https://t.co/IFPMHwHxYT
[2023-01-11] (x) 18/🔹B: However, even where a web3 app is not operated for profit, the app’s primary purpose may matter, potentially significantly, for regulatory purposes. 

If the app’s primary purpose is to facilitate the to-be-regulated activity, it is likely such regulation will apply.
[2023-01-11] (x) 17/ Where web3 apps are not for profit, the case for leniency is even more compelling - without a profit motive, the incentive to facilitate illicit activity is removed. 

So, where web3 apps are run as public goods, the benefits of leniency are more likely to outweigh the costs.
[2023-01-11] (x) 16/ Nevertheless, greater flexibility for nascent for-profit web3 apps may be justifiable because web3 protocols serve as public infrastructure (similar to email) and flexible app regs could increase protocol development and empower developers to self-fund such progress.
[2023-01-11] (x) 15/🔸Example: Permitting businesses to charge commissions on unlawful trading of securities/derivatives could reasonably be expected to lead to an increase in such trading.

In fact, aiding and abetting laws already use this argument as a central tenet.
[2023-01-11] (x) 14/🔹A: If the app is operated for profit, there's a presumption it should be subject to the reg b/c: (i) there could be an increased risk from COIs and (ii) allowing businesses to profit from facilitating illicit activity could lead to an increase in such illicit activity.
[2023-01-11] (x) 13/ 2⃣ Characteristics. 

Where web3 tech doesn’t obviate a reg’s purpose, the framework looks at two primary web3 app characteristics: 
🔹A: Whether the app is operated by a business for profit; and 
🔹B: Whether the app’s primary purpose is to facilitate the regulated activity.
[2023-01-11] (x) 12/🔸Example: It’s difficult to say that a for-profit centralized business should be prohibited from earning commissions on trades of securities/derivatives, but that a for-profit business facilitating access to decentralized infrastructure (eg, a DEX) should be allowed to do so.
[2023-01-11] (x) 11/ However, decentralization doesn’t obviate the purpose of all regs. Where the same risks and considerations apply to centralized and decentralized businesses, the default position will likely be that the rules should be consistent absent some overriding policy objective.
[2023-01-11] (x) 10/ So, it’s unnecessary to apply those regs to many DeFi apps, where the decentralization, transparency, and trustlessness of blockchain tech can protect users better than any self-regulatory or public regulatory regime in CeFi ever could.
https://t.co/oc6gam75ZB
[2023-01-11] (x) 9/🔸Example: Decentralization is a key benefit enabled by web3 technology that has significant regulatory implications, including obviating the need for many intermediary-focused regs that seek to address risks associated with conflicts of interest (COIs).
[2023-01-11] (x) 8/ 1⃣ Policy Objectives. 

The framework starts by looking at what the policy objectives of a regulation are and whether they’re obviated by the technology that underpins web3.
 
If so, we argue that such regulation should obviously not be applied.
[2023-01-11] (x) 7/ Now for some specifics - The three interrelated factors the framework analyzes are:

1⃣ Policy objectives
2⃣ Characteristics of web3 apps
3⃣ Constitutional implications
[2023-01-11] (x) 6/ Ultimately, we must apply regulation carefully and limit the creation of unnecessary gatekeepers to support innovation and preserve the benefits of web3.

We hope the framework helps find that balance and contributes to the ongoing respectful, open, good-intentioned discourse.
[2023-01-11] (x) 5/ Critically, that balance shouldn’t necessitate that we eliminate all potential uses of web3 technology for illicit activity - we don’t regulate SMTP just b/c email can be used for crime.

But it does require measures that disincentivize and reduce the risk of such activity.
[2023-01-11] (x) 4/ Neither of these extremes are viable. Both require a significant departure from historical norms. 
 
We suggest a more measured reassessment of existing regulatory schemes based on a deep understanding of web3 technology and a delicate balancing of policy objectives.
[2023-01-11] (x) 3/ Ultimately, we try to find a defensible middle ground between two extreme ideological positions gaining popularity in the wake of FTX - the wholesale application of existing regulatory regimes to web3 and the exclusion of web3 from all regulation.
[2023-01-11] (x) 2/ This Part 2 builds on our approach to regulating web3 businesses (not software), exploring how various factors should influence web3 app regulation and incorporating great feedback on our initial post from September.

Here’s a flowchart of the framework: https://t.co/R2JeoyDJZj
[2023-01-11] (x) 1/ This year comes with a renewed sense of urgency regarding web3 regulation. So, today @BrianQuintenz and I published Part 2 of the @a16zcrypto “Regulate Web3 Apps, Not Protocols” framework. 

Here’s a quick guide. 🧵👇
https://t.co/GfvOIkiiAU

[2023-01-12] (x) @david_r_barrera @skominers @Jad_AE @a16zcrypto It's a chicken-or-the-egg paradox: Total decentralization may necessitate the use of digital assets, but the use of digital assets necessitates decentralization.

[2023-01-12] (x) Great addition to the progressive decentralization canon.

[2023-01-12] (x) While designing with decentralization in mind can be more costly, it can become a source of competitive advantage. 

If two competing products both have to be decentralized eventually, the one that's better set up for it upfront is likely to be the winner. https://t.co/rEm7J3jg7i
[2023-01-12] (x) Alternatively, you might choose to increase decentralization entirely of your own volition – one instance could be that you see long-term business value in storing content in a decentralized way, and so you make the active choice to start using such a service.
[2023-01-12] (x) You might get pushed to decentralize on a specific dimension – for example, you might have too much user-generated content to manage on your own, making it critical to start involving the wider community in curation.
[2023-01-12] (x) There will be push and pull factors on individual MDUs as well.
[2023-01-12] (x) Ideally, you would also make the move at a time when the social and economic dynamics of your platform have stabilized enough that you can robustly predict how adjusting the level of decentralization will affect community behavior and outcomes.
[2023-01-12] (x) Any form of organizational change will slow down the operation. You want to time moving a slider so that the long-run benefit of slowing down outweighs the short-run cost/risks.
[2023-01-12] (x) First, it’s important that your overall system be relatively stable. 

@jessewldn encourages teams to assess where they are on the journey to/past product-market fit: How many more iterations do you still need to go through, and how quickly? https://t.co/8RDP174lOR
[2023-01-12] (x) And how do you know when it’s time to move the slider up?
[2023-01-12] (x) Visually, we think of this as like a set of slider bars – a “decentralization equalizer,” with a different adjustment for each MDU. 

You can slide each bar up at its own pace, and the difficulty of sliding a bar depends on the business’s readiness for change on that dimension. https://t.co/WMYxyCe01l
[2023-01-12] (x) And then even within a given MDU, you don’t have to go from 0 to 100 all at once. A platform might gradually decentralize curation, say, by first soliciting content recommendations from the community, before eventually turning over content decisions entirely.
[2023-01-12] (x) First, identify the different dimensions along which your business can become decentralized. 

Segment it into “minimum decentralizable units” (MDUs) that are mostly independent from one another. MDUs might include the core team, external contributors, the tech stack, and so on.
[2023-01-12] (x) Decentralization needn’t be “all-or-nothing.” But you have to plan in advance: https://t.co/rEm7J3iIhK
[2023-01-12] (x) Decentralization is core to web3, but starting out entirely decentralized can be impractical because centralization at early stages can make it easier to coordinate, launch, and rapidly iterate toward product-market fit. 

Nevertheless, you can adjust that over time!
[2023-01-12] (x) If your operational model starts out centralized, is it doomed to stay that way?

Not at all! 

Progressive decentralization is possible, as @Jad_AE and I discuss today in @a16zcrypto: https://t.co/rEm7J3iIhK

🧵👇

[2023-01-24] (x) Crypto protocols can have powerful rules built into them. If those rules are designed properly to protect users, we can decrease the need for regulation.

This is one of the greatest promises of crypto.
[2023-01-24] (x) @MeatTC_ You can change the value flows to eliminate incentives that run counter to policy objectives without sacrificing any of the fundamental principles that give protocols utility - open source, decentralized, autonomous, standardized, censorship resistant and permissionless.
[2023-01-24] (x) @fubuloubu It depends on where they draw the line of whether  you're facilitating the unlawful activity or not. I wouldn't expect Congress to legislate with that level of detail you're proposing, so the question will likely be left up to the discretion of regulators and the courts.
[2023-01-24] (x) @MeatTC_ If they're allowed to profit from the facilitation of regulated activity unlawfully, then DAOs would be incentivized to facilitate such activity which would run counter to the policy objectives behind the regulation. So, the point of Part 3 is to remove the perverse incentive.
[2023-01-24] (x) @MeatTC_ It would be, which is exactly why we don't want regulation to hold DAOs responsible for the functioning of protocols they don't control. But if DAOs are to be excluded, they can't then also be allowed to profit from the facilitation of regulated activity unlawfully.
[2023-01-24] (x) @NelsonMRosario @fubuloubu In the context of SEFs, I'd say that the frontend of Ooki does facilitate the trading of swaps, a frontend to a DEX doesn't (even though swaps could be traded through the DEX), and a block explorer certainly doesn't.
[2023-01-24] (x) @fubuloubu MM collects fees on routing swaps through DEXs, yeah?

Point is, if you collect fees from a protocol that's facilitating regulated activity unlawfully (either via a frontend or through a DAO/governance token), you're no longer in "this is just speech" territory.
[2023-01-24] (x) @NelsonMRosario @fubuloubu It's surprising to you that Congress uses ambiguous words that are open to interpretation?
[2023-01-24] (x) @NelsonMRosario @fubuloubu The interesting thing is that in interpreting "facilitate" the CFTC has taken different approaches depending on whether the tools were purpose built or general purpose.  This is why in Part 2 of our framework, we included an assessment of the primary purpose of the app. https://t.co/LAhg589FFP
[2023-01-24] (x) @NelsonMRosario @fubuloubu I wouldn't expect Congress to define it and would leave it up to regulators to interpret it, as they did with the Commodities Exchange Act.  Here's the definition of SEF: https://t.co/H1CKbhdHt6
[2023-01-24] (x) @fubuloubu I'd say no and think the Supreme Court would likely say such a restriction is unconstitutional. But there's a distinction between your fact pattern and one where a developer deploys a protocol that facilitates regulated activity unlawfully and collects a fee on every transaction.
[2023-01-24] (x) @fubuloubu No one is saying that developers can't deploy code or that such activity should be regulated. It shouldn't be. But if a developer deploys code that facilitates regulated activity and they receive profits from that facilitation, that isn't speech...
[2023-01-24] (x) @fubuloubu No. Regulation of frontends should be paired with narrowly-tailored regs of DAOs to prohibit them from profiting from non-compliant frontends (where frontends are regulated), as opposed to regulation of DAOs that prohibits their use of protocols to facilitate regulated activity.
[2023-01-24] (x) @MeatTC_ Whether a DAO operates a protocol would depend on facts and circumstances, but even where it has no control, it's hard to imagine governments standing idly by as DAOs profited from illicit activity. Enabling them to do so would incentivize DAOs to facilitate such activity.
[2023-01-24] (x) @MeatTC_ Agree that getting DAOs to care about compliance is an uphill battle. But the DVA model could be automated once implemented, such that the DAO doesn't get to decide whether or not it receives profits from non-compliant apps.
[2023-01-24] (x) @fubuloubu Think you should read the articles. Arguing that regulation shouldn't apply because it's challenging to apply or because blockchains are just communications software isn't a winning position.
[2023-01-24] (x) @fubuloubu The problem with the DCCPA wasn't that it just targeted frontends...It's that it was going to hold DAOs accountable for what protocols enabled, effectively regulating protocols.
[2023-01-24] (x) ++/ Finally, if you’re looking for Parts 1 and 2:
Part 1: https://t.co/era7X1Fx9y 
Part 2: https://t.co/GfvOIkiiAU
[2023-01-24] (x) +/ Thanks for reading. The full text of Part 3 can be found here: https://t.co/DlS4UNXIjx 

Feedback is welcomed/appreciated — the feedback we received from the community on Parts 1 and 2 has been enormously helpful.
[2023-01-24] (x) 11/ With this 3rd installment of RANP, we have a complete foundation for a regulation-agnostic web3  framework. 

We'll now seek to use the framework as the basis for protecting web3 protocols from the coming wave of rules and regulation.
[2023-01-24] (x) 10/ Safeguards like these could eliminate incentives to circumvent regulation and create incentives to encourage use of regulated apps, all while preserving the fundamental principles of protocols. 

That means app-based regulation can succeed without sacrificing web3 freedoms!
[2023-01-24] (x) 9/ For example, a hypothetical app-based regulation that prohibits trading of tokenized derivatives using web3 apps could be paired with safeguards that prohibit a DAO from profiting from such trading and from engaging in activities designed to increase such trading.
[2023-01-24] (x) 8/ Instead, we propose a novel approach — pairing app-based regulations with additional narrowly-tailored safeguards that limit incentive structures and restrict DAO activities that would otherwise undermine the policy objectives of app-based regulations. https://t.co/LuB6lp0O5M
[2023-01-24] (x) 7/ The most obvious solution to the dilemma is to make web3 protocols DAO-less. But, that would be a grave mistake — DAOs provide several critical benefits that underpin web3’s core value proposition. 

Such a drastic step is also unnecessary.
[2023-01-24] (x) 6/ Ultimately, these characteristics enable bad actors to operate DAOs like businesses to circumvent app-based regulatory schemes (the exact issue in CFTC v. Ooki). But, applying app regulations to DAOs would destroy the utility of protocols. 

This is the Web3 DAO Dilemma: https://t.co/MEPnJILVGi
[2023-01-24] (x) 5/ But, web3 protocols can be designed to accrue value (fees, commissions, etc.) and be semi-autonomous. 

So, the opposite is true. The DAO of a web3 protocol could be set up to profit from illicit uses of the protocol, and be incentivized to facilitate such illicit use.
[2023-01-24] (x) 4/ Web1 protocols do not accrue value and are fully autonomous. 

So, no one profits from the general use of the email protocol (SMTP) and no one is empowered to act on SMTP’s behalf, meaning SMTP’s functioning does not incentivize anyone to facilitate illicit uses of SMTP.
[2023-01-24] (x) 3/ The RANP framework relies heavily on using web1 as an analogy for web3 — but this analogy can only go so far because the unique characteristics of web3 protocols make them more susceptible to misuse by bad actors circumventing app-based regulation.
[2023-01-24] (x) 2/ Part 3 covers:
1⃣ the Web3 DAO Dilemma
2⃣ the importance of DAOs
3⃣ how to solve the Web3 DAO Dilemma with regulatory safeguards
4⃣ how to design compliant protocol and decentralized governance while preserving the fundamental principles of protocols.

[2023-01-25] (x) 13/ For more on value accrual mechanisms and decentralized governance designs that can help to reduce the risk of regulatory enforcement while preserving the fundamental principles of protocols, check out Part 3 of the @a16zcrypto RANP series: https://t.co/DlS4UNXIjx
[2023-01-25] (x) 12/ However, the DVA model should not be extended beyond DAO value accrual. Applying it to the value that accrues to a protocol’s users (eg, liquidity providers) doesn’t work. It would make the protocol censorable and subject to attack by bad actors.
[2023-01-25] (x) 11/ Ultimately, where regulation is applied to web3, it’s likely going to be critical for protocol developers to protect DAOs from profiting from illicit uses of their protocols, and mechanisms like the DVA can be used  to accomplish this.
[2023-01-25] (x) 10/ The DVA model can also significantly reduce the regulatory compliance burdens of DAOs, who would no longer need to assess the legality of fee switches on a per transaction or liquidity pool basis.

Lower reg compliance burden ultimately facilitates greater decentralization.
[2023-01-25] (x) 9/ Using DVA, a protocol could continue to be permissionless and censorship resistant, while removing incentives for its DAO to facilitate the circumvention of the applicable regulation. This supports the policy objectives of such reg and reduces the need for protocol-based regs.
[2023-01-25] (x) 8/ The default pool would be controlled by the DAO and used however the DAO wanted. 

The fees in the blacklist pool would not be controlled by the DAO and would be forfeited to a local government agency or the SRO overseeing the protocol.
[2023-01-25] (x) 7/ However, if the DAO received a notice from a government agency or self-regulatory organization (SRO) that an app was not compliant, the fees accruing to the DAO from the non-compliant app would be directed to the blacklist pool by the DAO. https://t.co/e999cSKfTb
[2023-01-25] (x) 6/ Using GSCs, the protocol would use a “Default Value Accrual” (DVA) model in which fees accruing to the DAO would be directed to: 
🔹a default pool, or 
🔸a blacklist pool

All fees from transactions initiated from a specific app would, by default, go to the default pool.
[2023-01-25] (x) 5/ Each app would then interact with the protocol via its own separate and unique GSC, with each transaction being authenticated by the app’s private key associated with such GSC. 

The protocol retains no censorship rights with respect to the GSCs.

Here’s a simplified diagram: https://t.co/H9ve2uFdXE
[2023-01-25] (x) 4/ To achieve this, a protocol could use a “factory” smart contract that deploys separate, protocol-controlled gateway smart contracts (GSCs) for each app seeking to interact with the protocol. 

App developers could permissionlessly trigger the “factory” smart contract.
[2023-01-25] (x) 3/🟩Solution: Eliminate the value accrual to DAOs from activity routed through non-compliant apps that circumvent regulations. 

This can be accomplished by introducing a new type of app-specific protocol design that can distinguish transactions based on the app initiating them.
[2023-01-25] (x) 2/🟥Problem: DAOs are a potential loophole in app-based regulations, so we need to eliminate incentives for DAOs to circumvent regulations, without sacrificing the open source, decentralized, autonomous, standardized, censorship resistant and permissionless nature of protocols.
[2023-01-25] (x) (1/13) Here’s how protocol design can help future app-based regulations achieve their policy objectives without compromising the fundamental principles of web3 protocols. 🧵 https://t.co/DlS4UNXIjx
[2023-01-25] (x) @mariorz @lay2000lbs @revertfinance If a human is involved, a system can't be trustless. I'm all for a less paternal approach, but I can also understand how policymakers and regulators might not get comfortable with users being responsible for verifying their transactions before they sign them.
[2023-01-25] (x) @mariorz @lay2000lbs @revertfinance You keep changing the characteristics of the example frontends you're using. As I've said, if risks don't exist with respect to an app, then the purpose of the regulation addressing those risks is negated and the reg shouldn't apply.
[2023-01-25] (x) @mariorz @lay2000lbs @revertfinance It was deliberate. In your example, the app is no longer trustless and you've reintroduced non-custodial risks like conflicts of interest. That isn't currently regulated, but if it were, we'd say the reg should apply at the app level (trusted) not the protocol level (trustless).
[2023-01-25] (x) @mariorz @lay2000lbs @revertfinance Protocols are global and can't comply with globally conflicting laws. Frontends are local and can comply. Plus, unless the frontend is decentralized/autonomous, it could be susceptible to manipulation by the human that controls it, which creates conflicts of interest risk.
[2023-01-25] (x) @mariorz @lay2000lbs @revertfinance Per Part 2, if a frontend is decentralized/autonomous and eliminates the risks that a regulation is meant address (for instance, conflicts of interest), then the reg shouldn't apply.

Throughout the series we refer to businesses operating apps, which assumes human control.
[2023-01-25] (x) @mariorz @lay2000lbs @revertfinance Custodial risks are not the only risks that exist that exist in the financial products and services world... https://t.co/JciGeHMDSY
[2023-01-25] (x) @mariorz @lay2000lbs @revertfinance The difference between a DeFi frontend and Etherscan is that Etherscan is a general tool that is not purpose-built to facilitate a regulated activity. If a DeFi frontend is purpose-built to facilitate a regulated activity, it'll likely be subject to regulation.  This isn't new. https://t.co/9NqFDh48B3
[2023-01-25] (x) @mariorz @lay2000lbs @revertfinance Frontends are typically owned and operated by companies, and given that they service users of a particular jurisdiction, they typically seek to comply with that jurisdiction's laws. 

Protocols are borderless and can't be subject to conflicting laws across jurisdictions.
[2023-01-25] (x) @mariorz @lay2000lbs @revertfinance If the law only targeted custodial risks then the DEX frontend shouldn't be subject to it, as the risk doesn't exist. But if the law targeted other risks applicable to both CEXs and DEX frontends, it'd be hard to argue for different treatment (other than as I've done in Part 2).
[2023-01-25] (x) @mariorz @lay2000lbs @revertfinance However, if the U.S. decided to approve a regulation that all digital asset trading facilities had to be registered in order to protect consumers, then I think there's a strong presumption that such a regulation would likely apply to the biggest/most profitable DEX frontends.
[2023-01-25] (x) @mariorz @lay2000lbs @revertfinance No one is suggesting an end to permissionless innovation. And if you read Part 2, you'll see that I make a case that the policy objective of fostering innovation could very well outweigh the policy objective of protecting consumers. https://t.co/ffOdJhRf7e
[2023-01-25] (x) @mariorz @lay2000lbs @revertfinance Further, there's no part of the framework that would impact the fundamental principles of protocols - which must be open source, decentralized, autonomous, standardized, censorship resistant and permissionless in order to foster an open, free, and credibly neutral Internet.
[2023-01-25] (x) @mariorz @lay2000lbs @revertfinance The entire framework is actually regulation agnostic. We do not take a position as to whether regulation is good or bad (other than when it doesn't have a purpose). The framework is instead advocating for an approach to applying regulation, if regulation must be applied.
[2023-01-25] (x) @mariorz @lay2000lbs @revertfinance I agree as well. In fact that's the main argument we make in Part 2 of the framework - Regulation should have a legitimate purpose and shouldn't be applied just for regulation's sake.

[2023-01-29] (x) @samlafer @drakefjustin Perhaps it would be clearer to call FRI and related polynomial commitments (like Ligero and Brakedown) "hashing-based" rather than "IOP-based", to clarify that the only cryptographic machinery in them is Merkle-hashing and Fiat-Shamir. (8/8)
[2023-01-29] (x) @samlafer @drakefjustin The IOP is the information-theoretic component of the FRI polynomial commitment and Merkle-hashing+Fiat-Shamir is the cryptographic component. Here, "standard" IOP" means P sends large vectors (instead of polynomials) and V reads just a handful of coordinates of the vectors (7/8)
[2023-01-29] (x) @samlafer @drakefjustin This is the case for FRI. The polynomial commitment scheme is obtained by first designing a "standard" (not "polynomial") IOP for proving that a claimed evaluation is consistent with the committed polynomial. One turns the IOP into a SNARK via Merkle-hashing+Fiat-Shamir. (6/8)
[2023-01-29] (x) @samlafer @drakefjustin The polynomial IOP is often called the "information-theoretic component" of the SNARK, and the polynomial commitment scheme the "cryptographic component". But polynomial commitment schemes themselves sometimes have both information-theoretic and cryptographic components. (5/8)
[2023-01-29] (x) @samlafer @drakefjustin A polynomial commitment scheme is exactly what one needs to turn a polynomial-IOP into a succinct argument. Rather than P explicitly sending large polynomial(s), the polynomial commitment scheme lets P succinctly commit to them and later reveal any requested evaluations. (4/8)
[2023-01-29] (x) @samlafer @drakefjustin In a polynomial IOP, V doesn't want to read the entire description of the polynomials that P sends, since that would take too much time (time at least n). What V is allowed to do is request the evaluation of the polynomial(s) at a handful of points. (3/8)
[2023-01-29] (x) @samlafer @drakefjustin A polynomial IOP is a protocol where the prover P can send one or more large polynomials to the verifier V. By a large polynomial, I mean, say, p(x)=a_0 + a_1*x + a_2 * x^2 +...+ a_n*x^n, where n is as big as the circuit for which P claims to know a satisfying assignment. (2/8)
[2023-01-29] (x) @samlafer @drakefjustin Good question. I hope this can be clarified in a single thread. Here goes:

Most SNARKs are obtained by combining a "polynomial IOP" with a "polynomial commitment" scheme. (1/8)

[2023-02-06] (x) 12/ Negotiate. Investors won't lose respect for you or pull an offer because you negotiate. If you are a strong and tough, but respectful, negotiator then an investor will be both impressed, and often willing to concede on some points.
[2023-02-06] (x) 11/ Do references. Investors will reference you, you should reference them. Get references from the investor and do your own backdoor references. Talk with founders who’ve succeeded and failed after working with the investor to get a realistic picture.
[2023-02-06] (x) 10/ Find good lawyers and advocate for yourself. The most expensive lawyer is a cheap lawyer that does a bad job.
[2023-02-06] (x) 9/ Slow down. Many first time founders seem to get pressured into accepting the first offer they receive. When a VC makes an offer they will often create artificial pressure and encourage you to make a hasty decision. Offers almost never get revoked or "explode."
[2023-02-06] (x) 8/ Be persistent. If you're not taking it personally (5) and are self confident (4), this is much easier.
[2023-02-06] (x) 7/ Never lie. There is a difference between presenting a charitable interpretation of reality and outright lying. People seem to lie because they’re struggling. If you lie people will avoid working with you, so lying will ironically only make things harder.
[2023-02-06] (x) 6/ Start with builders/angels you respect in a similar category. These people will often understand your product intuitively, connect you with good investors, and give you solid advice.  Again on (4), you're not wasting their time, you're giving them a gift.
[2023-02-06] (x) 5/ Don't take it personally. When you decide to fundraise a lot of people will pass. Many of these rejections are unrelated to you. To see this in another context note that a judge’s blood sugar is predictive of parole rates. https://t.co/czxvcK5GRa
[2023-02-06] (x) 4/ Be confident. Many first time founders are too self conscious. Don’t worry about wasting someone’s time. If you're an exceptional founder then spending time with you is a gift, not a burden.
[2023-02-06] (x) 3/ Have a team slide. Focus on what makes you (the founders) exceptional. At the early stages investors are almost entirely investing in you as a person and your ideas are only a conduit through which to assess your ability as a founder.
[2023-02-06] (x) 2/ Tell a story. On average humans do better with narratives than with facts. Humans are highly fallible and easily distracted, especially over Zoom. Without losing sincerity make your pitch/conversation engaging and interesting.

[2023-02-09] (x) @david_r_barrera Obviously, not impossible depending on the facts and circumstances, but the SEC hasn't provided any guidance on this subject in four years, despite the industry massively changing over that time period.
[2023-02-09] (x) @david_r_barrera I think we've only seen Howey successfully applied to issuers that sold tokens to investors, where the clear economic reality of the transactions at issue was that the issuer was fundraising to develop the product tied to the tokens. It's harder to say that about secondaries.
[2023-02-09] (x) Saber rattling about going after staking-as-a-service providers is a transparent attempt to exert greater leverage over exchanges.

"Do what we say or we'll sue you" posturing when you don't have clear legal authority is disgraceful and against the core mission of the SEC.
[2023-02-09] (x) @david_r_barrera This makes application of Howey to secondaries difficult, because there isn't even contractual privity with the issuer. Further, no case law supports the application of Howey to secondaries.

I'm not saying the courts won't find Howey applies, it's just a significant grey area.
[2023-02-09] (x) @david_r_barrera Well no, it's not. A share entitles to you to certain statutory rights and contractual rights under the entity's foundational documents. Investment contracts have no statutory rights and only entitle you to contractual rights if there's an actual contract.
[2023-02-09] (x) @david_r_barrera I’m not saying that’s an unreasonable perspective, but it has no supporting case law and it results in a massive expansion of the SEC’s authority. I think the best argument is that such an expansion would be inappropriate without congressional mandate.

[2023-03-06] (x) Got bitten by @TheBitten_, and now have an economic theory paper about vampire attacks. 👇👇 https://t.co/BjaK9uX6zD
[2023-03-06] (x) PPS #EconTwitter: @Tim_Org informed me that there is in fact an economics literature on the other type of vampire attack 🧛: https://t.co/LZ1AGSo01z
[2023-03-06] (x) And reminder: None of this is investment, business, legal, or tax advice. Please see https://t.co/QO4Df8iENI for more important information, including a link to a list of the firm’s investments. QED.
[2023-03-06] (x) ... Natalia Rigol, April Roth, Ben Roth, @Tim_Roughgarden, @bryanroutledge, @Tim_Org, @BlobWalker, @carrawu, and @alive_!
[2023-03-06] (x) Shoutouts to @BernsteinShai, @ccatalini, @smc90, Lauren Cohen, @cdixon, Piotr Dworczak, @Jad_AE, @joshgans, @zgggray, @hannahalaburda, @0xMasonH, @milesjennings, @MicheleKorver, @sriramk, @eddylazzarin, @Collin_McCune, @kimbatronic, ...
[2023-03-06] (x) PS: Any ideas for alternate names for the “vampire attack” concept? The name is evocative, but doesn’t on its face sound like something you’d necessarily want to help enable 😅.
[2023-03-06] (x) QED. If you enjoyed reading this thread, please consider sharing it: https://t.co/T0Uh4ko3p1 (And of course, please reply or reach out if you have comments!)
[2023-03-06] (x) But overall, our analysis suggests that vampire attacks may contribute to increased – and hopefully virtuous – platform competition. For how this might change the landscape of the web, check out this @CompPolicyInt article with @ccatalini: https://t.co/oaYzKkiTYI
[2023-03-06] (x) Moreover, as @BernsteinShai and I discussed in @HarvardBiz, the competition induced by vampire attacks can only benefit consumers if it doesn’t drive firms to engage in malbehavior (such as with centralized crypto finance platforms taking on excess risk). https://t.co/miq4Tsw1mO
[2023-03-06] (x) A caveat, however, is that it’s not clear how sustainable the current vampire attack models are, especially since many of them rely on strategies that are regulatorily suspect.
[2023-03-06] (x) In short: Vampire attacks restore competition for loyal customers. https://t.co/jMsbkZjEmA

This theory broadly accords with what we’ve seen among NFT trading platforms in recent weeks, with vampire attacks leading to intense competition for customer loyalty.
[2023-03-06] (x) This leads to much more competition over loyal consumers, driving the price they face down far below even the price before loyalty programs were introduced. 

[Vampire attack price in solid red, of course 🧛!] https://t.co/c2g0irWGDY
[2023-03-06] (x) But what happens with vampire attacks 🧛? Suddenly each firm can see who its competitors’ loyal customers are, and offer them the same price it’s giving its own loyal customers.
[2023-03-06] (x) … which reduces the incentive for a platform to try to steal its competitor’s loyal customers. 

As a result firms can charge even their loyal customers more. (Whoops 😬!)

[In the figure, the green solid line is the loyal customer price; blue was the price they faced before.] https://t.co/5bESaSZNmu
[2023-03-06] (x) The platforms monopolize their captive consumers. And this means they have to drop their price a lot – and take a big loss – if they want to attract their competitors’ loyal customers…
[2023-03-06] (x) It turns out that loyalty programs are actually bad for all consumers – even the ones who get the discount. 

Why’s that? Loyalty programs make it possible for a platform to price discriminate between captive and mobile consumers. 👇
[2023-03-06] (x) We then introduce the possibility that firms can have loyalty programs, as a way of convincing mobile customers to stay put. Consumers who are part of a platform’s loyalty program receive a discount at that platform, but have to pay the standard price at a competitor.
[2023-03-06] (x) When platforms are only able to offer a single price to both types of consumers, you get a price curve that declines in the share of the market that’s mobile [λ in the figure], which makes sense, since the more mobile consumers there are, the more firms want to compete for them. https://t.co/qnSjD7dMFk
[2023-03-06] (x) We build a simple model in which platforms have some customers who are “captive” (for whatever reason), and others who are mobile across platforms – and price-sensitive. https://t.co/jMsbkZjEmA
[2023-03-06] (x) In a new #EconomicTheory paper, @J_W_Hatfield and I show that vampire attacks can result in virtuous platform competition that drives down prices. https://t.co/jMsbkZjEmA
[2023-03-06] (x) With public transaction histories, a new entrant can just simply read its competitors’ transaction records, and provide direct rewards or other incentives to top customers who switch.

What’s this mean for consumers? 👇
[2023-03-06] (x) In a “vampire attack,” a platform tries to directly incentivize its competitors’ customers to switch.

This isn’t a new idea (airlines have had “status match” for ages), but it’s much easier in the world of crypto, where every transaction is stored publicly on a blockchain.
[2023-03-06] (x) Blood-sucking web platforms may be good for consumers.

“Wait, what!?,” you’re thinking 🧐

I’m serious. It’s time to talk about vampire attacks 🧛‍♂️, and @J_W_Hatfield and I have a theory: https://t.co/jMsbkZjEmA

[2023-03-09] (x) @weikengchen Yes, the cost of increased security in this setting is larger proofs and increased verifier time. It may be possible to push some of these costs onto the prover via recursion or increasing the FRI blowup factor.
[2023-03-09] (x) @weikengchen StarkWare already works over the 251-bit prime (see Section 3.1.1 here: https://t.co/eXw5PtiKiP). The prime matches the field used in StarkEx ECDSA signatures https://t.co/lvoi3M9EQi. So increasing security here requires increasing number of FRI queries, not the field size.
[2023-03-09] (x) @EliBenSasson At any rate, the protocols we use to secure millions of dollars of assets must not be breakable today. For that reason, FRI should not be run non-interactively at 80 bits of security if it is securing anything of value. (3/3)
[2023-03-09] (x) The tweet I'm responding to suggests that a protocol using non-hashing cryptography be deemed to have 0 bits of security when compared to a hashing-based one. This immediately leads to nonsensical conclusions, or at least ones that @EliBenSasson presumably disagrees with (2/3)
[2023-03-09] (x) Several have reached out confused about this third tweet. Why do SNARKs using elliptic curves have 0 bits of security, that doesn't make sense? That's the point. (1/3)
[2023-03-09] (x) To the extent that anyone might deem SNARKs that use elliptic curves as having 0 bits of security, StarkEx is also at 0 bits of security today due its use of ECDSA signatures, as is its L1, Ethereum. (3/3)
[2023-03-09] (x) These are the stakes. I congratulate StarkWare on upping the StarkNet (SHARP) verifier to 96 bits of security earlier this week as promised. However, the dYdX StarkEx verifier remains at 80 bits https://t.co/n9ZYKaT7fK. I continue to believe that this is too low. (2/3)
[2023-03-09] (x) Known attacks on FRI when run non-interactively at 80 bits of security are feasible. This is not an academic question. It's about securing hundreds of millions of dollars of assets and a systemic risk to the rollup ecosystem. (1/3)
[2023-03-09] (x) Halmos allows you to verify Solidity behavior (and even provides counterexamples) using symbolic testing.

@zachobront walks through how he applied symbolic testing to some tricky Yul code.

[2023-04-06] (x) // Shoutouts to the @1337skulls community, as well as all the communities that participated in the wave mint, and especially the @chain_runners for sparking our collaboration. Special thanks also to our editor, @Tim_Org, and many others at @a16zcrypto who helped with the piece!
[2023-04-06] (x) c/o The @1337skulls 532s, past and present: @BrainDraind, @Cham0x, @datadanne_eth, @0xDith, @domenicodistilo, @tonylongname, @0x_epikur, @fisheateth, @flashrekt, @hoanhan101, @kamtang, @skominers, @Maniinng, @0xmonno, @1980kcom, @nwmd, @thegallowboob, @SaintStereo, @51991eth ...
[2023-04-06] (x) And if you enjoyed this thread, please consider sharing it! 🙏 https://t.co/mNg1odRLDM
[2023-04-06] (x) PS: If you want even more wave mint reflections, check out this earlier thread @0xDith wrote soon after the @1337skulls mint: https://t.co/dPVPqKnC33
[2023-04-06] (x) And of course we’d love to see other groups use and modify the wave mint mechanism to launch their own projects. Please let us know how it goes. 🙏
[2023-04-06] (x) Overall we found the wave mint approach a 1337 solution for the @1337skulls drop. There’s much to say about both the mechanism and our experience. 

Read the full article for details! 👀https://t.co/lahyVG4KqK
[2023-04-06] (x) With waves opening and closing quickly, smart contracts and other key information must be available for examination in advance, and clearly explained and documented. 🧐
[2023-04-06] (x) But from an operations perspective, a wave mint is complex to execute. And there are security issues around having people mint for wallets that all other, potentially high-value digital assets.
[2023-04-06] (x) From a mechanism design perspective, the wave mint format means that people's wallets effectively function as a form of “proof of unique personhood.” And opening mint windows spontaneously and for short time periods makes the overall mint process harder to game.
[2023-04-06] (x) In our case, the wave minting process itself also drove attention to the project. And drip-feeding the mint made it easier to welcome and onboard new community members in real-time.
[2023-04-06] (x) ... and as long as the number of people participating in each wave is low relative to total token supply, minting can be free without leading to gas wars. https://t.co/ZC4GbfpkK1
[2023-04-06] (x) By design, the wave mint approach brings in a group of people from an established community together, immediately creating a network effect... https://t.co/OuIE6tSpqT
[2023-04-06] (x) (Crucially, each wallet should be allowed to mint only once, even if it qualifies for multiple waves, to help ensure a broad distribution of holders.) ‼️
[2023-04-06] (x) The next waves should follow "strong ties" – communities with significant holder overlap, where interest can spread through word-of-mouth. 🌊🌊 

Then finally come shorter waves targeting communities with weaker ties. 🌊🌊🌊 https://t.co/2K8VQgAZ7H
[2023-04-06] (x) Early waves start with "trusted ties," to help build a core community with faith in the project – and provide an opportunity to watch for bugs or other anomalies. 🌊
[2023-04-06] (x) Different groups of people are invited to mint in sequence, drawing on established communities with shared interests. For example, @1337skulls offered minting waves for other cc0 projects such as @nounsdao and @blitmap. https://t.co/gX17e8JLbZ
[2023-04-06] (x) A “wave mint” leverages people’s on-chain data and reputation to select for holders who are well matched to a project's culture and goals – while at the same time driving attention to the project, and helping avoid gas wars and bots. 🌊
[2023-04-06] (x) Look for people in related NFT communities and invite them in – in waves. 🌊

That's what @1337skulls did, and we wrote about this "wave mint" strategy for @a16zcrypto: https://t.co/lahyVG4KqK
[2023-04-06] (x) An NFT mint largely determines who your community is, and how they and the broader market view the project. 

The question is, then: How can a new NFT project recruit holders who share the project’s values and goals?  

Read on! 👇👀 https://t.co/lahyVG4KqK

[2023-04-17] (x) 13/What has your approach to 1-1s been and what have you found most effective?
[2023-04-17] (x) 12/So if you're not already having regular 1-1s with your employees, I highly recommend giving them a try. With the right approach, they can be one of the most effective management tools available.
[2023-04-17] (x) 11/Finally, let's address a common misconception about 1-1s: that they're a waste of time. In fact, the opposite is true.

By investing time in 1-1s, managers can help their employees perform at a higher level, which ultimately benefits the entire team.
[2023-04-17] (x) 10/Now, you might be thinking: "But I already have regular team meetings. Do I really need 1-1s as well?"

The answer is yes. While team meetings are important for keeping everyone aligned and informed, they don't provide the same level of personalized coaching that 1-1s do.
[2023-04-17] (x) 9/It's also important to schedule 1-1s regularly - either every week or every other week - so that employees don't feel disconnected from their manager. And the meetings should last between 30-45 minutes, so that the employee has time to cover their key topics.
[2023-04-17] (x) 8/In return, the manager needs to commit to reading and reflecting on these topics in advance and showing up to the meeting on time and prepared so that they can give more thoughtful guidance and feedback.
[2023-04-17] (x) 7/But in order for 1-1s to be effective, managers need to approach them in the right way. For example, it's important for managers to ask employees to send them a list of topics for the 1-1 at least 12 hours in advance.
[2023-04-17] (x) 6/For employees, 1-1s are a chance to get inside their manager's head to better understand things like what they're most excited about and what's keeping them up at night.

Employees can leverage this insight to tune their priorities and areas of focus.
[2023-04-17] (x) 5/In other words, the 1-1 is an opportunity for the employee to get personalized, one-on-one coaching from their manager.

This is incredibly valuable, as it allows the employee to improve their skills and perform at a higher level.
[2023-04-17] (x) 4/This might include feedback on their work, guidance on a particular project, or help navigating a difficult situation.
[2023-04-17] (x) 3/Think of the time you spend in 1-1s as a cheat code to help your employees unlock their superpowers.
[2023-04-17] (x) 2/First, let's clarify the primary purpose of a 1-1. It's not for the manager to get status updates from their employees.

Instead, it's for the employee to get from their manager whatever they need to be more effective in their job.
[2023-04-17] (x) 1/As a startup CEO or manager, you know that managing your team effectively is key to success. But one tool that's often misunderstood is the 1-1 meeting.

Here are a few thoughts on why having regular 1-1s with employees is one of the most effective management tools available.

[2023-04-19] (x) With more clients, the Optimism ecosystem becomes more varied and resilient — more able to endure bugs and other challenges, but also more flexible and evolvable.

Think about single-species monoculture vs. a natural rainforest. Only one can support a thriving ecosystem. https://t.co/DmKBcu2gFX
[2023-04-19] (x) Like Ethereum, the OP Stack requires two clients types to run: an execution client like op-geth + a consensus client like op-node. And now, Magi.

[2023-04-25] (x) 7/ A successful executive staff meeting requires thoughtful planning and execution. Don't let it be a half-baked affair. Make sure it's worth the time/bandwidth of your most highly compensated employees.
[2023-04-25] (x) 6/ Don't overcook it! Meetings that drag on are a recipe for disaster. In my experience, two hours, is the absolute max.
[2023-04-25] (x) 5/ Don't just focus on the appetizers or the main course, strike a balance between strategy and execution discussions. Make sure you and your team are all on the same page about the most important aspects of your overall product and business strategy.
[2023-04-25] (x) 4/ Want to add some flavor? Invite additional employees for specific discussions. It's like adding a special seasoning to your dish, adding a new perspective that might just be the secret ingredient you need.
[2023-04-25] (x) 3/ Keep the guest list intimate. Like a fancy dinner party, the more people you invite, the less chance there is for meaningful conversation. Keep it small for high-bandwidth communication, and watch your ideas sizzle.
[2023-04-25] (x) 2/ Share materials beforehand, and ask attendees to pre-read them. This saves valuable meeting time for juicy discussion and debate, rather than dry presentations.
[2023-04-25] (x) 1/ Think of your agenda as the ingredients list for your meeting dish. Share it with your team at least 24 hours in advance, so everyone can marinate on the topics and come prepared to spice things up.

[2023-05-01] (x) 19/ By outlining the above, we hope to encourage the UK to avoid applying regulatory obligations under the Financial Services, Promotions and Prospectus regulatory regimes described above that would impede or make it impossible for projects to decentralize.
[2023-05-01] (x) 18/ For 1⃣, we explained how tokens have varying risk profiles, how decentralization can reduce/eliminate those risks, why the dissemination of tokens is necessary to achieve decentralization and how consumers can be protected in token transactions.
[2023-05-01] (x) 17/ We sought to encourage this approach by discussing how decentralization should influence the regulation of: 
1⃣ Token Transactions (primary sales, airdrops, ongoing incentive-based rewards and secondary markets) and 
2⃣ DeFi.
[2023-05-01] (x) 16/ The focus on regulatory outcomes means the UK’s regulatory framework for web3 should ultimately account for the fact that decentralization, the critical feature and benefit that blockchain technology enables, can eliminate many risks.
[2023-05-01] (x) 15/ Over the weekend, we filed a response letter to the Consultation. 

We offered our support for the UK’s thoughtful approach and applauded the Consultation’s core design principle of “same risk, same regulatory outcome.” 
https://t.co/w0eUnZQt2H
[2023-05-01] (x) 14/🔺Finally, the UK will seek to address DeFi in a future phase, so the Consultation included a call for evidence and provided preliminary guidance that the UK may focus regulation on the businesses offering DeFi services to consumers, not the underlying software protocols.
[2023-05-01] (x) 13/ The Consultation proposes to expand these requirements to tokens. 

While we agree strong protections are warranted for public offerings, prospectus requirements for listings of decentralized tokens could be problematic unless the disclosure regime is narrowly tailored.
[2023-05-01] (x) 12/🔸(3) Prospectus Requirements.

Generally, the UK places restrictions on public offers of securities and imposes certain prospectus requirements in relation to public offers of “transferable securities” or listings of such assets on trading venues.
[2023-05-01] (x) 11/ The Consultation’s proposed expansion of the licensing regime will similarly apply to the promotions regime, limiting promotions by CeFi companies unless they are licensed. 

This makes sense, but as above, could cause issues for certain types of issuers and protocols.
[2023-05-01] (x) 10/🔸(2) Restriction on Financial Promotions. 

This regime is designed to prevent unlicensed persons from marketing services that are licensable under the regime described above, unless those persons are licensed or an exclusion applies.
[2023-05-01] (x) 9/ However, this expansion could also potentially apply broadly to token issuances.

This may make sense in the context of primary sales by issuers, but it could be too restrictive for airdrops and incentive-based rewards issued by decentralized protocols.
[2023-05-01] (x) 8/ The Consultation proposes to expand the power of the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) to regulate tokens, which will expand its authority over CeFi. This is good. 

In the U.S., we’ve seen the harm that can come from a lack of guidance and appropriate regulatory involvement.
[2023-05-01] (x) 7/ Currently, most utility and governance tokens do not qualify as “specified investments,” so many CeFi businesses like exchanges, custodians and lenders that only deal with such assets and many token issuers do not require a license.

The Consultation will change that.
[2023-05-01] (x) 6/🔸(1) Financial Services Licensing Requirements. 

This regime requires businesses engaged in certain “regulated activities” in the UK to obtain licenses if those activities relate to an investment of a specified kind (a “specified investment”).
[2023-05-01] (x) 5/🔸Next, the Consultation proposes many changes to the UK’s existing regulatory regimes with the primary goal of expanding its “financial regulatory perimeter” to include tokens and to scope in centralized crypto financial services (CeFi). 

In particular:
[2023-05-01] (x) 4/🔹The UK’s stablecoin regime focuses on issuance and custody of fiat-backed stablecoins as well as payment-related activities, putting sensible guardrails in place that will enhance consumer protections while still fostering innovation.

Decentralized stablecoins are EXCLUDED.
[2023-05-01] (x) 3/ The UK published a Consultation in Feb that will result in meaningful legislation and rulemaking in three phases:
🔹 Phase 1 focuses on fiat-backed stablecoins
🔸 Phase 2 focuses on tokens and CeFi
🔺 future phases will focus on DeFi and other areas

https://t.co/jkjI8fSDEQ
[2023-05-01] (x) 2/ The UK wants to become a global hub for web3 technology and investment and the government has been keen to demonstrate a pragmatic and constructive approach to crypto. 

As a result, there is cause for significant optimism, but the next 18 months are critical.
[2023-05-01] (x) This weekend, we filed a letter applauding 👏 the UK's efforts to provide regulatory clarity and enhance consumer protections in web3.

[2023-05-10] (x) 10/ Would love your thoughts below on anything I missed or insights you have on working with or serving on boards.⬇️
[2023-05-10] (x) 9/ The best board meetings are the uneventful ones in which you have already pre-briefed and aligned with board members individually on any critical or controversial topics.

Surprising people in the meeting with a big reveal, positive or negative, rarely goes well.
[2023-05-10] (x) 8/ When you want to achieve board alignment on a potentially spicy strategic topic, don’t shy away from enlisting the support of one or more influential board members to help you bring the rest of the group along on the journey.
[2023-05-10] (x) 7/ 1) Product highlights &amp; lowlights, 2) a financial update &amp; outlook, 3) a sales/deal pipeline review, 4) important legal or reg updates, 5) an exec session in which board members can give direct, candid feedback to you as the CEO w/o concern for freaking out your exec team.
[2023-05-10] (x) 6/ Just like a great movie, successful board meetings follow a cohesive narrative with a clear beginning, middle and end. Key topics include:
[2023-05-10] (x) 5/In times of headwinds and turbulence, which every startup experiences, ensure that bad news travels faster than good news in order to build and maintain strong alignment and trust.
[2023-05-10] (x) 4/ A board’s number one job is to exercise their fiduciary duty on behalf of shareholders and investors. As a CEO, it is incumbent on you to make fulfillment of this duty easy and straightforward.
[2023-05-10] (x) 3/ As a founder/CEO, it is essential to develop the ability to listen intently to feedback from your board, find the kernels of insight and wisdom in what they are telling you and then translate learning into an action plan relevant and specific to the company you are building.
[2023-05-10] (x) 2/ Failure to listen to feedback from your board may ultimately get you fired but running your company based on direction from your board will definitely get you fired.
[2023-05-10] (x) 1/ Board members are not your friend or your therapist. They're valuable partners in building your company.

Just like your employees &amp; exec team, you need an organized, structured approach to engaging with &amp; managing your board in order to get the most out of the relationship.

[2023-05-15] (x) So, so sorry to hear this 😥😥😥. Lucas was the best of the best. 

I remember running into him in the hallway in my first week at @BeckerFriedman, and somehow he -- a macro legend -- already knew who I was and what I worked on. [2023-05-15T15:15:53+00:00] (transcript) So almost every Web3 business is an example of a platform.
[2023-05-15T15:15:53+00:00] (transcript) a lot of classical platforms have managed network effects by creating walled gardens. But Web3 is about doing the opposite. Web3 platforms operate on blockchains, which are public, and according to technology standards, which are interoperable.
[2023-05-15T15:15:53+00:00] (transcript) And so on one hand, almost every Web3 business is fundamentally building a platform. On the other hand, we're in an environment where maintaining. Platform dominance has gotten much harder.
[2023-05-15T15:15:53+00:00] (transcript) And so on every one of these dimensions, there's at least one pressure that makes the competitive forces stronger.
[2023-05-15T15:15:53+00:00] (transcript) There's an opportunity when you convert your users into people who have a true preference for your platform. They actually want to use your platform over competitors. And ironically, that comes through the same thing that creates this lack of switching costs, right? The giving users a slice of digital ownership
[2023-05-15T15:15:53+00:00] (transcript) while competition is maybe getting more intense in a lot of dimensions, those personally invested users also have, you know, a strong incentive to create a lot of value.
[2023-05-15T15:15:53+00:00] (transcript) in Web 1, you were the consumer. In Web 2, you were the product. And in Web 3, you are the brand.
[2023-05-15T15:15:53+00:00] (transcript) Whereas in Web 3, value is much more shared, right? Platforms and users, you know, grow and co-create together and share value together.
[2023-05-15T15:15:53+00:00] (transcript) if you build something that works really well, there's a much greater threat in Web3 that like your entire network could just get like moved to a competitor, could even be like a copy.
[2023-05-15T15:15:53+00:00] (transcript) the network effect now accruing to the asset rather than to the platform or the portal. And that's sort of what's going on here, right? It's like, you know, elite skulls NFT or a sub duck or a board ape or whatever. The network is embedded in the collective of people who own these assets.
[2023-05-15T15:15:53+00:00] (transcript) I think there really is competition. I think there's, there's a lot more cooperation than there might've been, there might've been in previous iterations of technology platforms. There's very much a sense in which it's platforms all the way down.
[2023-05-15T15:15:53+00:00] (transcript) So I think it's changing the character of competition.
[2023-05-15T15:15:53+00:00] (transcript) composability is to crypto, what compounding is to finance. It's this idea that... it's like an exponential force.
[2023-05-15T15:15:53+00:00] (transcript) Web3 is just that, but for everything. It's that for the underlying software that runs the platforms. It's that for all the brands and identity formation that people are doing. It's that for reputation markers. It's that for like our personal data. It's all of that rolled into one big snowball
[2023-05-15T15:15:53+00:00] (transcript) So the bottom line is that, Web3 has strengthened a lot of classical network effects, but it's also moved where they reside, right? The strong network effects are often tied to the digital assets, to the sort of, you know, the networks that sprawl across the blockchain, rather than to sort of any individual platform that operates on top of those assets.
[2023-05-15T15:15:53+00:00] (transcript) In Web3, we're seeing a lot of like new network effects, the new opportunities for positive network effects, right? This goes back to the composability point we were talking about earlier.
[2023-05-15T15:15:53+00:00] (transcript) the core insight, like with our conversation about the 5G, the five forces earlier, is that most things have gotten much harder. Previous sources of platform competitive advantage, platform moats
[2023-05-15T15:15:53+00:00] (transcript) As Web3 is more complex and often more high-tech than anything that's come before it in category, right? Talent moat has widened and deepened.
[2023-05-15T15:15:53+00:00] (transcript) But like the core platform competitive advantages have mostly weakened. There's one though that really stands out as having strengthened tremendously. And that is? Which is embeddedness.
[2023-05-15T15:15:53+00:00] (transcript) This is where like, wallets have a lot more potential for embeddedness.
[2023-05-15T15:15:53+00:00] (transcript) Web3 makes it possible to turn your consumers and your your or your. Users into people who are personally invested in the platform success and who often interact and interface with each other towards the goal of the platform success
[2023-05-15T15:15:53+00:00] (transcript) So a platform can build a moat around a user base that like really wants to attach to it that wants to use its product rather than a competitors or or rather than building their own and even better wants to contribute to making what they do higher value. We haven't really seen these things play out at scale yet because it's just too early.
[2023-05-15T15:15:53+00:00] (transcript) it's strengthened the vampire attack strategy, like, because now there are much lower costs to doing it. But ironically, like, loyalty is still to some degree the defense.
[2023-05-15T15:15:53+00:00] (transcript) Loyalty now becomes integrating people into the brand.
[2023-05-15T15:15:53+00:00] (transcript) There's this unbounded Web3 value in theory. But to make it practicable, we actually, like, have to solve these strategy questions to figure out how value capture is going to work.
[2023-05-15T15:15:53+00:00] (transcript) absolutely, I think we're going to need completely new business frameworks. It's like, how do you even start to define general principles for value creation and capture in Web3?
[2023-05-15T15:15:53+00:00] (transcript) my rough instinct is that what we see in Web3 is that it's not just a theory. It's a theory. And what we see in Web3 is what you might call, like, expanding returns. That, like, every new thing that's created sort of creates a new, like, ecosystem of possibilities
[2023-05-15T15:15:53+00:00] (transcript) the way I think about the big change here in Web3 is you have many new ways to subsidize. It used to be that the form of subsidization was usually giving discounts.
[2023-05-15T15:15:53+00:00] (transcript) Here, there's this opportunity, at least in principle, where you can subsidize people by giving them tokens that sort of give them more value that's aligned with the activity they're conducting.

[2023-05-19] (x) New generalized matching survey article just dropped 👀👀👀

(coauthored with @J_W_Hatfield, Ravi Jagadeesan, @a_nichifor, @mostrovs, @t8el, and @AlexanderWestk3 https://t.co/KzDLfJMvfs)
[2023-05-19] (x) @bedehomender @J_W_Hatfield @a16zcrypto Great question! 

What happens in the model is that loyalty programs enable price discrimination between (1) captive customers who for one reason or another have to use a specific platform, and (2) mobile/repeat customers who are price sensitive and might in principle switch back and forth. 

The introduction of a "loyalty" price for a platform's mobile/repeat customers makes it possible to charge captive customers a higher price. And if a customer who's in a given platform's loyalty program wants to switch to the competitor, without their status being portable, they have to face the high price the competitor is charging to its captive customers. This significantly decreases the incentive to switch, and actually means that everyone can end up paying a higher price than if there were no loyalty program at all.
[2023-05-19] (x) Full paper at https://t.co/xqqri9WwCE, and associated Twitter thread here: https://t.co/PmkZVwnyZO
[2023-05-19] (x) How can "vampire attacks" -- recruiting a competing platform's top customers -- impact competition?

Simple and shareable summary of my paper with @J_W_Hatfield via @a16zcrypto: https://t.co/CxQn1bUwLr

[2023-06-13] (x) The SEC disclosures today confirm that the SEC thought decentralization was the key difference in the risk profile of certain digital assets as compared to traditional securities, and that it was therefore the appropriate attribute to asses for purposes of Howey. 

While this revelation is unlikely to help centralized token issuers that conducted ICOs, it is very good for the industry — since 2018 the industry has followed the SEC’s guidance and has focused on building more robust decentralization.

The disclosures also prove why calls for more guidance from the SEC are well founded (even the SEC is unsure how to define decentralization) and why, absent such guidance, congressional action is necessary.

[2023-06-20] (x) The license that a protocol's code is published under is an important factor in the protocol's decentralization. 

It makes sense to use a restrictive license when a protocol is pre-token in order to guard against the protocol being forked/tokenized maliciously. 

Post-token launch, such protection is less necessary, but may nevertheless be important to fostering growth of the protocol's network. Where restrictive licenses are used post-token launch, they should not impede app-layer diversity (which could undermine the protocol's permissionlessness or censorship resistance) or be used to accrue value to the DevCo instead of the community.

[2023-06-24] (x) @asv141 Oh sorry! Should have clarified. The second stage auction is open to everyone, not just the original buyers. Assume that the initial set is much smaller than the total prospective buyer pool....
[2023-06-24] (x) @oldmrktplace WOAH. Thanks for flagging -- on reflection I had been vaguely aware of this years ago, but had totally forgotten about it. Any chance you know whether Sandeep and Jeff ever did a (public) equilibrium analysis? Haven't been able to find one on a quick search....
[2023-06-24] (x) A big challenge for bidders in the first stage is that they have to estimate the second-stage clearing price. One possible modification to the mechanism would be to promise them an extra discount to (partially) insure them against the risk of underestimating the clearing price.
[2023-06-24] (x) Hey #EconTwitter has anyone seen the following two-stage mechanism before? Thoughts on the equilibrium? 

N goods for sale (probably common values, but let's abstract away from that).

(STAGE 1) A restricted set of potential buyers has the right to lock in purchases at price P.

(STAGE 2) Assuming supply not exhausted, run a descending auction starting at P for the remaining units, to find a clearing price p. Then refund all the purchasers from Stage 1 the price difference P - p.

(Auction in practice likely will be pay-as-bid, although I'm personally more interested in the uniform-price version.)

[2023-06-24] (x) @thomasruppel @Azuki @BEANZOfficial Haha yes, sorry -- this post is very much inside baseball for an NFT community I'm part of called @Azuki. 

TLDR is that they are about to sell a new series of tokens, and it initially looked like they had taken a very clever step to help balance demand with supply (which is often a core problem in NFT sales).

But now some members of their team are saying that is not the case, which means there may instead be significant demand/supply imbalance in the sale -- which would lead to what's called a "gas war," where people bid higher and higher transaction fees in hope of getting their purchase transaction cleared first.

[2023-06-25] (x) @0xCygaar @waleswoosh @Tim_Roughgarden (@waleswoosh the issue with your argument is that the gas level is endogenous to the level of demand/supply imbalance. If the imbalance is really significant, then people will in effect bid gas up to the difference between the pre-sale price and the market-clearing price. So no matter how efficient the contract is, price paid trends towards the market-clearing price.)
[2023-06-25] (x) @0xCygaar @waleswoosh 💯. And fundamentally gas wars are about supply/demand (im)balance -- as @Tim_Roughgarden and I discussed here: https://t.co/r3Ebla7s22. Optimizing the contract can help the whole thing go faster, but it doesn't get away from the incentive for people to bid extra gas to jump the queue....
