[2023-06-28] (x) Think the 2 biggest questions about Prometheum that remain unanswered are:

1. How will the listing of a digital asset on Prometheum be anything other than a death sentence for most projects?

2. How will they get around the disclosure requirements of Rule 144 or Rule 15c2-11?

On the first point, if a digital asset has a functional purpose but is deemed to be a security, then it's highly likely that its functional purpose will be negated. For instance, even if ETH had an issuer and that issuer agreed that ETH was a digital asset security and wanted to list on Prometheum, the recognition of ETH as a security would destroy the functionality of the Ethereum blockchain. The blockchain would no longer be able to issue ETH to validators and users wouldn't be able to expend ETH to pay fees, because now all of those transactions would be securities transactions. As a result, the Ethereum blockchain couldn't continue to function.

On the second point, it's theoretically possible that a combination of Rule 144 and Rule 15c2-11 could be used to trade "digital asset securities" on an ATS. But they can't facilitate such trades without the information required by those rules being made publicly available. That information includes audited financials and other information that isn't relevant to the price of a given digital asset and that no issuers make available. So how will Prometheum do it? Aaron regularly deflected questions on this subject, so I don't think they have a plan, but at the end of the episode he indicated that he thought on-chain data might actually be more useful for investors. 

So, my suspicion continues to be that there's another step to this parlor trick. If Gensler's back is against the wall (ie, if it looks likely that Congress will move forward with a market structure bill), he may provide extremely narrow No Action Letter relief regarding the disclosure requirements for "digital asset securities" under these rules. This would enable Prometheum to list a few digital assets and continue the illusion of there being a compliant pathway, which could dissuade Congress from acting on market structure. Meanwhile, the NAL would enable the SEC to maintain strict control over the listing of digital assets (a power Congress never intended them to have) and drive the industry to move outside the U.S.

[2023-06-29] (x) It may seem minor, but the UK empowering its financial regulators to foster innovation is brilliant.
[2023-06-29] (x) Two additional thoughts on Prometheum:

I don't think that Prometheum unilaterally listing an asset would have much legal meaning in terms of the analysis of whether a given asset is a security. Their opinion on the subject doesn't matter any more than anyone else's. However, if an issuer were to seek a listing, it would likely undermine any argument that their digital wasn't a security. As a result, it's hard to see where it would make sense for issuers to participate in the listing process. Given that the applicable disclosure requirements require financial information of the issuer, it's therefore unlikely Prometheum would be able to convince issuers to help them meet such requirements.

That being said, one way Prometheum could avoid 15c2-11 information requirements is to develop a closed trading system that does not publish quotes publicly and, instead, privately provide quotes to password enabled users that want to trade. However, that sounds a lot more like an OTC desk than an exchange, and I would hope Congress wouldn't see this approach as being anywhere close to sufficient.

[2023-07-05] (x) @economeager @ThomasJDurfee Emphatically not defending the utterly surreal process of buying a research project like in the email quoted above (😱), but to the broader point, I *do* think it's possible for high school students to do high-quality research.

I've mentored multiple high-schoolers who wrote publishable papers; and I've seen many more such students in my time judging in the mathematics category at the International Science and Engineering Fair. (I also did a bit of research in high school myself, as did many of my friends.)

For some students, exploring research early can be a powerful way to discover a true interest in ideas/scholarship, and to attempt a form of work that's much harder than typical high school or even college fare. For the right type of student, that can be pretty valuable imo, and can give them the spark to keep going.

So overall I'm in favor of making these sorts of opportunities more broadly and evenly accessible....

[2023-07-06] (x) The role of an impossibility result like this is not to discourage but to illuminate, highlighting the most promising paths forward. From it, we learn that our options are (i) constrained; and (ii) already
being actively explored by the community:
11/12 https://t.co/KHOGNIxcm8
[2023-07-06] (x) The proof isn't short enough to summarize here, but this inequality captures part of it:
10/12 https://t.co/wyqdIUYEqH
[2023-07-06] (x) Our main result shows that the problem is fundamental, rather than a flaw in the EIP-1559 design: with MEV, *no* non-trivial or approximately welfare-maximizing TFM can be both DSIC and BPIC!
9/12 https://t.co/Rlwwce4IRu
[2023-07-06] (x) Intuitively, the issue is that, when there's MEV, a user can't know whether to underbid to take advantage of a block producer that might be willing to subsidize the difference.
8/12
[2023-07-06] (x) And boy does MEV change things; for example, EIP-1559 is no longer incentive-compatible for both users and block producers (even with a correctly set base fee)!
7/12
[2023-07-06] (x) We model a post-MEV world via block producers with private valuations for blocks (e.g., due to application-layer value that they can extract), above and beyond whatever fees they might earn at the consensus layer:
6/12 https://t.co/m0aRDNJtiX
[2023-07-06] (x) DSIC+BPIC have been studied in a pre-MEV world (e.g., with EIP-1559 achieving both, as long as its base fee is not crazy low for the current demand).
5/12 https://t.co/wz1q9jiRvn
[2023-07-06] (x) A second is a form of credibility called "BPIC," meaning that a validator responsible for supplying the TFM's inputs should be properly incentivized to behave as intended.
4/12
[2023-07-06] (x) One minimal thing you'd really like from a TFM is good UX, meaning that bidding is easy for users (one of the original motivations for EIP-1559). This idea is encoded by a mathematical property called "DSIC."
3/12
[2023-07-06] (x) Background: a transaction fee mechanism (TFM) is the part of a blockchain protocol responsible for figuring out which transactions should be included and who should pay what. Example: EIP-1559.
2/12
[2023-07-06] (x) Lots of competing proposals out there for how to deal with MEV: OFAs and other competitive markets, encrypted mempools, MPC, TEEs, etc. But do we truly need any of these? Yes! And, with @bahrani_maryam and @PGarimidi, we have the math to prove it:👇
1/12 https://t.co/8vObSFFrfd

[2023-07-11] (x) @ZKPenjoyer What you are proposing is reminiscent of some decoding algorithms that work, though they are a bit more complicated, and impractically slow (see for example "Theoretical Decoder" here: https://t.co/7zgtAFAkRe)
[2023-07-11] (x) @ZKPenjoyer If I understand correctly, you are trying to decode the Reed-Solomon code in the presence of errors? This is a highly non-trivial topic that is not covered in PAZK since it's never needed in proof systems. (1/2)
[2023-07-11] (x) @ZKPenjoyer Yes! Another way of looking at it is simply that each of the 5 letters/numbers in HELLO=(72, 69, 76, 76, 79) can be viewed as listing the evaluation of P at a designated point. Since there are 5 numbers, there should be 5 points (one per number).
[2023-07-11] (x) @ZKPenjoyer Whoops, the interpolating set has to have size 5, not 4, since HELLO has 5 letters (I can't count)
[2023-07-11] (x) @ZKPenjoyer If you instead interpreted those entries as *evaluations* of P over some interpolating set (say, {-1, 0, 1, 2}) then Lagrange interpolation would be used to quickly evaluate P(r). (3/3)

Does that clarify things?
[2023-07-11] (x) @ZKPenjoyer You don't need Lagrange Interpolation here because you are interpreting the entries of the vector being fingerprinted as the coefficients (in the standard monomial basis) of the associated polynomial P. (2/3)
[2023-07-11] (x) @ZKPenjoyer Yes, and you should omit the Error Correction and Decoding steps since Reed-Solomon fingerprinting isn't used to correct errors, but rather just as an Equality check. (1/3)
[2023-07-11] (x) @ZKPenjoyer With those changes, your example will (with overwhelming probability) successfully detect that HELLO and HELPP are not the same vector! (n/n, I think)
[2023-07-11] (x) @ZKPenjoyer Your point 3 chooses several different values of r deterministically (namely -1, 0, 1, and 2), rather than one random r  (3/n).
[2023-07-11] (x) @ZKPenjoyer In your Point 3, the fingerprint only needs to consist of a single point (r, P(r)), but r needs to be chosen at random from the field to ensure that the fingerprints of two different vectors disagree with high probability. (2/n)
[2023-07-11] (x) @ZKPenjoyer In PAZK, fingerprinting is just used to determine whether two vectors are equal, not to correct errors in a vector. (1/n)
[2023-07-11] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @PratyushRT @0xAlbertG @mpfzajac Also, even for 2-round protocols, a factor-2 loss in bits of security would be catastrophic. And (ignoring FRI) lots of deployed protocols do use 2 rounds before being Fiat-Shamir-ed. The paper also rules out any loss of security in many such constant-round protocols.
[2023-07-11] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @PratyushRT @0xAlbertG @mpfzajac It's the super-constant number of rounds. 

Applying Fiat-Shamir to an r-round protocol with lambda bits of security can result in a non-interactive protocol with only lambda/r bits of security. No one had ruled out such a security loss for FRI.

When executed unwaveringly, a single year of progress can generate exponential results.

[2023-07-11] (x) 9/ The goal is not for everyone to join hands and sing kumbaya but to develop situational awareness around how each team member prefers to communicate and be communicated with.

This level of insight can unlock dramatic improvements in team effectiveness.
[2023-07-11] (x) 8/ I’ve found that doing a team personality inventory exercise (like @thmbti, @Insights, Colors or your favorite version) can be incredibly valuable in terms of building trust and empathy within the team.
[2023-07-11] (x) 7/ As a CEO, build trust by making it clear to your execs that you have their backs and you expect them to have each others’.If your team can come into work everyday comfortable in this knowledge, you’re already ahead of 99% of other teams/companies.

[2023-07-15] (x) @rel_zeta_tech I agree that specifying the number and size of MSMs is a bit tedious, but given the magnitude of the runtime differences between n scalar multiplications vs. an MSM of length n, being precise is necessary IMO (3/3)
[2023-07-15] (x) @rel_zeta_tech When x is in the scalar field of an additive group G, x*g should be called a scalar multiplication. The word "scalar" ensures there is no ambiguity, i.e., it's clear that one is not referring to the group operation of a multiplicative group. (2/3)
[2023-07-15] (x) @rel_zeta_tech "Number of group ops" should refer to the # of applications of the group operation, i.e., additions performed in an additive group, or multiplications in a multiplicative group. This is the standard meaning of group operation: https://t.co/Ao60ppaKkd
https://t.co/tOw8gBnpEF (1/3)

[2023-07-17] (x) @adamsternbach @BrianQuintenz Agreed that a different asset-based disclosure framework would be appropriate for many types of digital assets, but if the value of a digital asset is dependent on an issuer, then issuer-based disclosure and other protections are likely warranted. Otherwise, what's to protect consumers/retail investors from being dumped on by insiders who have asymmetric information? Ultimately, decentralization is what changes the risk profile. 

Approaching this from another angle. Suppose a biotech company tokenized revenue rights with respect to a to-be-developed drug. If the company still needed to guide that drug through clinical trials and FDA approval, then I think it's pretty hard to argue that issuer-based disclosure wouldn't also be necessary even though future revenue is going to be dependent on patient demand. If that wasn't the case, this pathway would be a fairly significant loophole to securities laws and it would change how the biotech industry currently finances drug development, with investors ultimately being exposed to significantly more risk.
[2023-07-17] (x) @adamsternbach @BrianQuintenz Decentralization is the the key characteristic of digital assets that changes their risk profile. It should therefore inform regulatory treatment. 

If the value of a digital asset is completely dependent on the efforts of a management team, the risks related to such digital asset (ie, information asymmetries) are similar to those of an ordinary security, so securities laws should likely apply to transactions of such asset.

Where a digital asset is decentralized, its value isn't dependent on the efforts of a management team, and its risk profile is very different from a security. In such case, there would be very low risk of information asymmetries. As a result, a disclosure regime like what's applied to equity, isn't necessary.
[2023-07-17] (x) If the SEC couldn't even convince the Ripple judge what the rules of the road are, how are entrepreneurs supposed to figure it out?

Enough is enough. Over the weekend, @BrianQuintenz and I published an article calling for an end to the uncertainty.
https://t.co/emPJL421Kw
[2023-07-17] (x) "The only way forward at this point is through thoughtful, well-calibrated legislation that protects consumers from scams and conflicts of interest—while still embracing the innovations of blockchain technology."

@milesjennings and @BrianQuintenz in @FortuneCrypto 👇💡👇:

[2023-07-23] (x) Apropos of nothing in particular: The vast majority of companies could at most dream of having their brand be adopted as a verb, like "to Google" meaning "to search" or "to Xerox" meaning "to copy."*

Once a brand somehow accomplishes that feat, it's generally considered optimal to hold onto the position, rather than just X-ing out the name and starting again from scratch.

__

* There are of course risks of "genericide" if your brand name becomes too associated with the entire category -- see https://t.co/7ZafBcA7Oz

- Offer opportunities to make meaningful contributions to the core  company mission
- Connect employees with inspiring mentors from your extended network, both in-house &amp; industry-wide
- Support your employees' growth by enhancing their skills &amp; broadening their networks

[2023-07-24] (x) 6/ Turn the answers to these questions into a personalized career development plan for each employee &amp; work closely with them to help them realize their goals.

Check in regularly to understand how it’s going &amp; if anything has changed.

Iterate &amp; adjust accordingly.
[2023-07-24] (x) 5/…

- What initially attracted you to our company?
- What do you hope to contribute/learn/achieve while you’re here?
- What would it take to make this the best place you’ve ever worked?
- What do you need from us to help you achieve your goals?
[2023-07-24] (x) 4/ Ask your employees these 4 simple questions (which almost no leader ever does): ...
[2023-07-24] (x) 3/ Leaders frequently fail to think about retaining top performers until they have another offer.

Don’t let this happen in your company!

[2023-08-01] (x) @fewture @michaelselig They’re testing that hypothesis out against the exchanges now. 

SEC only has so many arrows in its quiver. Going after an individual secondary seller would have little impact. Winning against the exchanges would have maximum impact. Same theory either way.
[2023-08-01] (x) @fewture @michaelselig Howey is and always has been a transaction-based test. You apply it to a transaction to determine whether securities laws should apply to said transaction. It's not an asset-characterization test that imparts permanent security-or-not status on an asset.
[2023-08-01] (x) @michaelselig Why is this part bad for the SEC? 

Has the SEC ever actually included in a complaint a charge that the tokens themselves were ICs/securities? All of the complaints I'm familiar with allege that certain transactions were subject to securities laws, not that the tokens themselves were securities. 

Admittedly, they use sloppy language in a lot of their filings, but in the actual charges for securities violations, I don't think I've ever seen them say a given token was and always will be a security (even though that's generally what the industry thinks the SEC is says).

Further, the guidance and releases they put out in 2017-2019 make it pretty clear that the SEC thinks Howey is a transaction based test, not a tool for classifying whether a specific asset is a security. So, I'm not sure the SEC would even dispute that portion of the ruling in the Ripple case.
[2023-08-01] (x) Huh. I guess it's kind of too bad that there's no current social media app fully optimized for watching breaking news in real time. 

Maybe someone could build that and give it a cute mascot or something.

[2023-08-04] (x) @david_r_barrera I don’t know what to tell you. I’m quoting the SEC’s own interpretation of Howey. If you disagree with it, take it up with them.
[2023-08-04] (x) @david_r_barrera I don't think so. How would an interest in a security not be a security? Literally anything can be sold in a transaction that has the hallmarks of an investment contract, which means securities laws should apply to that transaction. But that doesn't make the object of the IC a security.
[2023-08-04] (x) Might surprise a lot of people to learn that even the SEC doesn't think that ordinary tokens are securities. 

No SEC complaint has ever called for a judge to rule that an ordinary token was a security, and the SEC's own guidance dating back to 2018 (below) notes the distinction. 

Despite the noise on this topic, Howey is, and always will be, a transaction based test. An ordinary token sold in one transaction might be an investment contract or it might not be, but it never has been a security.
https://t.co/JKydEnW3sg

[Duke] Leto lifted his water flagon, held it aloft where the suspensor lights shot beams of reflection off it. “As a Chevalier of the Imperium, then,” he said, “I give you a toast.” …

“Here I am and here I remain!” he barked….

Again, the Duke lifted his water flagon, and this time emptied its remaining half onto the floor, knowing that the others around the table must do the same.

Jessica was first to follow his example.

There was a frozen moment before the others began emptying their flagons. Jessica saw how Paul, seated near his father [the Duke], was studying the reactions around him. She found herself also fascinated by what her guests’ actions revealed…. This was clean, potable water, not something already cast away in a sopping towel. Reluctance to just discard it exposed itself in trembling hands, delayed reactions’ nervous laughter … and violent obedience to the necessity.

[Frank Herbert, Dune, pp. 215–217]

[2023-08-10] (x) Thanks to @samrags_ and @moodlezoup, an initial Lasso implementation is now available, and we are looking for contributors to finish building Jolt. Here’s their overview: https://t.co/mZXboKjfNw (5/6)
[2023-08-10] (x) Our preliminary benchmarks indicate the initial Lasso implementation is about 10x faster than Halo2’s lookup argument, and we expect 30x-40x after optimization. (4/6)
[2023-08-10] (x) The key technical tools underlying Lasso and Jolt are the sum-check protocol and Spark, the sparse polynomial commitment scheme from Spartan (which itself uses sum-check). I'll have more to say about these tools in the coming weeks. (3/6)
[2023-08-10] (x) Lasso is a new lookup argument with a much faster prover and other attractive properties. Jolt builds on Lasso to offer a new paradigm in zkVM design. Together, we believe they realize @barrywhitehat's vision of a "lookup singularity" for simpler/more auditable SNARKs. (2/6)
[2023-08-10] (x) Introducing Lasso and Jolt, two new works that I hope will change how SNARKs are designed and built. You can read about them here: https://t.co/vQIoYnGQxE (1/6)
[2023-08-10] (x) .@SuccinctJT is one of the least self-aggrandizing people I know. So when he calmly told me, "I think this could be big," I knew to take him very, very seriously. And now, 9 months later, the lookup singularity is here.
[2023-08-10] (x) Lasso and Jolt are groundbreaking.

Lasso is already 10x faster than Halo2's lookup argument, even before optimizations.

[2023-08-11] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @samrags_ @moodlezoup Sorry, messed up the comparison here. The Lasso prover only commits to 3 small field elements per subtable lookup, not 18. That’s the number analogous to ~70 for cq. 

18 is the number of committed field elements for Lasso across all subtable lookups if using c=6.
[2023-08-11] (x) @dan_tehrani Actually, one can probably avoid the linear-in-subtable-size field ops in the grand product argument for the prover if the MLE of the table is quickly evaluable. But it's not a bottleneck--the "access counts" commitment will be.
[2023-08-11] (x) @dan_tehrani The factor limiting subtable size is that the prover has to commit to a vector of "access counts" for each entry of the subtable. This tweet and the ones leading up to it have more discussion of this issue: https://t.co/XDf2F5XfXr
[2023-08-11] (x) @dan_tehrani The prover does do some field ops for each subtable entry in the grand product argument in offline memory, but that's not the key issue limiting subtable size, since field ops are much cheaper than cryptographic operations. (1/2)
[2023-08-11] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @samrags_ @moodlezoup Yes, Lasso with c=1 has P commit to 1) a length-m vector of counts, and 2) the vector of counts for the entire table, plus an evaluation proof. The only reason to go to chunks is make 2) cheaper.
[2023-08-11] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @samrags_ @moodlezoup Lasso basically *is* (an optimized variant of) Spark. It's circular to say that the vector of counts for the big table is sparse and we have a sparse poly commit, so no problem, we're done. (5/5)
[2023-08-11] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @samrags_ @moodlezoup The Spark sparse poly commit deals with this a different way than cq does. By going to subtables, it keeps the cost of the evaluation proofs to subtable size (or smaller, if you use a poly commit for which evaluation proofs can be computed in sublinear time). (4/n)
[2023-08-11] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @samrags_ @moodlezoup When the verifier asks to evaluate the committed polynomial at a random point, the evaluation proof takes time N, regardless of how sparse the committed vector was. (3/n)
[2023-08-11] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @samrags_ @moodlezoup With Pedersen-vector-commitments (KZG, Bulletproofs, etc), one can commit to a size-N vector of sparsity m with m group exponentiations. (Of course, with KZG the SRS is of size N, let's ignore that). 

But that doesn't give you a sparse polynomial commitment scheme because: (2/n)
[2023-08-11] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @samrags_ @moodlezoup Generalized-Lasso goes beyond decomposable tables, to any table that has a low-degree extension that can be evaluated quickly by the verifier. But we actually don't yet have specific examples where that generality is useful. (7/7)
[2023-08-11] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @samrags_ @moodlezoup You are right that since Lasso works by decomposing one lookup into a big table into several lookups into smaller tables, one could do something similar with earlier lookups. But I think with prior lookups this would be substantially costlier. (6/n)
[2023-08-11] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @samrags_ @moodlezoup 2) No SRS, no honest party committing to table

3) Lasso collates the results of the subtable lookups into the big-table lookup internally and w/o additional commitment costs. (5/n)
[2023-08-11] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @samrags_ @moodlezoup Regarding cq I think the main benefits are: 

1) cq commits to 7 random field elements per lookup, which is the same amount of time as committing to about 70 small ones. Lasso's P with c=6 is about 18 small field elements per lookup. (4/n)
[2023-08-11] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @samrags_ @moodlezoup If people are fine doing, say, size 2^{32} MSMs, that would actually reduce our commitment costs in Jolt a bit, as we could set c=4 rather than c=6 and save ~6 committed field elements per instruction. (3/n)
[2023-08-11] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @samrags_ @moodlezoup We could make the subtable of size 2^30 or larger no problem, it just means that P commits to a vector of counts that is of size 2^30, as well as an evaluation proof for an associated polynomial. (2/n)
[2023-08-11] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @samrags_ @moodlezoup For each subtable, the prover has to commit to (the MLE of) a vector of access counts for every subtable entry, and later reveal an evaluation proof. We just want that cost to not dominate that committing to 3 (small) field elements committed per subtable lookup. (1/n)
[2023-08-11] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @samrags_ @moodlezoup BTW In case it wasn't clear, in the context of a range check, the indices into the table are the same thing as the values being range checked.
[2023-08-11] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @samrags_ @moodlezoup The Lasso prover does commit to "chunks" of indices. Meaning it will break, say, an 128-bit index into a table of size 2^{128}, into, say, eight 16-bit chunks or six 22-bit chunks, and it will commit to each chunk (one committed field element per chunk). (3/3)
[2023-08-11] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @samrags_ @moodlezoup Conceptually, Lasso allows computing simple functions on the bit-representations of field elements, since those are the sorts of functions that have nice MLEs, without cryptographically committing bit-by-bit. (2/3)
[2023-08-11] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @samrags_ @moodlezoup The Lasso verifier evaluates the MLE of the table at a random point. The MLE naturally corresponds to indexing the table by bits, and combining those to get the output. But no one cryptographically commits to the individual bits. (1/3)
[2023-08-11] (x) @zkproofs @srinathtv @rel_zeta_tech @fede_intern @dan_tehrani @CPerezz19 @shumochu One more clarification if it's helpful: for bitwise AND of two 64-bit inputs, there might be, say, c=8 lookups into a subtable of size 2^16, or c=6 lookups into a subtable of size 2^22.
[2023-08-11] (x) @zkproofs @srinathtv @rel_zeta_tech @fede_intern @dan_tehrani @CPerezz19 @shumochu See Remark 4 in Section 5 of Lasso for mention of that ancillary linear constraint. (3/3)
[2023-08-11] (x) @zkproofs @srinathtv @rel_zeta_tech @fede_intern @dan_tehrani @CPerezz19 @shumochu V needs to evaluate the MLE of the big table at a random point. There are no ancillary booleanity constraints. In the context of indexed lookups (the kind used by Jolt), there's one ancillary linear constraint to ensure the chunks of the index indeed decompose that index. (2/3)
[2023-08-11] (x) @zkproofs @srinathtv @rel_zeta_tech @fede_intern @dan_tehrani @CPerezz19 @shumochu I think you want to look at the description of Surge (Section 5 of the Lasso paper). The prover commits to 3 field elements per lookup into each subtable: an index into the subtable, a count, and the result of the subtable lookup. (There's only one subtable for bitwise AND) (1/3)
[2023-08-11] (x) @fede_intern StarkWare is using an RS rate of 1/16. That would presumably 8x the space on a trace of similar size. Even more when the bigger field is taken into account. (2/2)
[2023-08-11] (x) @fede_intern FRI proofs with MBs of space? 

As one example, Polygon zkEVM requires 250+ GBs of space. The zkEVM circuit trace has 2^23 rows, 1164 columns, and RS rate of 1/2. I don't consider that circuit to be large, though this is subjective. (1/2)
[2023-08-11] (x) @imkharn On the first point, a largely accurate one-sentence summary of what's going on is that DSIC+BPIC would require bids that depend on the BP's (unknown) valuation. The exact statement is necessarily more nuanced, and the proof requires care. The second point is addressed in the first paragraph of Section 2.3.

[2023-08-11] (x) @SteveSweer Sincerest condolences. So sorry for your loss, QED 😥
[2023-08-12] (x) @fede_intern Thanks for the reply.   

I agree that in general the community is nowhere near precise enough in cost accounting, as discussed at length in my recent "misconceptions" blog post. However, with a precise accounting it is indeed possible to understand the ballpark concrete costs of these protocols through analysis.  

One group op is takes roughly 10-15 field ops for most curves. So 80*m group ops is definitely cheaper than 2 FFTs of length 800*m in a 251-bit field. And current experiments with FRI-based SNARKs already show that Merkle-hashing is by far the bottleneck on the small circuits people are using, not FFTs.   https://t.co/a6EmTmfbFR

Final clarification: the prover costs in Lasso are roughly the same for the AND instruction as for all other RISC-V instructions.
[2023-08-12] (x) @cmpeq @dan_tehrani There are several ways you could identify the overflow bit from the limb decomposition. The simplest would just be to do one more lookup, at index b_c, into a table whose i'th entry spits out the relevant high-order bit of i.  4/4
[2023-08-12] (x) @cmpeq @dan_tehrani Each b_i is also range checked via a lookup into the subtable that stores {0, 1, ..., [max value any limb should take]}, namely 2^{32/c}. 

At this point V is guaranteed that (b_1, ..., b_c) is really the prescribed limb-decomposition of z.  3/n
[2023-08-12] (x) @cmpeq @dan_tehrani Let me flesh out more how the Jolt prover identifies the overflow bit of x+y. P commits to the limb-decomposition (b_1, ..., b_c) of the field element z:=x+y. 

A constraint is added to the R1CS to confirm z=sum_{i=1}^c 2^{i-1} * b_i. i.e. This is really a decomposition of z. 2/n
[2023-08-12] (x) @cmpeq @dan_tehrani "cost is another subtable" may not be the way to think about costs. The Lasso prover costs are mainly determined by the number of lookups into the subtables. More subtables don't significantly increase prover costs unless they also mean more lookups into those subtables (1/n)
[2023-08-12] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @srinathtv @samrags_ @moodlezoup Ultimately, that's all the Spark prover commits to: the (indices of the) reads into the subtables, the returned answers, and the returned counts. Those fully specify both RS and WS. (5/5)
[2023-08-12] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @srinathtv @samrags_ @moodlezoup To check that the lookups into each subtable are done correctly, one just confirms RS and WS are permutations of each other. RS and WS are both uniquely specified by the reads into the subtables, the returned answers, and the returned counts. (4/n)
[2023-08-12] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @srinathtv @samrags_ @moodlezoup All this algorithm does is: 

a) initialize c many subtables, one per chunk, where the i'th table evaluates all Lagrange basis polynomials at the i'th chunk of r. 

b) The algorithm then does one lookup into each subtable, for each of the m non-zero coefficients. (3/n)
[2023-08-12] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @srinathtv @samrags_ @moodlezoup When the verifier requests an evaluation of the committed polynomial at point r, the prover proves it correctly ran an evaluation algorithm that takes as input the committed vector(s) and outputs the evaluation. (2/n)
[2023-08-12] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @srinathtv @samrags_ @moodlezoup Not sure this is going to be helpful but let's see:

The commitment in Spark to the m-sparse polynomial is just a commitment to the length-m vector(s) specifying the non-zero coefficients of the polynomial, in chunked form. (1/n)
[2023-08-12] (x) @cmpeq @dan_tehrani For mult(x, y), Jolt does the multiplication in the field, and again it uses lookups to identify all overflow bits and adjust the result appropriately. Is that helpful? (3/3)
[2023-08-12] (x) @cmpeq @dan_tehrani For add(x, y), Jolt has an optimization/simplification: it computes x+y in the finite field, and then uses a single lookup to identify the overflow bit, if any, and adjust the result accordingly. (2/3)
[2023-08-12] (x) @cmpeq @dan_tehrani Oh, I misunderstood what you meant by permutation, I see now that you're asking about how the decompositions work for certain instructions. (1/3)
[2023-08-12] (x) @cmpeq @dan_tehrani I'm not sure what n refers to in the question, but no Lasso/Jolt costs are quadratic in any relevant parameters.
[2023-08-12] (x) @cmpeq @dan_tehrani There is just one permutation check per subtable.  The cost of each for the prover is just a linear number of field operations, as the inputs to the permutation check are already committed (namely the indices of the reads, the resulting values, and the counts).
[2023-08-12] (x) @dcbuilder @fede_intern At this point I'm aware of three criticisms/claims (not all from this thread) that haven't yet been addressed. Let me address each.   

1) If we could do everything with lookups we would already be doing that.  This misses two things. First, Lasso is more than an order of magnitude faster than prior lookups. See the experimental comparison with halo2's, or this tweet for analytic comparison: https://t.co/9gKGQxnEgZ.

Second, Lasso combines subtable results into big-table results with no extra commitment costs. Non-sum-check based techniques can't do that, at least not if the combination involves multiplications.   Both of these, and taking full advantage of them in Jolt, required new techniques and insights.  

2) The performance of Jolt will be bad.   

Here, it would be helpful if you could point to an inaccuracy in our accounting, which clearly shows lower commitment costs for the prover than prior zkVMs, not higher.   

I see two reasonable objections at this stage:  

a) The prover's field work will be more expensive than the commitment costs, so focusing on commitment costs is missing the bottleneck. I am confident this is wrong, but people may be skeptical until Jolt is built and they can see for themselves.  

b) Even though Jolt's P does just ~80 group ops per RISC-V step, that will be more expensive than the FFTs and Merkle-hashing of existing FRI-based zkVMs. 

If we're comparing to FRI with RS rate of 1/16 over 251-bit field, I don't think this is plausible. It's ~80*m group ops (for m steps of RISC-V) to at least (for m steps of Cairo) two FFTs of length 16*51*m over a 251-bit field, and Merkle-hashing the resulting 16*51*m 251-bit field elements.   

I also think it is wrong for FRI other rates/fields, though it may be depend a bit on hardware and hash-function choice.  

And Jolt can work with hashing-based polynomial commitments too.   

2) Jolt's decompositions are obvious and there is nothing new here.  

I agree the AND decomposition is obvious. Other decompositions are less so. Some heavily leverage sum-check's abilities to perform useful computation without commitment costs.  

I would be thrilled if the conclusion everyone comes to is Jolt is obvious! That would be great news for the lookup-singularity approach.

But if that's the case, why are we spending millions of dollars and thousands of person-hours hand-coding optimized circuits, rather than just specifying evaluation tables? 

The possible answers I can see are: 

1) it's not actually obvious or 

2) the performance isn't good. I've addressed the performance issues above.  

BTW When we'd written Lasso but not Jolt, some reacted with "I don't know what structured tables are useful for. Maybe just range checks." Now we've described Jolt and some react "It's obvious that everything can be decomposed".
[2023-08-12] (x) @zkproofs @srinathtv @rel_zeta_tech @fede_intern @dan_tehrani @CPerezz19 @shumochu Pippenger's algorithm conceptually let's the prover commit to one chunk at the same cost as committing to a single bit, namely one group operation (group addition). The chunking takes advantage of that. (3/2)
[2023-08-12] (x) @zkproofs @srinathtv @rel_zeta_tech @fede_intern @dan_tehrani @CPerezz19 @shumochu The output of one lookup (instruction) may be stored into memory and later be read and used as input to another instruction (index of another lookup). At that point it is chunked by the lookup. Nothing is ever decomposed all the way to bits. (2/2)
[2023-08-12] (x) @zkproofs @srinathtv @rel_zeta_tech @fede_intern @dan_tehrani @CPerezz19 @shumochu Didn't see this post before, don't know if it's already been addressed. "To use this in future computations, I need to bit-decompose the output, no?" No. Nothing is ever bit-decomposed in Jolt. Many bits are always packed into a single field element-log(subtable size) many. (1/2)
[2023-08-12] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @samrags_ @moodlezoup If the subtable lookups return b_1, ..., b_c, the final result is   

b_c + \sum_{i=1}^{c-1} 2^{k * (i-1) + 1} * b_i.   

The multiplication by 2^{k * (i-1) + 1} shifts all bits in the first c-1 blocks one spot to the right. (5/4)
[2023-08-12] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @samrags_ @moodlezoup The +a_k here shifts the high-order bit a_k to the low-order bit of big-table result. And the 2^{(c-1)*k + 1} factor shifts other bits in the chunk one spot to the right. (4/4 whoops, one more to come)
[2023-08-12] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @samrags_ @moodlezoup The last chunk leads to a lookup in a table whose (a1, ..., a_{k})'th entry is: 

a_k + 2^{(c-1)*k + 1} \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} 2^{i-1}*a_i. (3/4)
[2023-08-12] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @samrags_ @moodlezoup If there are c chunks each of k bits, the first c-1 chunks lead to a look-up in the "identity" table whose (a_1, ... a_k)'th entry is \sum_{i=1}^k 2^{i-1} * a_i. (2/4)
[2023-08-12] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @samrags_ @moodlezoup I think this is decomposable. It's almost the same as shift instructions that we have to deal with in Jolt. The only difference is that the RISC-V shifts aren't circular, so they treat bits that get shifted past the end of the word differently. (1/4)
[2023-08-12] (x) @david_r_barrera @a16zcrypto @paradigm @coinbase Thanks. I understand your view, but it doesn't get me over the line. Aren't all the cases you cite related to primary transactions? In my view, Howey is much better suited for primary transactions than secondary transactions. For primaries, it's easy to apply. For secondaries, it's really difficult.

Further, I don't think our argument only wins if the court believes there's a slippery slope.

Our argument is that the SEC's contortion of Howey to apply it to secondary transactions in the CB matter is so expansive that it has boundless potential to subject ordinary asset transactions to securities laws. It's not that this is just the first stepping stone to further expanding securities laws to cover additional transactions. With this case, the SEC is anointing itself with a new power - a tenuous expansion of Howey to give itself new vast authority.

As such, a finding that the secondary sales of tokens on CB were securities transactions would mandate in the name of justice that Tesla, used Tesla dealers, Taylor Swift, Ticketmaster and StubHub all be subjected to the same fate.

Just like the token issuers did, Tesla touts future functionality and utility for Teslas that could increase their value (and I don't know about the rest of the country, but in SF many people are buying them as investments) and Taylor hyped the epicness of her to-be-played concerts, driving a very active secondary market for them. Further, both could be said to indirectly benefit from the existence of secondary markets, because the mere existence of such markets makes people more comfortable participating in primary markets.

But neither Tesla nor Taylor marketed and sold Teslas or tickets as investment opportunities or as part of a scheme to generate secondary market activity in those assets. And the SEC similarly failed to allege sufficiently that the token issuers named in the complaint were doing that. It's also worth noting that the token issuers likely put in place 12 month minimum lockup periods on any private sales, thereby ensuring that token acquirers were not purchasing with a view to further distribute to the public (per Rule 144). Neither Tesla nor Taylor apply lockups to their primary sales. 

Ultimately, it'd be wrong result if Teslas or Taylor's concert tickets were deemed to be offered and sold as part of investment contracts. It'd similarly be wrong to find that secondary market transactions of the tokens in the CB complaint are investment contracts.

Furthermore, even if all of the above weren't true, the proper approach here would have been for the SEC to go after the token issuers themselves, and having established that they engaged in unregistered securities offerings, the SEC then could have brought actions against exchanges. Though, as we argued in the brief, the success of those cases (and this one) would depend on whether or not the original distribution and subsequent secondary transactions were all part of a scheme and that common enterprise existed among the issuer and the secondary purchasers.

But the SEC skipped that step because it's inconvenient and time consuming. Instead, it's acting arbitrarily and capriciously in order to target the chokepoints for an industry that it has verbally denigrated and clearly has a vendetta against. No where is this more evident than it's transparent attempts to treat good actors and bad actors as though they're the same.

The world has changed since Howey. Liquid secondary markets are now much more common for all types of assets, not just securities. If Congress wants the SEC to have jurisdiction over those markets, it should say so.  But the Supreme Court says that otherwise cannot be the default.
[2023-08-12] (x) @NYcryptolawyer I don't think it's that simple. In both cases, there wouldn't be an investment contract if courts follow Supreme Court jurisprudence, as outlined in CB's brief.

But if courts reject the requirement that there be an ongoing contractual obligation, then in both cases you could have an investment contract in the primary sale if the issuer/promoter is promoting the asset as an investment opportunity (which is obviously unlikely in the case of concert tickets). Easy to say in those circumstances that the primary sale (whether anonymized or not) is a "scheme" (if that word is read more broadly than to only encompass enforceable contractual obligations).

Even if the primary sales are investment contracts though, subsequent secondary sales shouldn't be unless the primary/secondary sales are all part of a distribution scheme like what takes place in underwritten offerings.

The SEC didn't allege the existence of any scheme like that in the CB matter, so even if the court rejects CB's arguments, the court should still find that the transactions on CB's exchange aren't investment contracts, and should rule in favor of CB.

[2023-08-13] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @dcbuilder @fede_intern The \eq(r, k) values from Line 2 of Figure 4 are random even if the subtables contain small values, but those \eq(r, k) values are not committed.
[2023-08-13] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @dcbuilder @fede_intern In the tables arising in Jolt, those subtables will only contain small values. (continued)
[2023-08-13] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @dcbuilder @fede_intern In Fig 4, E1, ..., Ec contain the results of all reads into subtables. In Spark, those subtables indeed contain large field values--they are all Lagrange basis polynomial evaluations at the evaluation point r. (continued)
[2023-08-13] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @dcbuilder @fede_intern So there's no need for P to commit to those values, or run a lookup argument at all for them. 

These eq(k, rx) values arise in essentially all applications of sum-check, including those that have nothing to do with lookup arguments. (end)
[2023-08-13] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @dcbuilder @fede_intern The only info the verifier in a lookup argument needs to know about the looked-up values is one evaluation of their MLE. V can compute that on her own for these values in logarithmic time. (continued)
[2023-08-13] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @dcbuilder @fede_intern You can view those values as lookups into an MLE-structured table, where we know in advance that the k'th lookup will be to the k'th entry. (continued)
[2023-08-13] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @dcbuilder @fede_intern No, we don't need to commit to those. Those values are all computed by P via field work in sum-check with no explicit proof that P computed them correctly (sum-check itself implicitly checks this). Here's why this works:
[2023-08-13] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @dcbuilder @fede_intern Yes, the log^2(n) hashes for GKR grand product are concretely and asymptotically fewer than the O(lambda * log^2(n)) hashes in FRI.
[2023-08-13] (x) @levs57 @alpeh_v @fede_intern @moodlezoup @nibnalin @samrags_ In case you are instead asking about overhead of Spark vs. Lasso/Surge: 

In Spark (the sparse polynomial commitment) run with parameter c on an m-sparse polynomial, of the 3*c*m + c*N^{1/c} field elements committed, c*m of them are random. In Lasso all of them are small.
[2023-08-13] (x) @levs57 @alpeh_v @fede_intern @moodlezoup @nibnalin @samrags_ Overhead relative to just verifying the lookups into each subtable?

The only extra work is one application of sum-check (see Step 2 of Figures 3/4 in the Lasso paper). That's linear field work for P, and highly parallelizable. It won't be a bottleneck. (1/2)
[2023-08-13] (x) @levs57 @alpeh_v @fede_intern @moodlezoup @nibnalin Some more details of the benchmarks are in the slides from my first talk, which I've now posted here: https://t.co/OhylENCIAb. We'll eventually provide a comparison where both systems use the same curve libraries (apologies to @moodlezoup and @samrags_ if this is super painful)
[2023-08-13] (x) @levs57 @alpeh_v @fede_intern @moodlezoup @nibnalin There are two reasons to expect speedups for small tables: committing to fewer field elements, and small field elements. For m lookups into table of size N, it's m+N small elements for Lasso vs. about 3*max{m, N} random field elements for Halo2 (plus some more small ones).

[2023-08-14] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @srinathtv @UHaboeck @benediktbuenz @mitschabaude For some polynomial commitments (Bulletproofs/IPA in particular), that evaluation proof is very slow and could be a bottleneck even with the batching. Other polynomial commitments support much faster evaluation proof computation.
[2023-08-14] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @srinathtv @UHaboeck @benediktbuenz @mitschabaude Those are all dense polynomials, so no need for Spark. 

Evaluation proofs can be batched, so there is just one for the whole protocol. If Lasso is used as a subroutine in a larger protocol like Jolt, the batching can include any polynomials committed outside of Lasso as well.
[2023-08-14] (x) @benediktbuenz @UHaboeck @srinathtv @mitschabaude MLE structured means that this polynomial t you mention is quickly evaluable. Am I misunderstanding?

To prove that MLE-structure isn't any more general than decomposability, you'd have to take any quickly-evaluable MLE and turn it into a subtable-decomposition. I don't think that should be possible in general.

BTW GeneralizedLasso doesn't actually require MLE-structure, any low-degree extension will do (the prover's field work does increase with degree of the LDE).
[2023-08-14] (x) @UHaboeck @srinathtv @benediktbuenz @mitschabaude By avoiding large values, I mean all committed field elements are in (say) {0, 1, ..., 2^20}, no matter how big is the underlying field for the lookup argument.

Surge uses a tensor representation (what I call subtables) primarily to reduce the size of the committed vectors, not to avoid large values. 

The tensor representation does have a positive side effect that subtables typically have smaller values than the big table, so the tensor decomposition does help value size a bit. But it's not actually any better to commit to two b-bit values relative to committing to one (2b)-bit value, so I view this effect as minimizing the cost of using a tensor decomposition, rather than an overall win.
[2023-08-14] (x) @UHaboeck @srinathtv @benediktbuenz @mitschabaude In summary, the main situation where our work would involve commitments to random field elements is for a very large MLE-structured table, where you'd want to invoke GeneralizedLasso with c&gt;=2. In other cases, all committed field elements are small.
[2023-08-14] (x) @UHaboeck @srinathtv @benediktbuenz @mitschabaude To be clear, in the unstructured case, there has to be a commitment to the MLE of the table produced in pre-processing.

In this case, all the committed field elements are small. But the evaluation proof may be the dominant cost, depending on the choice of polynomial commitment.
[2023-08-14] (x) @UHaboeck @srinathtv @benediktbuenz @mitschabaude If the table is totally unstructured, it's probably best to use Lasso with c=1, which is a bit of a special case. This has the prover commit to m+N counts (all small) and produce one evaluation proof for (the aggregation of) multilinear polynomials of size an most max{m, N}.
[2023-08-14] (x) @UHaboeck @srinathtv @benediktbuenz @mitschabaude But we don't yet have a specific example of a useful table that's MLE-structured but not decomposable.
[2023-08-14] (x) @UHaboeck @srinathtv @benediktbuenz @mitschabaude If the table is MLE-structured instead of decomposable (it's MLE can be evaluated in say logarithmic time), that's right. You'll need to use GeneralizedLasso for this case. It invokes Spark, which involves lookups into arbitrary-valued subtables E_rx and E_ry.
[2023-08-14] (x) @UHaboeck @srinathtv @benediktbuenz @mitschabaude Yes. The lookup argument that achieves this (assuming the subtables only contain small values) is in Section 5 of the Lasso paper. Jolt describes the subtables/decomposition for each of the RISC-V instructions.
[2023-08-14] (x) @srinathtv @UHaboeck @benediktbuenz @mitschabaude The subtables E_{rx}, E_{ry} arising in the Spark sparse polynomial commit indeed contain Lagrange basis polynomials evaluated at r_x, r_y, which will be large/random field elements.

But Jolt (by applying Lasso to decomposable tables) avoids subtables with large values.
[2023-08-14] (x) @srinathtv @UHaboeck @benediktbuenz @mitschabaude In case it's helpful, this topic was discussed here and in the following discussion: https://t.co/kTB2OwBZ9D
[2023-08-14] (x) @benediktbuenz @mitschabaude Yes! I have in mind a different modification of Plookup, dealing with the i ==&gt; i+1 issue as per Section 5.1 of the CCS paper here: https://t.co/3fPQ7ezKrm. This may have benefits in terms of avoiding indexing and improving constant factors.
[2023-08-14] (x) @mitschabaude @o1_labs Yes! The lookup table formulation may seem artificial/unnecessarily complicated. But it leads to the proposed "lookup singularity" approach that is a major departure from how things are done today.
[2023-08-14] (x) @mitschabaude Whoops, I think I overstated the number of multiplications that are strictly necessary in the aggregation procedure for LTU by a factor of 2, since there are "partial products" that can be reused. It doesn't affect the qualitative comparison of Jolt to existing techniques though.
[2023-08-14] (x) @mitschabaude In terms of techniques, I do not actually think there is huge novelty in Lasso. The techniques are already there in Spark. Plus, I suspect you could get a lookup argument with similar costs to Lasso with c=1 from Plookup by simply replacing the grand product argument with Thaler13 or Section 6 of the Quarks paper (there are some details I haven't worked through here). 

Here's my view of what's going on. Today's popular SNARKs have mainly been optimized for proof size instead of prover time. It's awesome that we have SNARKs with very short proofs. Those will be forever useful as the final step in SNARK recursion/proof aggregation to get low V costs.

But the scalability bottleneck for awhile now has been prover time. And the right technique to unlock this is sum-check, due to its ability to minimize commitment costs for the prover. (To be clear, sum-check isn't the only technique we'll need. For example, it likely should be combined with folding to control prover space: https://t.co/6EPH5s2vj5) 

What Lasso/Jolt mainly do is clarify exactly how sum-check-based protocols can completely avoid the key bottlenecks in today's popular SNARKs, and then push this to a limit with the lookup singularity approach to front-end design. (2/2)
[2023-08-14] (x) @mitschabaude I agree that people are already doing decomposition for tables like bitwiseAND. This is explicit in Sam and Michael's post on "Understanding Lookups": https://t.co/XmNHz7p82Y

The advantages of Lasso are a matter of performance, generality, and perspective. 

An example might help. Consider the LTU(x, y) instruction (less-than unsigned) in 64-bit RISC-V, see Section 4.2.2 of Jolt. 

Jolt decomposes (x, y) into, say, c=6 chunks of ~22 bits each, and for each chunk it does a lookup into two different subtables of size 2^22 (namely, LTU and EQUALITY on two 11-bit inputs). 

That means P commits to about 6*2*3=36 small field elements in total across all the subtable lookups.

The results are combined via something like 1+2+3+4+5=15 multiplications and 5 additions. Those come "for free" in Jolt, meaning they don't increase P's commitment costs, only field work.

Let's say you applied Plonk to a circuit with 15 multiplications and 5 addition gates. P would commit to ~7 random field elements per gate, or ~140 random field elements in total (plus more small field elements). For P time, that's the equivalent of over 1400 small field elements. That's not even counting the cost of subtable lookups, just aggregating the results. 

This comparison isn't really fair since Plonk is not targeted at uniform circuits, while Jolt heavily exploits uniformity to minimize commitment costs. But it gives a sense of how Lasso/Jolt compare to some SNARKs that are popular today. (1/2)
[2023-08-14] (x) @srinathtv @rel_zeta_tech @dcbuilder @fede_intern Yes, other than exposition (and the need to explicitly describe Spark for all c&gt;2, for use in GeneralizedLasso), the only new content in Sections 3-4 is the stronger security analysis (showing it's secure even when counts may be maliciously committed).
[2023-08-14] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @dcbuilder @fede_intern If the subtables T_i are MLE-structured, you don't need to precompute commitments to them. All of the subtables in Jolt are structured. 

The only info V needs about T_i is to evaluate its MLE at a random point. If that's fast, no need for a commitment.
[2023-08-14] (x) @alpeh_v It also doesn’t reflect user or developer adoption, and doesn’t directly map onto revenue for most protocols. It’s a very bad metric.
[2023-08-14] (x) Can confirm from my attempt to have GPT write Jeopardy-style questions this afternoon...

[2023-08-15] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @mitschabaude @o1_labs In Section 5, g gets evaluated on every one of the m lookups, so V can't afford to do that work, P has to do it, and prove it was done correctly. V winds up evaluating g just once in Lasso.  

Yes, the Plonk costs I referred to in previous tweets were for Plonk without custom constraints. But the commitment cost of Plonk's P (with custom constraints) grows linearly with the degree of the constraints (which is 6 for the LTU instruction). This is not the case for Lasso or other sum-check-based techniques, where only P's field work grows with that degree.
[2023-08-15] (x) @mitschabaude @o1_labs Adding to this after reflection, we came upon the notion of decomposable tables because they naturally arise in sparse polynomial evaluation, where the "big table" is all Lagrange basis polynomials evaluated at the evaluation point r, and the subtables are Lagrange basis polynomials evaluated at chunks of r. And the best-known algorithm for the problem literally fills in these subtables, and then does lookups into them.

We then came to view examples like bitwise AND as a very special case of a decomposable table.

So while the notion of lookups into decomposable tables may seem an unnecessarily complicated way of viewing certain instructions, this view first arose in other settings where it is completely natural. And it seems to be a useful way of viewing even the simpler cases.

[2023-08-15] (x) @david_r_barrera @a16zcrypto @paradigm @coinbase Your assertions not only conflict with one another, but they run counter to what Howey actually says and the SEC's own interpretation of the law. In particular:

(1) You say: "The SEC is not applying Howey to secondary transactions at all. The SEC is only applying Howey to determine whether a token-based investment contract scheme was originally established and is ongoing."  

But what's the test for whether an *investment contract scheme is ongoing*? It's Howey. As the SEC has outlined, if purchasers of tokens are no longer relying on the managerial efforts of others, then securities laws should not apply to such purchases (https://t.co/JKydEnVvCI). Maybe the SEC tries to distance itself from that guidance, but that's easier said than done in court.

(2) You say:  "The SEC’s response to Coinbase’s motion will be the first time the SEC properly puts forward a legal theory for why it thinks the tokens are securities when traded (the complaint was not the place to do that)."

Why wouldn't the complaint have been the proper place to explain why secondary market transactions in the assets were securities transactions? That's the SEC's primary argument as to why CB needed to register. You're assuming the SEC is going to now introduce a novel legal theory it hasn't presented in any other context?  

(3) The fact that all of those other asset transactions are in the "gray zone" is exactly the problem with the SEC's legal theory. It potentially sweeps in a vast number of asset transactions that have never been thought to be under the purview of the SEC, and therefore will introduce significant uncertainty to many markets. The SEC cannot anoint itself with such powers. Only Congress can.

[2023-08-16] (x) @mmblaszczyk @UNDLawReview Nice work. I agree that the DAO LLC forms that have been adopted to date don't provide a more relaxed framework than what is already available under existing LLC statutes. The same can be said about the decentralized Unincorporated Nonprofit Association (UNA) entity forms that were recently proposed in CA and TX. I think that's a good thing, as there's no way to justify providing blockchain-based startups with advantages under legal entity laws as compared to other industries.

The one caveat I'd make is that Utah's DAO law came close to departing from the above trend, as it was based on the model COALA. That law was developed in Europe and incorporates a mix of legal entity attributes (including in regards to tax) that you don't find in existing legal entity structures in the US. So, had Utah stuck to the model COALA and not broken from it in a few key ways, it would have potentially been evidence of the "race to the bottom" that LoPucki alleges.

[2023-08-20] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @srinathtv @samrags_ @moodlezoup Interesting! I still think Option 2 of Pippenger is optimal for the parameter regime we consider. 

The parameter p from the DJB paper you linked to is our number of variables (what Lasso calls log N where N is big-table size) and the parameter q is sparsity (what Lasso calls m). 

It seems Pippenger showed that about pq/log(pq) mults are *necessary* for evaluating all m monomials with non-zero coefficients (in some parameter regimes anyway). 

Let me translate to Lasso's notation. Suppose N=m^c and c is not too big (say, a constant like 6, or perhaps even something slightly superconstant). Then DJB's pq/log(pq) equals roughly c*m in Lasso. This is exactly the number of multiplications that Pippenger's Option 2 uses (not counting the extra ~c*m mults required to fill in the subtables). 

At the end of Section 6 of the link, DJB states that 2-level Recursive Pippenger can reduce this by a factor of about 2 to p*q/(2 log q), which in our notation would be about (c/2) * m, a factor-2 improvement. However, this is hiding an ~O(m^2) term, which DJB is considering to be low-order, but in Lasso/Jolt's parameter regime would hugely dominate the c*m term. 

In summary, a lot of the literature on Pippenger's algorithm seems to consider the regime where m is vastly smaller than log(N). But in Lasso (and I think for SNARK-relevant MSMs more generally, where m is typically the size of the MSM and log(N)~=256 is log of group size), this is not the relevant regime, and non-recursive variants/special cases of Pippenger are what's relevant.

[2023-08-29] (x) @zkproofs @williamborgeaud @recmo One last point: (D*z)^2 = 0 is the same as D*z=0. 

Did you mean D*z \circ E*z = 0? This fits squarely in the definition of CCS quoted by Remco.

 Or did you mean D*[z entrywise squared]=0?
[2023-08-29] (x) @zkproofs @williamborgeaud @recmo BTW I suppose one could view this as exactly the reason copy constraints are unnecessary.
[2023-08-29] (x) @zkproofs @williamborgeaud @recmo No. To have P commit to a vector guaranteed to be of the form (z, z), just have P commit to z. 

The MLE of (z, z) has one extra variable relative to z itself. The first variable is simply ignored, and the remaining variables are fed into the commitment to z.

[2023-09-03] (x) @rel_zeta_tech @Zac_Aztec EC-based schemes are definitely best if working over large fields (either because an application calls for that, or because it simplifies implementation). 6-12 field ops per ~20-bit-committed-value is a tough bar to clear. 

To commit to N values, (subsequent improvements to) Brakedown use roughly ~10*N field ops plus 1.5*N hash evaluations, and may be memory-bound (perhaps @dlubarov has some information available here). 

On the other hand, Brakedown has the benefit of working over any field, without paying the very high price of something like ECFFT. That matters a great deal for applications natively defined over a field that's not FFT friendly and doesn't match up with some standard elliptic curve. But otherwise, speedups from field-agnosticism shouldn't outweigh increased field ops and/or the need for hashing and/or memory-boundedness. 

I do have high hopes that improvements to the code will bring 10*N down and that we'll see faster hash functions, which could flip things if the progress is really substantial. 

Brakedown and descendants should be better over small fields. I think that's already true today (not by a huge amount) if you don't worry about recursion.

It's not clear to me how close we are to full-fledged recursion with Brakedown. Orion only did "partial" recursion, leading to MB-sized proofs. Vortex did better in this regard but uses Reed-Solomon codes, so gives up a lot of the benefits of Brakedown. 

Long-term, I expect any recursion struggles to be addressed, and hence for Brakedown to be better than EC-based commitments whenever working over small fields makes sense.

[2023-09-05] (x) @dlubarov @PapiniShahar @rel_zeta_tech @bfarmer @wyatt_benno @cmpeq Whoops, I meant 75% increase in FFT costs (15 cycles to 26 cycles), not 40%. Additions are surprisingly expensive by this measure.
[2023-09-05] (x) @dlubarov @PapiniShahar @rel_zeta_tech @bfarmer @wyatt_benno @cmpeq Oh I see, thank you! 

I was confused by the combination of ambiguous parentheses and the slide ignoring the cost of additions in the FFT, which are ~40% of the multiplication costs according to the reference they are taken from (Table 1, Apple M1, here: https://t.co/YgFkTu8Z7n).

My comment should be rephrased. There's not a missing FFT. Sincere apologies for the error. 

But if you don't ignore additions, the FFT cycle counts still go up by 40% and total cycle count by 25%. 

I agree that, after accounting for additions, this is an upper bound on the FFT cost.
[2023-09-05] (x) @PapiniShahar @rel_zeta_tech @bfarmer @wyatt_benno @cmpeq Sorry, are you saying that the FFT + IFFT cost is:

(1/2) * (B+1) * log(N) for N=2^20?

The slide had the (B+1) inside the log (the parentheses were ambiguous, but as best as I can tell, inside the log is what the cycle counts correspond to). 

With B+1 outside of the log, the cycle count for the FFT + IFFT alone is as high as 30. And the total cycle count for FRI goes up to 54 from 40.
[2023-09-05] (x) @bfarmer @wyatt_benno @cmpeq Do you at least agree there should be two FFTs instead of one in the counts for FRI?

*I don't think I understand the claim that a single MSM of length 2^25 is going to increase latency. 2^25 is tiny. There are hardware companies that can do 2^30-length MSMs in under a second (see https://t.co/GYVpNtsmy2 for discussion of this).

The real sequential bottleneck to be wary of is deep recursion. You can't parallelize that: I can't begin to prove I know proof A until I've actually computed proof A. 

To get the kind of blockchain scalability people are hoping for, we're going to have to roll up a lot of transactions in each on-chain proof. If you're breaking things into the tiniest pieces possible, the depth will be high. And if everything is highly parallelizable except the recursion, this is going to be the latency bottleneck. 

So I'd make the opposite point: if one is concerned about latency, one should avoid breaking things into the tiniest pieces possible. 

Finally, as one concrete data point: today Polygon zkEVM uses a circuit with 2^23 rows and 1164 columns, which is 2^33 cells, to process about 10 million gas of transactions (as I've said before, I don't consider this circuit to be big, even if it does currently require hundreds of GBs of prover space. Or at least I don't consider the functionality it's computing to be big). 

I hope Lasso/Jolt help make circuits smaller, but I'm not sure how one can object to one (2^25)-size MSM as a latency bottleneck, when the circuits we're dealing with today for simple functionalities are 2^23 rows and 2^33 cells.

*The slide considers the Baby Bear field, with blowup factor 2, and Blake2 as the hash. At least four of the most prominent projects in the entire ecosystem use or will use FRI (Polygon zkEVM, RISC Zero, StarkWare, and Matter Labs), and AFAIK not a single one uses more than one of those three parameters. You don't think that's relevant? Either every one of these four projects is doing something sub-optimal, or there's a good reason they aren't using these parameters. 

Each of those parameters was chosen in the benchmark to make the comparison as favorable as possible to FRI. 

A more general point is that the public discussion in this space has been deeply confused because of a failure to distinguish between what projects are actually doing, and what they could be doing but aren't. See for example Items 3 and 7 of my blog post on misconceptions about SNARKs https://t.co/8Jozniv0ug. 

This is a much bigger concern for security considerations, but it matters for performance discussion too. 

*There is an obvious benefit to using the 252-bit field, which is that it allows for native field arithmetic in proving knowledge valid ECDSA signatures (see https://t.co/3MPZLIETVx and https://t.co/xRqqbqzQ7d). 

The slide itself (after accounting for the missing FFT) implies that using the 252-bit field is resulting in a 33x slowdown for the prover (probably more like ~10x after accounting for prover costs from outside of FRI). If the big field is a mere artifact, with no advantages and such enormous costs, yet fixing the massive performance issue is not a priority, then why should anything about this deployment inform benchmarking choices?

*To raise another benefit of using large fields: In Jolt, you can implement RISC-V multiplication by multiplying two 64-bit data types natively in the field and correcting for any overflow. This approach requires a prime of size at least (2^64-1)^2. 

This is smaller than the order of an elliptic curve group, but much bigger than Baby Bear, Goldilocks, etc. Handling something like RISC-V multiplication over such small fields entails committing to extra field elements, which is not something the slide accounts for.

*I brought up curve25519 to point out that vectorization of EC operations is possible, not to say that it is as easy as with 31-bit field arithmetic. 

My general sense is that vectorization of EC operations is much less mature than it could be, possibly because large-scale applications needing high-throughput didn't really exist before SNARKs. I think the ZPrize helped with this, but that was just one event.

I think if EC-based SNARKs took over the world, there would be lots of progress on vectorizing EC operations, on GPUs and other hardware. 

And the objection that I brought up a curve that is not recursion friendly is a double-standard. The slide advocated for Blake2 even though it's not very recursion-friendly, and this tweet is coming in a thread where you advocated using elliptic curves over prime-power fields even though they are not recursion friendly. 

*To clarify my views, small fields definitely have a role to play and I have high hopes advance them more in the near future.

Alf -- short for Alfred -- attended Doromere University, where he studied forensic pattern analysis. He has the uncanny ability to see patterns in the way that others see shapes and colors. He left school after a disagreement over authorship of a seminal research paper.

[2023-09-15] (x) And if you're feeling mathematically nostalgic for the year we're leaving behind, check out this thread: 👇👀 https://t.co/L4iGYqPkBY
[2023-09-15] (x) 𝐒𝐡𝐚𝐧𝐚𝐡 𝐭𝐨𝐯𝐚 𝟓𝟕𝟖𝟒! 🍎🍯

This year will be made up of many powerfully golden moments, and will be especially sweet and delicious, no matter which calendar you're using.

How do I know that? 𝐌𝐚𝐭𝐡!

5784 the sum of (the ceilings of) the first 17 powers of the Golden Ratio.

And 5784 + 2023 = 7807 is a "cake number" – delicious 🍰! 

(The cake numbers reflect the largest number of pieces that can be made from N planar cuts through a cube-shaped cake. The next odd cake number is 10701, so the next "double-cake year" will be 7231, in the calendar year 3470!)

And that's not all... 

* Every digit in 5784's square (33454656) appears precisely twice. This also happens in 5789, and then not again until 5830. There are only finitely many such years in total; the last one is 9994363488.

* A circle of diameter 87 centered at the origin encloses 5784 unit-square lattice cells.

* The 5784-th prime is a Boustrophedon prime.

* And there are 5784 3-regular simple graphs on 20 vertices with girth at least 5. (With 18 vertices, there are only 455 – and with 22, there are 90940.)

* Moreover, 5784 is a "lazy Pell–Niven number" (i.e., it is divisible by the sum of the digits in its lazy Pell representation, 2020220220), as are the next two years, 5785 and 5786. (We next kick off a string of three such years in 17459.)

* And 5784 is the number of 8-step self-avoiding walks on a cubic lattice confined inside a 2x2x2 box, starting from the center of the box.

* Plus 5784 is a multiple of 24 whose digits sum to 24, and 5784 = 10*24^2 + 24.

* The sum of the divisors of 5784 reversed (i.e., 4875) is equal to the sum of the divisors of 5784-1 = 5783 reversed plus the sum of the divisors of 5784-2 = 5782 reversed. This next happens in 7852.

* 5784 = Sum_{k=1}^{8} [(k(k+1))^2/2].

* And 5784 is the number of triples (w,x,y) with terms in {0,...,21} such that w, x, y, |w-x|, |x-y|, and |y-w| are all distinct. It is also the number of quadruples (w,x,y,z) with terms in {1,...,4} such that w+x = 3(y+z).

* Moreover, 5784 is the number of permutations of 2,...,9 with no element divisible by an adjacent element. 

* The sum of 5784's anti-divisors ({16, 23, 43, 48, 269, 503, 3856}, with sum 4758) is a permutation of 5784's digits.

* Moreover, 1+16*5784^2, 1+16*(5784+1)^2, and 1+16*(5784+2)^2 are all prime. (This next happens in 6244.) 9*10^5784 + 2*111...111 - 1 is prime, as well (where here 111...111 has 5784 digits all equal to 1).

* And on top of that, 5784 is the number of sets of nonempty words with a total of 10 letters over a quaternary alphabet, containing the fourth letter, such that within each prefix of a word, every letter of the alphabet is at least as frequent as the subsequent alphabet letter. 

If that all doesn't bode well for the year, then I don't know what does!

QED 😀

[2023-09-20] (x) @zach__davidson @bgurley Great speech, but not all regulation is created equal. 

There are certainly regulations that could consolidate power among centralized actors in crypto (like California’s new AB 39).

But because web3 is all about decentralization, a lot of web3 regulation focuses on protecting that category. And by definition, that favors startups over centralized incumbents (who mostly can’t decentralize). 

The Jobs Act is another example of an effort to favor startups over incumbents.

[2023-09-25] (x) @jessewldn Agreed. But I think it’s harder to get a bigger pie if you don’t have decentralization along every line. For instance, technical decentralization also gives devs comfort that the original team won’t rug them.

Ultimately, what we currently have isn’t optimal. FIT would force some decentralization but it doesn’t forbid teams from continuing to develop, so it’s probably a step in the right direction. But whether the decentralization threshold needs to be higher or lower is definitely open for debate.
[2023-09-25] (x) @jessewldn Agreed, but also think users would ultimately lose without true decentralization. The teams building protocols would find ways to ensure the network effects of the protocols accrue to themselves rather than to users, as that’s the economically rational outcome. So, we’d end up with the same problems monolithic orgs create in web2.

Regulation can be a good forcing mechanism for true decentralization, but when the rules aren’t clear and evenly enforced, builders are incentivized to cut corners. As a result, we end up with a lot of decentralization theater.

[2023-10-03] (x) The SEC's opposition to @coinbase's motion has a lot of holes. Even if the court were to agree with the SEC's main contention (that investment contracts don't require legal contracts), the SEC's case should still fail.

As we discussed in our brief in August, the SEC's theory of an investment contract has endless breadth. While purporting to apply Howey, the SEC untethers that decision from its bedrock requirements, converting the requirement of reasonable profit expectations from the efforts of others into a free-floating expectation of profits even if the expectation is not enforceable, reasonably believed to be enforceable, or tied to the actions of the developer or promoter itself.
[2023-10-03] (x) @__tux @kobigurk Yes, but users that get blocked mistakenly can always use another wallet. So, as long as they don't lose access to their funds, the cost of being blocked is low. 

But you're right. Even if the cost was low, this mechanism would leave open the possibility that someone deploys a bot that seeks to have every user of a protocol banned. That's a valid concern. 

I'm not sure regulators would say it's sufficient to warrant not including a banning mechanism. But it seems like it could be justification for removing a banning mechanism in the event an attack is carried out.

[2023-10-04] (x) We're watching this, too. The market is a factor, but since the app layer of crypto is becoming more active we're curious how much crypto dev is now happening outside of the open source infra repos reflected here.

Take four of our recent funding announcements: @blackbird, @StoryProtocol, @ProofOfPlay, and @iyk_app. All are working principally in the app layer in private repos, and none are counted in this measure.

Our State of Crypto Index also includes an important counter-metric, which is the number of NPM downloads for some key crypto libraries — which hit an all-time high this month. This is why it's important to use multiple methods to track key metrics.

In order to track active crypto developers, we use @ElectricCapital's popular ecosystems project (linked in the next post) to identify crypto-related Github repos. If you have suggestions for how to do this better in an open source way, we'd love to hear from you.
[2023-10-04] (x) @btshapir @jt_kerwin @asv141 @soashworth I think the idea is that there is often useful signal in reading the text of how the referees reacted to your paper. For example, I recently had a paper where two different referees raised the same issue about the exposition in slightly different ways. I'm not sure I would have gotten the same amount of value out of the editor just saying "you have this problem with the exposition".

[2023-10-10] (x) @ErlendMf @PikaGoldin @nberpubs Nope! Unlike with many other major awards, Nobel laureates are not notified in advance -- they find out just minutes before the public announcement -- usually around 5:00 a.m. Eastern. And by that time, the NBER email had already gone out. It's sent automatically in the middle of the night -- in this case, it went out at 1:10 a.m. on Monday morning. So the email actually arrived in inboxes ~4 hours before @PikaGoldin would have been aware she won the prize 🤯.

[2023-10-16] (x) Every time I teach the Prim-Jarnik minimum spanning tree algorithm (like last week), I start by telling the students "Dijkstra's shortest-path algorithm works great, let's try to copy that." 

And every time, I think to myself, "that sounds like the dumbest idea ever":
-shortest-path problem has a source, MST does not
-Dijkstra needs nonnegative edge lengths/costs, PJ doesn't care
-PJ needs an undirected graph, Dijkstra doesn't care
-correctness of PJ fundamentally driven by matroid structure of MSTs, Dijkstra by the dynamic-programming-style recursive optimal substructure of shortest paths (PJ even spits at the matroid structure by treating vertices as first-order objects)

And yet, "copying Dijkstra" couldn't really work out any better. 🤷‍♂️
