[2023-10-16] (x) 11/11  Overall, AB 39 is a good step towards regulating centralized actors in web3, but it has many flaws.

Applying a one-size-fits-all reg regime to financial institutions and video games is an obvious mistake.

If the legislature doesn’t fix the bill, Californians will lose.
[2023-10-16] (x) 10/ 4⃣Finally, the bill impedes stablecoin innovation - it prohibits companies from giving Californians access to stablecoins that aren’t 100% fiat backed, unless regulators approve. 

While consumers need protection, this approach creates regulatory capture. That's bad.
[2023-10-16] (x) 9/ Of course, this language won’t drive decentralized systems to obtain licenses - their decentralized status precludes that from happening. 

Instead, developers will either block Californians or remove emergency pause functions, exposing users to more risk. 

That’s lose-lose.
[2023-10-16] (x) 8/ 3⃣The bill’s “Control” definition threatens decentralization - it potentially requires decentralized systems to obtain a license merely because their smart contracts have emergency pause functionality.

So, AB 39 punishes developers for trying to protect consumers. https://t.co/JJiZ1jgAZM
[2023-10-16] (x) 7/ When combined, these two issues are a crushing blow to the video game industry, particularly in California. 

For indie game developers, the cost of compliance will likely be prohibitive, meaning they’ll have to choose between California and innovation.
[2023-10-16] (x) 6/ 2⃣To make matters worse, the bill contains debilitating ambiguities that create significant uncertainty.

For example, while the authors of the bill have indicated they don’t intend NFTs to be captured, the definition below could be read to include anything and everything. https://t.co/3DOJjBds1P
[2023-10-16] (x) 5/ The bill essentially proposes to protect gamers from risks of ownership of in-game digital assets by ensuring they don’t ever become owners.

This not only deprives gamers of the value they create, it paves the way for corporations, not consumers, to own the metaverse. https://t.co/jTAhjg94ml
[2023-10-16] (x) 4/ The Bad: Four Areas of Concern

1⃣ The bill is an unnecessary attack on the video game industry’s future - it requires developers to obtain a license if they want to enable users to truly own their in-game digital assets.

So, AB 39 punishes developers for empowering gamers. https://t.co/5KQSwQbTDu
[2023-10-16] (x) 3/ Second, AB 39 is a substantial improvement over previous CA bills, including the bill that @GavinNewsom vetoed last year.

AB 39 provides greater clarity and reduces the overly burdensome compliance requirements included in the prior bill.

But…
[2023-10-16] (x) 2/ The Good:

First, the bill forces trusted centralized intermediaries like exchanges and CeFi into a licensing regime that should mitigate some of the most significant risks plaguing the space.

This is a great and much needed step forward.
[2023-10-16] (x) 1/ On Friday, @GavinNewsom signed into law a licensing regime for web3 companies with any users in California. #AB39

While the regime does some good, it has several issues and could be a disaster for the video game industry. To his credit, Newsom has called for fixes.

Quick 🧵 https://t.co/QVhK05xcpg

[2023-10-26] (x) 7/ But we strongly agree with Commissioner Mersinger’s dissent that the CFTC should have  brought these products/services into a sandbox that would foster innovation. 

The CFTC’s mandate to promote responsible innovation is being undermined by a lack of action.
[2023-10-26] (x) 6/ The CFTC has demonstrated a more principled approach. 

The regulations that the CFTC uses clearly apply to the business activity challenged and their application was foreseeable. 

The actions, in our evaluation, do not violate notions of fairness and due process.
[2023-10-26] (x) 5/ The SEC's extension of broker rules under U.S. securities laws to the activity engaged in by Coinbase in relation to its wallet is both unnecessary and counterproductive – guidance and tailored rulemaking would do more to protect investors and foster financial innovation.
[2023-10-26] (x) 4/ Nevertheless, both the CFTC and SEC actions received lower ratings because of their regulation-by-enforcement approach and their failure to foster innovation. 

Beyond that, the actions differ substantially with respect to their application of existing laws.
[2023-10-26] (x) 3/ Overall, the actions we analyzed are generally consistent with RANP’s focus on businesses rather than software - there is no conclusive evidence of “targeting developers” solely for developing, publishing, and deploying code.
[2023-10-26] (x) 2/ We examine these actions under our “Regulate Web3 Apps, Not Protocols'' framework (RANP), looking at whether the actions appropriately target business activity rather than software and its developers (the key tenet of RANP).

[2023-11-03] (x) It's the beginning of the month, so we've updated our State of Crypto Index. As part of this update, we've also made improvements to two measures: (1) mobile wallet users and (2) developer library downloads.

(1) We reexamined the source for our mobile wallet data and after comparing it with a variety of other first and third party sources, chose to switch to SensorTower instead.

(2) We've added viem to our dev lib downloads. Although it is relatively new, viem has grown quickly to ~1m monthly downloads — which is a modest but meaningful change to the measure and better reflects actual developer use.

[2023-11-05] (x) @ben_golub Not to mention the piece of evidence he describes actually goes in the opposite direction of his claim: the fact that these products have moved upmarket to the degree that they can price at the same level as incumbents actually means they have *succeeded* at being disruptive!  Disruption doesn't mean having lower price than incumbents in equilibrium, but rather providing new options and affordances that eventually become good enough to compete in the main market. (Plus I'd be surprised if the presence of these competitors hadn't affected overall market prices by changing the level and form of demand....)

[2023-11-17] (x) More than 40 million Americans have purchased crypto, but there are signs that the US may be losing its lead in web3. Good policy can help keep the development of this critically important technology onshore while also protecting consumers. This is a matter of national security.
[2023-11-17] (x) Next up: a hierarchy of models, ordered according to the "degree of permissionlessness" (in the spirit of the classical hierarchies of fault models and message delay assumptions) (8/9)
[2023-11-17] (x) So by a "permissionless consensus protocol," we mean one that functions correctly (e.g., satisfies safety and liveness) even in the presence of one or more of these challenges (7/9)
[2023-11-17] (x) All three challenges are built in to our model from the start: (6/9) https://t.co/D9OrcLAQgW
[2023-11-17] (x) Every subset of these challenges defines a distinct model, with its own set of possibility and impossibility results for reaching consensus (5/9)
[2023-11-17] (x) In our paper, we formalize this idea via three distinct challenges that Nakamoto consensus overcomes: (4/9) https://t.co/2zy4Nfo6kM
[2023-11-17] (x) So what's novel about Bitcoin's consensus protocol ("Nakamoto consensus") is not the problem it solves (SMR), but the very weak assumptions under which it solves it. Namely, the protocol assumes nothing about who might be running it (3/9)
[2023-11-17] (x) The Bitcoin protocol solves a classical consensus problem known as "state machine replication (SMR)." In Bitcoin, the "state" is basically the current set of UTXOs. Researchers in distributed computing/systems have worked on SMR protocols since at least the 1980s (2/9)
[2023-11-17] (x) A quick thread on what @AndrewLewisPye and I mean by "permissionless consensus" (1/9)

[2023-11-23] (x) I was involved with Coinbase for many years, and I was proud of @brian_armstrong and team for always taking the compliant approach. Even when it wasn’t always clear what the compliant approach was, they took every measure to follow the letter and spirit of the law. This came at an enormous cost - both in terms of investing in a large legal & compliance team and in terms of the speed at which Coinbase could introduce new products.

It was a completely uneven playing field. Competitors like FTX and Binance were taking market share and rapidly introducing new products in ways that at the time appeared to push – but now we know exceeded – the boundaries of the law.

It’s great to see that our judicial system and regulators are enforcing our laws and that a compliant approach like the one Coinbase took can win in the long term.

There are hundreds of crypto/web3 companies in the US who are similarly trying to follow the law, often with little guidance on how to comply or interpret the rules. Coinbase had the fortitude and resources to stick it out in the long run. Not every company will be able to make a similar journey - some will get taken out by unscrupulous competitors along the way, or will get overwhelmed with legal and compliance costs. That’s why it’s so important for our policymakers and regulators to take the actions needed to protect innovation and consumers, and weed out bad actors. Clear market structure regulation is the best place to start. Such regulations will make compliance possible and affordable for well-intentioned startups, helping to keep them in the U.S., and will also empower enforcement agencies to remove bad actors before they take customers and markets away from good actors.

[2023-11-27] (x) What are *positive control* and *negative control* of multisigs and why does it matter?

🔹"Positive control" is the ability to enact a change – the multisig can unilaterally pause a smart contract, change its parameters or upgrade it.

🔹"Negative control" is the ability to stop a change – the multisig can veto an action taken by tokenholders.

The two have different risk profiles and justify different regulatory treatment. More risk justifies more regulatory intervention.

There are a lot of additional ways to reduce risk (increasing signatories, delays, transparency, decentralized token ownership, etc.), but none are more significant than switching to a negative model from a positive model.

As a result, the entire sector would be better off if builders, investors and users pushed for multisigs to adopt negative control models.

[2023-11-29] (x) See future threads for results on how possibility/impossibility results fundamentally change as you vary the degree of permissionlessness
[2023-11-29] (x) Tl;dr: (from least to most permissionless)
Perm &lt; QP &lt; DA &lt; FP
[2023-11-29] (x) E.g., Ethereum is effectively aspiring toward the best of both worlds: the basic guarantees of a longest-chain protocol provided the DA assumptions hold, plus the extra benefits of a PBFT-style protocol should the stronger QP assumptions hold
[2023-11-29] (x) PBFT-style PoS protocols can stall in the DA setting due to low participation, but can have lots of nice properties (e.g., finality) in the QP setting. Longest-chain PoS protocols continue to function in the DA setting but sacrifice finality and other properties in exchange
[2023-11-29] (x) In our "quasi-permissionless (QP)" setting, all potential current participants (e.g., all current stakers in a PoS protocol) are assumed to participate. In our "dynamically available (DA)" setting, some but not necessarily all of them participate
[2023-11-29] (x) A middle ground is where a protocol does not have advance knowledge about who will run it but does, in the moment, have some idea about the current participants
[2023-11-29] (x) At the other extreme, which we call the "fully permissionless (FP)" setting, the protocol knows literally nothing about who's running it (e.g., Bitcoin operates under this assumption)
[2023-11-29] (x) At one extreme is the permissioned setting (Perm) where the participants are hard-wired into the protocol (as in e.g. Tendermint Core)
[2023-11-29] (x) We propose a four-level hierarchy (Perm &lt; QP &lt; DA &lt; FP) that parameterizes the amount of knowledge that a protocol has about who's running it, which can be used as a yardstick to measure progress in permissionless consensus
[2023-11-29] (x) The goal is then to figure out the weakest-possible sets of assumptions under which good consensus protocols exist
[2023-11-29] (x) E.g., the synchronous and asynchronous models are opposite ends of a “degree of asynchrony” hierarchy;  crash and Byzantine faults bookend a “fault severity” hierarchy
[2023-11-29] (x) Researchers needs ways to organize and measure progress. What makes a result "better than" the last one? Hierarchies of successively weaker assumptions are a good way to do this
[2023-11-29] (x) Can a proof-of-stake protocol be "truly permissionless"?
What does this question even mean?
A thread on the "hierarchy of permissionless" proposed by @AndrewLewisPye and I: https://t.co/eRbRSMJqdq

[2023-12-01] (x) @shumochu Re: a PoS protocol in the FP setting, the issue is that you need to ensure liveness even when everyone with any stake is inactive. (Whereas the DA and QP settings assume away this scenario.) So you'd need some kind of backup decision-making procedure (e.g., based on external resources) to make it work

[2023-12-02] (x) Charlie Munger was a remarkable figure in the world of investing. His recent passing is not just a loss for the business community but for everyone who learned from his unique insights.

One of my favorite Munger sayings is: “Show me the incentives and I’ll show you the outcome.” What I like about this quote is it is representative of Munger’s characteristic thinking about systems. Systems thinking means it isn’t enough to consider, say, a business or investment decision in isolation. Instead, a decision needs to be considered in the context of larger economic, legal, social, and cultural systems. It also means considering not just the 1st order but also the 2nd (and 3rd) order effects of decisions. Seemingly small changes to initial conditions can have cascading downstream consequences.

To do system thinking well, you need to be consistently learning and cultivate wide-ranging, multidisciplinary interests. As Munger said “Nothing has served me better in my long life than continuous learning. I went through life constantly practicing (because if you don’t practice it, you lose it) the multidisciplinary approach and I can’t tell you what that’s done for me.”

While I didn’t always agree with his tech views, I always learned something reading him. Besides Munger's own writings, I recommend @trengriffin's book and posts on him, including https://t.co/4TUJsvpGcL and https://t.co/qwHKJKFMRR.

[2023-12-04] (x) Here’s a Founder/CEO power move:

Resist the siren song of the many conference & event invites you’re likely receiving and which seemed to hit new highs in 2023.

Instead, emphasize quality over quantity by attending a few high impact events per year and making them count.

Create a game plan prior to each conference:
- Who do you want to meet?
- What are the conversations you hope to have?
- What outcomes do you hope to achieve by the end of the event?

Here are the simple questions I used to ask myself when evaluating whether I should go or stay home:

Access to customers, partners and investors. 
Can I meet a set of customers, partners or investors that I otherwise wouldn’t be able to access?

Save time. 
Can I save time by meeting the folks above all over the course of a few days rather than over weeks or months?

Brain Boost. 
Will I be exposed to a set of people, ideas, or inspiration that might help me unlock new strategies for creating value in my company?

Opportunity cost. 
What are the high value activities I’m giving up by spending my time at the conference?

Punching above our weight. 
Can we host an exclusive dinner, cocktail hour or a gathering in the context of the event that will amplify our product/brand/message above everyone else?

By resisting FOMO and embracing JOMO (Joy of Missing Out), it’s likely that you can have a much bigger impact on your long term success and goals.

With all of this in mind, what are some of the conferences or events that will make your short list in 2024?

[2023-12-06] (x) None of the above should be taken as investment advice; please see https://t.co/0OvRnpU91U for more information
[2023-12-06] (x) AI + blockchains come together

Decentralized blockchains are a counterbalancing force to centralized AI. AI models (like in ChatGPT) can currently only be trained and operated by a handful of tech giants, since the required compute and training data are prohibitive for smaller players. But with crypto, it becomes possible to create multi-sided, global, permissionless markets where anyone can contribute — and be compensated — for contributing compute or a new dataset to the network for someone who needs it. Tapping into this long tail of resources will allow these markets to drive down the costs of AI, making it more accessible.

But as AI revolutionizes the way we produce information — changing society, culture, politics, the economy — it also creates a world of abundant AI-generated content, including deep fakes. Crypto technology can be used here as well to open the black box; track the origin of things we see online; and much more. We also need to figure out ways to decentralize generative AI and govern it democratically, so that no one actor ends up with the power to decide for all others; web3 is the laboratory for figuring out how. Decentralized, open-source crypto networks will democratize (vs. concentrate) AI innovation, ultimately making it safer for consumers.

[2023-12-06] (x) None of the above should be taken as investment advice; please see https://t.co/55lOHruWeJ for more information"
[2023-12-06] (x) We're entering a new era of decentralization.

As we keep seeing over and over again, when control of a powerful system or platform is in the hands of a few (let alone a single leader), it’s too easy to encroach on user freedoms. 

That’s why decentralization matters — It’s the tool that allows us to democratize systems by enabling credibly neutral, composable internet infrastructure; promoting competition and ecosystem diversity; and allowing users more choice, as well as more ownership.

But decentralization has been hard to achieve in practice — at scale — when pitted against the efficiency and stability of centralized systems. Meanwhile, most web3 governance models have involved DAOs (decentralized autonomous organizations) that use simplified yet burdensome models for governance based on direct democracy or corporate governance — which are not designed for the sociopolitical realities of decentralized governance. 

However, thanks to the “living laboratory” of web3 over the past few years, more best practices for decentralization have been emerging — These include:
🔹 models for decentralization that can accommodate applications with richer features; and 
🔹 methods such as DAOs embracing Machiavellian principles to design more effective decentralized governance that holds leadership accountable. 

As these models and methods evolve, we should soon see unprecedented levels of decentralized coordination, operational functionality, and innovations.

Find more of our ideas for 2024 here:
https://t.co/b0ZcPN9tji

[2023-12-06] (x) My take for this year’s @a16z tech trends roundup: 𝐍𝐅𝐓𝐬 𝐚𝐫𝐞 𝐩𝐨𝐢𝐬𝐞𝐝 𝐭𝐨 𝐛𝐞𝐜𝐨𝐦𝐞 𝐮𝐛𝐢𝐪𝐮𝐢𝐭𝐨𝐮𝐬 𝐛𝐫𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐚𝐬𝐬𝐞𝐭𝐬 𝐢𝐧 𝟐𝟎𝟐𝟒.

More and more established brands have been introducing digital assets to mainstream consumers in the form of NFTs. Starbucks, for example, has introduced a gamified loyalty program in which participants collect digital assets as they explore the company’s coffee offerings (not to mention an AR pumpkin spice maze!). Nike and Reddit, meanwhile, have developed digital collectible NFTs that they’ve explicitly marketed to a broad audience.

But brands can do so much more: They can use NFTs to represent and reinforce customer identity and community affiliations; bridge physical goods and their digital representations; and even co-create new products and experiences alongside their most dedicated enthusiasts.

Last year, we saw a growing trend toward inexpensive NFTs for large-scale collection as consumer goods — often managed through custodial wallets and/or “Layer 2” blockchains with correspondingly low transaction costs. Heading into 2024, many of the conditions are in place for NFTs to become ubiquitous as digital brand assets — as @NFTbark and I explain in a forthcoming book (https://t.co/Pqf3wgakcd) — for a wide array of companies and communities.

[2023-12-08] (x) If your argument is that "you need the casino to bootstrap the space", think again anon—you've got it wrong.

Yes, we need mechanisms for funding new decentralized networks early on when the value they'll create is uncertain. But no, we don't need tokens without soul or purpose that moon on nothing but memes and then fall off a cliff.

Yes, we need functioning markets to make the incentive structures that we build into our protocols real. But no, we don't need yet another perps exchange with 100x leverage.

Yes, we need ways of distributing ownership of a network to a community. But no, we don't need gamified schemes that cater to parasitic airdrop farmers just to artificially pump some vanity TVL metric.

The logic that we need the casino is just a thinly veiled attempt to excuse bad behavior in the space. We don't need the casino. We never did. It's the primary force holding the space back.

[2023-12-11] (x) Founders/CEOs,

Get your 2024 plan in place before your team takes time off for the holidays.

One effective strategy to help you get there: Implementing a lightweight approach to annual planning.

Here’s the tried and true approach that worked best for me:

Where do you want the company to be a year from now?
Think about this across each of the key company dimensions: Product, org, quality, brand, key deals and customer wins, etc. Write this down.

What needs to get done in order to make your vision a reality?
- What features and capabilities do you need to ship?
- What key deals do you need to win?
- Who do you need to hire?
- What processes do you need to create or optimize in order to increase company velocity, efficiency and quality?

Share your thinking with the smartest folks on your team.
Don’t worry about their level or role in the company, optimize for intellect and creative problem solving. Ask your team to deconstruct your plan and make it better. I promise you’ll be inspired and amazed by the resulting insights and unlocks.

Assemble the team and encourage vigorous debate.
Discuss, debate and analyze the various points of view until you’ve forged your original thinking into an actionable plan with prioritized projects.

Essential additional step if you’re building in crypto: Engage your ecosystem
One of the most powerful capabilities of blockchain networks is the ability to align and incentivize decentralized teams and individuals towards a common goal. A good planning process includes explicit thinking around how best to engage your community and ecosystem.

Executing well in this area will very likely amplify and accelerate the impact of your plan. Failure to account for the needs of your community can result in finger pointing, infighting, loss of momentum and worse.

Return from the holidays and commence relentless execution of the plan.
Improving execution even 3% month over month results in an incredible compounding level of success over time. At the end of each quarter, reflect on what worked well and identify opportunities for improvement. Adjust the plan based on learning, feedback and in-market data.

Building and leading a team that’s focused, mission-aligned and aggressively executing is the goal and the dream.

If you want to give this a try, hit me back with questions. I’d love to help!

[2023-12-13] (x) Next up: separating the FP and DA (dynamically available) settings! (20/20)
[2023-12-13] (x) Tl;dr 2: Full permissionlessness is at least as hard as asynchrony (19/20)
[2023-12-13] (x) Tl;dr 1: Bitcoin's probabilistic guarantees are an unavoidable consequence of its total lack of assumptions about who might be running it (18/20)
[2023-12-13] (x) At a high level, the proof is a bivalency argument in the spirit of FLP, but the details are tricky (and it took us forever to get them right), for more info see Section 12 of the paper at https://t.co/xzEQbxyYmi (17/20)
[2023-12-13] (x) Thus, our result shows a sense in which (full) permissionlessness is as hard as asynchrony. In effect, a constantly changing player set (and deliberate unbounded message delays by a small number of Byzantine players) can substitute for an asynchronous network (16/20)
[2023-12-13] (x) (Deterministic BA in the synchronous and permissioned setting is easy because one can use timeouts, as in the Dolev-Strong protocol, see https://t.co/xnfGAwVzw2) (15/20)
[2023-12-13] (x) But the new impossibility result quoted above holds in the *synchronous* setting, with bounded message delays. This result is driven not by asynchrony, but by the player inactivity and unknown players challenges in the FP setting (see https://t.co/BBYxUgOyUq) (14/20)
[2023-12-13] (x) From 30K feet, the ambiguity any protocol must face in the asynchronous setting is: If you haven't heard from someone, is it because they've crashed, or because their messages simply haven't been delivered yet? (13/20)
[2023-12-13] (x) The FLP impossibility result is driven by *asynchrony*, meaning unbounded message delays. It applies even to permissioned protocols (with an a priori known and unchanging set of participants) (12/20)
[2023-12-13] (x) The conclusion of the theorem---the impossibility of deterministic BA, even with a tiny number of benign faults---looks a lot like the FLP theorem, one of the most famous and influential impossibility results in distributed computing (see https://t.co/P38mbHzUKw) (11/20)
[2023-12-13] (x) Here's the (semi-)formal statement of the impossibility result: (10/20) https://t.co/ILQ71EJfN7
[2023-12-13] (x) Necessity of (i) is an easy exercise (and applies even to permissioned protocols). Necessity of (ii) will be addressed in a future thread, in a more general setting. This thread is about the unavoidability of probabilistic safety + liveness in the FP setting  (9/20)
[2023-12-13] (x) Answer: *not at all*.  (i)-(iii) are all fundamental compromises that every (arbitrarily clever) protocol must make in the FP setting (8/20)
[2023-12-13] (x) Key question: to what extent are the drawbacks (i)-(iii) an artifact of Bitcoin's specific design? Maybe we can tweak the protocol somehow to fix one or more of them? (7/20)
[2023-12-13] (x) and (iii) the safety + liveness guarantees are only probabilistic (after enough time, even an attacker with a small amount of hashrate will get lucky enough to pull off a long re-org attack) (6/20)
[2023-12-13] (x) (ii) message delays must be bounded (else, a network partition can lead to a big re-org) [this is what "in synchrony" means] (5/20)
[2023-12-13] (x) The fine print: (i) Byzantine players must control less than half the hashrate (else, they completely control the protocol) (4/20)
[2023-12-13] (x) By "works" I mean it can be used to solve the state machine replication (SMR) and Byzantine Agreement (BA) problems, with guaranteed safety and liveness (for background, see https://t.co/xnfGAwVzw2) (3/20)
[2023-12-13] (x) The Bitcoin protocol is remarkable in that it assumes literally nothing about who is running it at any given moment---in our terminology, the protocol works even in the fully permissionless (FP) setting (2/20)
[2023-12-13] (x) The first main result in the "Permissionless Consensus" paper with @AndrewLewisPye is an FLP-type impossibility result for Bitcoin-style protocols (even in synchrony). What does that mean, exactly? (1/20)

[2023-12-14] (x) restricting attention to identifiers with non-zero initial stake has the effect of bounding the maximum number of identifiers that could be controlled by faulty players, opening the door to a Dolev-Strong-style approach (see https://t.co/xnfGAwVzw2 for background) (7/9)

[2023-12-14] (x) Too often, American colleges and universities are failing in a core part of their mission:

Training students to think rigorously and analytically and engage in civil discourse about complex ideas and issues.

While continuing to dunk on the congressional testimony delivered by the @harvard, @penn, and @MIT presidents is tempting and cathartic, it distracts from the mission drift that has been brewing in academia over the course of the last 30 years.

I was honored to serve as a @pomonacollege trustee for over two decades and credit the education I received there with unlocking numerous personal and professional opportunities for me in subsequent years.

During the majority of my time as a trustee, there were intense, productive debates amongst faculty, staff, students and trustees about how best to deliver on the core mission of the college in the service of future generations of students.

Frustratingly, over the course of the last decade (and accelerating with the 2016 election and COVID), the width of the Overton window in which these conversations could be had shrank dramatically at colleges and universities across the country. The focus of discussions shifted to ever more niche areas of inclusion with an emphasis on using the right language often at the expense of clarity, results and progress.

Historically, our colleges and universities have been a major source of competitive advantage for the United States and the need for rigorous intellectual training is more important than ever as the system of the world becomes increasingly nuanced and complex.

In order for these institutions to remain relevant and and vital in the decades ahead, I believe it’s essential to ask college presidents, trustees, faculty, and staff the following questions:

Teaching students to think critically:
Are institutions doing everything in their power to train students to think critically such that they can hold two seemingly conflicting ideas in their mind at the same time while dispassionately analyzing the merits of each point of view? If not, what changes could be made both in and out of the classroom to help students develop these skills?

The role of the faculty:
Are there members of the faculty who are more interested in being activists outside of the realm of scholarly discourse rather than educators? Is it in keeping with the role of a professor to demand that students follow their views rather than asking students to form their own views and chart their own path? If a faculty member has a track record of using their college or classroom as a platform for advancing and espousing their personal beliefs, this should have an impact on tenure decisions and their future employment.

Upholding the free exchange of ideas:
If one of the core tenets of a high functioning institution is the open exchange of ideas, are the right policies in place to ensure that faculty, staff, or students who seek to stunt this debate through verbal or physical intimidation face the appropriate consequences? If so, how are these policies being pursued and enforced in the current environment?

Creating a safe, inclusive environment for all students:
For the free exchange of ideas to flourish, it is imperative that all students feel safe on campus. Many institutions have worked hard for decades to increase representation and to foster a sense of inclusion for students from diverse backgrounds. However, as we’ve seen since October 7th, there is much work to be done to ensure that these efforts encompass all members of the student body. What programs and systems need to be overhauled or invented to achieve success in this area?

American colleges and universities have a potentially critical role to play in educating and training the next generation of leaders. However, the time is now to ask the hard questions and make the necessary course corrections to ensure they are up to the opportunity and challenge ahead.

[2023-12-15] (x) @rel_zeta_tech First, the estimates for GKR are too high by a factor of about 4. Details below.

(Thaler13 didn't optimize constants well. I pay much more attention to them in this new paper: https://t.co/JUQhP0aT03)

>Now we need to estimate the constant in the dynamic programming for all the eq
 values. 

You can build the dynamic programming tables T_1, ..., T_{log n} for eq with only n mults, not 5n. In the i'th step of the dynamic programming, for each x in {0, 1}^i, set T_i(x, 1) \gets r_i * T_{i-1}(x)  and T_i(x, 0) \gets (1-r_i ) * T_{i-1}(x)   = T_{i-1}(x) - T_i[x].

> Hence requiring in total 8n mults after required  and vals are given.

You can do this with only 2n mults. E.g., after round one, for each x in {0, 1}^{n-1}, T[x] \gets r_1 T[x, 1] + (1-r_1) T[x, 0] =T[x, 0] +  r_1 (T[x, 1] -T[x, 0])

> But assuming even one mult is needed per eval [that's 8n more mults].

I don't understand this reasoning. Given the values in the tables discussed above, the number of field mults needed for all runs of sum-check is at most 2n. I can elaborate on this if that'd be helpful. 

Adding it all up, it's about 5n field mults for GKR. 

Second, in a standalone comparison of GKR grand product to Plonk, I don't consider it fair not to count the MSM of size t*n in the Plonk approach for committing to the quotient polynomial when the constraints have degree t (t=4 in the note). That cost can be amortized effectively in the context of broader protocols, but so can the field ops of the GKR prover. 

(For example, in Jolt as we're building it today, we do invoke GKR to compute a grand product for each subtable used across all big-table decompositions, where each grand product has size equal to number of steps of the computation. But most of the terms in all of the products are 1 so don't lead to any field work for the prover.)

That's a difference of another factor of t^2=16 in the comparison.

Finally, the use of univariate polynomials leads to problems elsewhere in many SNARKs, e.g., this why Halo2's lookup's commitment costs grow like 5*max{num_lookups, tablesize} and Lasso's are num_lookups+tablesize, why Binius can work over fields of characteristic 2 and others can't, etc. 

The relative costs of Lasso-with-GKR-for-grand-products vs. Plonk-like techniques (Halo2's lookup) have been looked at experimentally: https://t.co/XmNHz7p82Y

[2023-12-16] (x) @rel_zeta_tech > "Hence requiring in total 8n mults after required eq and V_{i+1} vals are given." is correct I think:

One thing I can point out in a tweet is that in each round, if the prover sends a univariate polynomial s_i, then s_i(1) = s_{i-1}(r_{i-1}) - s_i(0), so no work is needed to compute s_i(1). Compared to your calculation, that gives a 25 percent reduction in prover work (4 evaluation points per round down to 3). So can I convince you now that 8n should be at most 6n?

2n mults should be achievable in an amortized setting, but it's a little intricate. I will write it up in the next few days.

> I think it's reasonable to neglect costs that are amortized like the quotient, it's similar imo to neglecting opening proof costs cause they are batched (which seems in fact important for Lasso's no large scalar claim?).

I don't think it's analogous because there are polynomial commitment schemes where the opening proofs are much faster than committing. So the amount of amortization needed to make the opening proofs a low-order cost is less. 

I agree that whether/how to count stuff that amortizes is tricky and context-dependent. I do think the quotient polynomial can be a meaningful cost in end-to-end SNARKs (its MSM cost is 16x your grand product estimate, and there are FFTs too). 

At any rate, if we're not counting stuff that amortizes, you shouldn't count the cost of filling in the table of eq evaluations at all. Because by running many grand products in parallel as you suggest at the end of the post (which is what we're doing in Jolt), the same eq table can be used in all of them. 

Regarding logarithmic derivatives, Lasso may not be faster than Shahar and Ulrich's work (which is inspired by Lasso and uses the techniques I'm advocating for). Lasso is faster than all the logarithmic-derivative-based lookups that came earlier.

However, logarithmic derivates inherently yield *unindexed* lookups, and applications like Jolt *need* indexed lookups. There are transformations from unindexed lookups to indexed ones, but they destroy key performance improvements of Lasso, by replacing small field elements with random ones.
[2023-12-16] (x) Capitalism — private property freely tradable

[2023-12-16] (x) Modularity Paradox 👇

It unlocks innovation AND introduces constraints.

How does it unlock innovation? It makes it possible for a whole ecosystem of players to emerge, each experimenting with their own approach and specializing on what they do best. For example, the 110 volt electrical socket is a modular interface that has led to an ecosystem of appliance manufacturers. Because of the socket standard, they can innovate on their products without having to know or care about the details of running an electrical utility company.

How does it introduce constraints? The interfaces between modules are standards that are agreed upon by a large group of people. Once they're adopted and modules begin to depend on them, they become very hard to change. Imagine trying to change the 110v socket standard to a different voltage. Whether they like it or not, appliance manufacturers are constrained to what the socket standard has to offer.

Modularity is a way of decoupling different parts of a system and allowing them to evolve independently from one another. You can think of the interface between any two modules as a kind of "contract" that specifies the "terms" that the modules need to satisfy in order to be able to interoperate.

The trick is knowing where to draw the dividing lines between modules and how to define the constraints. If you draw them in the wrong places, you might accidentally separate components that would strongly benefit from evolving together. If you set the constraints too tightly, then there may not be enough leeway on either side. Or, if you set them too loosely, it will be almost as if the separation didn't happen at all.

A good interface is a constraint that de-constrains.

[2023-12-17] (x) @rel_zeta_tech You are right about the unordered-to-ordered lookup transformation not needing commitments to random field elements. Thanks for correcting my mistake!

Yes, committing to partial products makes sense with multivariate polynomials too. This is exactly what Section 5 of SL20 does (the Quarks paper). Your proposal is analogous to SL20 applied to a degree-4 tree of multiplication gates.

But the comparison between this variant of SL20 and Thaler13 is clear: the Thaler13 prover is faster (the tradeoff is that Thaler13 proofs are longer).

The extra prover work in this variant of SL20 is coming from two places: (a) the commitments to partial products, for which you calculated the cost (b) the field work in sum-check for SL20 is about the same as Thaler13 (probably a little higher).

Your calculations reflect the cost of (a), but don't have any field work cost at all, i.e., no analog of (b). The analog of (b) in the univariate setting is work to compute the quotient polynomial. I think the univariate-based grand product argument you're examining should have a field-work cost here, even in an amortized setting? 

That is, while the commitment to the quotient polynomial can be amortized, there should be some field work required to compute the quotient polynomial that grows with the number of constraints?
[2023-12-17] (x) If you're researching or experimenting with methods to combat deepfakes, and deceptive content I'd love to hear from you.
[2023-12-17] (x) It may still feel distant, but we aren't far from the internet devolving into a place where *most* content has been generated (or can be  generated on demand).

Every robust method to prove the origin or history of content involves cryptography and blockchains.

[2023-12-18] (x) Last week, @bhorowitz posted about how @a16z will become more active in supporting pro-technology candidates across the country.

Aligned with that strategy, today we announced our contribution to the Fairshake PAC, which will work to elect leaders who champion thoughtful crypto regulation that balances innovation with consumer protection.

There is a battle in Washington about the future of blockchain technologies: certain policymakers believe it should be banned, while other people think it should have no guardrails. Neither of those options will allow the technology to reach its full potential and realign the future of the Internet away from Big Tech to the people who use it.

As I’ve spent more time with lawmakers, it has become more apparent that the only way to counteract the lobbies of the big banks and big tech is to show that crypto and blockchain can be a force, too.

There are two parts to this, coalition building and raising funds to support the cause. That is what this PAC is all about – bringing together responsible actors in web3 and crypto to help advance clear rules of the road that will support American innovation while holding bad actors to account.

While we won’t control how Fairshake spends our donation, we are encouraged by their vision. And we’ll continue working to make sure our allies in the crypto world make their voices heard as well.

I look forward to continuing my trips to DC to meet with policymakers and represent the hard-working and responsible web3 entrepreneurs in our portfolio so that we can build a better internet together.

[2023-12-19] (x) @CryptoLawRev @Orlando_btc Yes, but the investor has leverage. It’s a lot harder to give something up and try to take it back than it is to have never of given it up in the first place.
[2023-12-19] (x) @_Jonahw 1:1 can still be dilutable. If you fix the token rights (I.e., investor X gets 5% of tokens), then your token supply quickly diminishes upon each subsequent equity investment round. 

1:1 is also sub-optimal as that means 100% of your tokens goes to your cap table, leaving the ecosystem without a treasury.
[2023-12-19] (x) @Orlando_btc Agreed. That’s also somewhat by design. If you make fundraising more difficult, you shorten the runway of the company and force token issuances to happen earlier, often before the company is ready. 

It’s fucked.
[2023-12-19] (x) @insider0x Tie token allocation rights to equity ownership at the point of token launch.
[2023-12-19] (x) @brokendown_node Post initial fundraising, equity and token ownership is dilutable like any other investment.

[2023-12-19] (x) If you're a founder in web3, you should never agree to fixed token allocations for investors. 

Market standard is for token allocations to be dilutable, which is critical for maintaining flexibility for future fundraisings.

Fixed token allocations are predatory.

[2023-12-20] (x) @_Jonahw Agree to disagree. I’ve done hundreds of venture financings in this space since 2017 and represented dozens of companies. Locking in token allocations is bad for founders.

[2023-12-20] (x) @_Jonahw Not sure you appreciate how tokens are typically issued to the cap table. Employees and investors usually get the same percentage of tokens based on equity ownership.  

If 50% of tokens go to the cap table, then that means 10% equity ownership translates to 5% token ownership overall.

Further, this only really works if you make the equity and token interests dilutable. Otherwise, next time you sell equity, the investor's equity drops below 5% (because they're diluted), but token rights don't drop below 5%. So it breaks the 1:1 mechanic you've described.
[2023-12-20] (x) @_Jonahw Most rounds are structured as equity rounds with token rights. People typically don't just sell tokens, so what you're proposing doesn't work in the vast majority of circumstances.

[2023-12-21] (x) @sidiropo AFAIK it's still open whether it's possible to get such a result with epsilon = 0 (i.e., where honest behavior is a Nash equilibrium, not just epsilon-Nash)
[2023-12-21] (x) @sidiropo If you mean specifically for PoS longest-chain protocols, then more or less yes (up to a security parameter epsilon &gt; 0), see the game-theoretic analysis in the Ouroboros and Snow White papers
[2023-12-21] (x) Next thread: how to achieve optimistic responsiveness, accountability, and partition-resilience under the stronger participation assumptions of the quasi-permissionless setting (using a PoS PBFT-style approach) (19/20)
[2023-12-21] (x) Tl;dr, a brutal but unavoidable trade-off: if your blockchain protocol is robust to fluctuating participation by honest players (i.e., functions correctly in the DA setting), then it cannot be optimistically responsive, accountable, or robust to network partitions (18/20)
[2023-12-21] (x) The logic of the proof is similar to that of the first impossibility result (about partial synchrony). Honest players can't figure out if the network is fast (messages delays=\delta) and their honest comrades are inactive or if the network is slow (message delays=\Delta) (17/20)
[2023-12-21] (x) Formal statement of third impossibility result (about optimistic responsiveness). Holds even in the synchronous setting and with all players honest (16/20) https://t.co/XPPHd4s0RP
[2023-12-21] (x) The weak assumptions of the DA setting drive the proof: (i) active honest players must confirm transactions even when the other players might plausibly be inactive; (ii) “late-arriving” players cannot disambiguate conflicting sets of allegedly confirmed transactions (15/20)
[2023-12-21] (x) Formal statement of second  impossibility result (about accountability), which follows directly from the excellent work of @jneu_net @ErtemTas @dntse (https://t.co/SK3JYMbHbK). Holds even in the synchronous setting (14/20) https://t.co/8Eqjyeh2Zd
[2023-12-21] (x) The logic of the proof resembles that of the CAP theorem from distributed systems, and rests on the ambiguity between unbounded network delays and unbounded periods of inactivity by honest players (13/20)
[2023-12-21] (x) Formal statement of first impossibility result (about partial synchrony). Holds even if all players are honest (12/20) https://t.co/woTcBTw4dc
[2023-12-21] (x) The fully permissionless (FP) setting in which PoW LC protocols like Bitcoin operate is even more challenging than the DA setting, so these three impossibility results apply directly to Bitcoin-like protocols (fulfilling an earlier promise) https://t.co/ZZ9VVag1u7 (11/20)
[2023-12-21] (x) Property 3: Optimistic responsiveness, which is basically an instance-optimal version of liveness: latency (for transaction confirmation) should scale only with the realized message delays, which may be far smaller than the assumed worst-case bounds on message delays (10/20)
[2023-12-21] (x) Property 2: Accountability. I.e., if there's a consistency violation (like a double-spend), the protocol can automatically identify some validators to blame for it (think Ethereum slashing conditions) (9/20)
[2023-12-21] (x) Property 1: Robustness to network partitions/periods of asynchrony. I.e., consistency always, and liveness whenever there's no partition/under normal network conditions (8/20)
[2023-12-21] (x) The bad news is that *any* protocol that functions correctly in the DA setting (whether a PoS LC protocol or some other arbitrarily crazy protocol) provably cannot provide any of the following three desirable properties: (7/20)
[2023-12-21] (x) The good news about well-designed PoS longest-chain protocols is that they function correctly (i.e., solve state machine replication) even in the DA setting (assuming also that, at all time steps, a majority of the active stake is honest)  (6/20)
[2023-12-21] (x) In the dynamically available (DA) setting, the only assumption about participation is that, at each time step, somebody with a non-zero amount of registered stake is bothering to run the protocol (5/20)
[2023-12-21] (x) "Stronger assumptions about participation" is made precise using our "hierarchy of permissionlessness" https://t.co/op8jnsiY8G (4/20)
[2023-12-21] (x) The focus of this thread is the fundamental limitations of (PoW or PoS) longest-chain protocols; next thread will discuss the extra assumptions on participation that are necessary and sufficient for PBFT-style PoS protocols to overcome all of these limitations (3/20)
[2023-12-21] (x) The short answer is that PBFT-style PoS protocols make stronger assumptions about participation than longest-chain PoS protocols but, if these assumptions hold up, they can offer stronger guarantees like accountability, optimistic responsiveness, and partition-resilience  (2/20)
[2023-12-21] (x) Should proof-of-stake blockchain protocols follow the longest-chain tradition of Bitcoin, the PBFT-style tradition of permissioned protocols, or something else entirely? The trade-offs are pretty fascinating, so let's dive in (cc @AndrewLewisPye) (1/20)

[2023-12-29] (x) @biserdimitrov @BrianQuintenz I think Coinbase does still offer yield on USDC deposits. However, that product can be easily distinguished from Terra/Anchor's product. [2023-12-29] (x) USDC is likely okay, but yield bearing stablecoins should take note. 

Any commodity (including a stablecoin) can be offered in a securities offering. That’s what happened with UST. The stablecoin was just part of the offering that included profits coming from Terra’s ongoing efforts (operating the Anchor protocol and its bullshit 20% yield, which also wasn’t decentralized).

CeFi products offering yield on stablecoins to US retail should be careful, but there’s a key distinction between them and Terra. Terra was both the issuer/promoter of UST and the operator of Anchor, whereas most CeFi companies aren’t issuers of their own stablecoin.  

None of this should be a surprise. The SEC was very clear on this in the “When Howey Met Gary” speech in 2018.
https://t.co/JKydEnW3sg
[2023-12-29] (x) @llamaonthebrink @wintermute_t You can reduce risk here by specifically prohibiting market making on US exchanges. Market making in the US is extremely risky.
[2023-12-29] (x) @panekkkk Thats right. I think that should be clear from the intro paragraph, but fair enough.
[2023-12-29] (x) The SEC's victory over Terraform Labs today shouldn't surprise anyone. Applying the Howey test to public sales of tokens is straightforward.

This is a good reminder of well established and critical rules of the road for token launches:

(1) Don't sell tokens to U.S. persons for fundraising purposes.
(2) Even if you aren't selling tokens, don't ever market/hype them as an investment opportunity.
(3) Don't prioritize/emphasize secondary market liquidity or listings.
(4) Decentralization matters.
(5) Communication matters.

For more, see: https://t.co/iWYiJYYFK9

𝑇ℎ𝑖𝑠 𝑐𝑟𝑦𝑝𝑡𝑖𝑐 𝑐𝑜𝑚𝑚𝑢𝑛𝑖𝑞𝑢𝑒 𝐼 𝑗𝑢𝑠𝑡 𝑖𝑛𝑡𝑒𝑟𝑐𝑒𝑝𝑡𝑒𝑑 𝑠𝑢𝑔𝑔𝑒𝑠𝑡𝑠 𝑡ℎ𝑎𝑡 𝑡ℎ𝑒𝑦 𝑎𝑟𝑒 𝑝𝑙𝑎𝑛𝑛𝑖𝑛𝑔 𝑡𝑜 𝑚𝑜𝑣𝑒 𝑚𝑜𝑟𝑒 𝑖𝑛𝑡𝑜 𝑡ℎ𝑒 𝑝𝑢𝑏𝑙𝑖𝑐 𝑒𝑦𝑒 𝑖𝑛 𝑡ℎ𝑒 𝑐𝑜𝑚𝑖𝑛𝑔 𝑦𝑒𝑎𝑟. 𝐵𝑢𝑡 𝐼'𝑚 𝑠𝑡𝑖𝑙𝑙 𝑛𝑜𝑡 𝑒𝑣𝑒𝑛 𝑐𝑜𝑚𝑝𝑙𝑒𝑡𝑒𝑙𝑦 𝑐𝑒𝑟𝑡𝑎𝑖𝑛 *𝑤ℎ𝑜 𝑡ℎ𝑒𝑦 𝑎𝑟𝑒.* 

𝑆𝑜𝑟𝑟𝑦 𝑡𝑜 𝑏𝑒 𝑑𝑟𝑜𝑝𝑝𝑖𝑛𝑔 𝑡ℎ𝑖𝑠 𝑜𝑛 𝑦𝑜𝑢𝑟 ℎ𝑒𝑎𝑑 𝑟𝑖𝑔ℎ𝑡 𝑎𝑡 𝑁𝑒𝑤 𝑌𝑒𝑎𝑟'𝑠, 𝑏𝑢𝑡 𝑖𝑡 𝑠𝑒𝑒𝑚𝑠 𝑐𝑟𝑖𝑡𝑖𝑐𝑎𝑙 𝑡𝑜 𝑓𝑖𝑔𝑢𝑟𝑒 𝑜𝑢𝑡 𝑎𝑠𝑎𝑝. 𝐶𝑎𝑛 𝑦𝑜𝑢 𝑚𝑎𝑘𝑒 𝑎𝑛𝑦 𝑠𝑒𝑛𝑠𝑒 𝑜𝑓 𝑖𝑡?

---

𝗜𝗻 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗺𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗵𝘀, 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗛𝗘𝗕𝗥𝗘𝗪 𝗜𝗡𝗙𝗜𝗡𝗜𝗧𝗬 𝗦𝗬𝗠𝗕𝗢𝗟 𝘄𝗶𝗹𝗹 𝗿𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗮𝗹 𝗶𝘁𝘀 𝘀𝗲𝗰𝗿𝗲𝘁𝘀. 𝗟𝗼𝗼𝗸 𝘁𝗼 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗕𝗥𝗜𝗧𝗜𝗦𝗛 𝗪𝗔𝗧𝗘𝗥𝗕𝗜𝗥𝗗 𝗟𝗢𝗢𝗡. 𝗔𝗻𝗱 𝗵𝗲𝗲𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗢𝗙𝗙𝗜𝗖𝗜𝗔𝗟 𝗣𝗥𝗢𝗖𝗟𝗔𝗜𝗠𝗘𝗥'𝗦 𝗠𝗘𝗦𝗦𝗔𝗚𝗘. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗽𝗮𝘁𝘁𝗲𝗿𝗻 𝗶𝘀 𝘄𝗿𝗶𝘁 𝗶𝗻 𝗛𝗢𝗠𝗘𝗥'𝗦 𝗦𝗜𝗘𝗚𝗘 𝗘𝗣𝗜𝗖. 𝗟𝗢𝗥𝗗, 𝗛𝗔𝗩𝗘 𝗠𝗘𝗥𝗖𝗬 𝗼𝗻 𝗮𝗹𝗹 𝘄𝗵𝗼 𝗱𝗼 𝗻𝗼𝘁 𝘀𝗲𝗲. 𝗧𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲 𝘄𝗶𝗹𝗹 𝗯𝗲 𝗮 𝗖𝗢𝗡𝗙𝗨𝗦𝗘𝗗 𝗕𝗔𝗧𝗧𝗟𝗘. 𝗪𝗲 𝘄𝗶𝗹𝗹 𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗸𝗲 𝗮 𝗕𝗨𝗡𝗗𝗟𝗘 𝗢𝗙 𝗔𝗫𝗢𝗡𝗦. 𝗪𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝗦𝗜𝗚𝗡𝗜𝗙𝗜𝗖𝗔𝗡𝗧 𝗘𝗡𝗘𝗥𝗚𝗬. 𝗧𝗵𝗼𝘀𝗲 𝘄𝗵𝗼 𝗙𝗢𝗥𝗖𝗘 𝗖𝗥𝗘𝗔𝗦𝗘𝗦 𝗜𝗡𝗧𝗢 𝗚𝗔𝗥𝗠𝗘𝗡𝗧𝗦 𝘄𝗶𝗹𝗹 𝗯𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗳𝗶𝗿𝘀𝘁 𝘁𝗼 𝘀𝗲𝗲. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗺𝗼𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝘀𝗵𝗮𝗹𝗹 𝗯𝗲 𝗮𝗸𝗶𝗻 𝘁𝗼 𝗮 𝗖𝗢𝗟𝗟𝗔𝗕𝗢𝗥𝗔𝗧𝗜𝗩𝗘 𝗣𝗢𝗘𝗠. 𝗢𝗿 𝗽𝗲𝗿𝗵𝗮𝗽𝘀 𝗮 𝗦𝗔𝗨𝗖𝗬 𝗟𝗔𝗧𝗜𝗡 𝗗𝗔𝗡𝗖𝗘. 𝗪𝗲 𝘄𝗶𝗹𝗹 𝗮𝗹𝗹 𝗳𝗲𝗲𝗱 𝗼𝗻 𝗧𝗥𝗜𝗧𝗜𝗖𝗨𝗠. 𝗔𝗻𝗱 𝘄𝗲 𝘄𝗶𝗹𝗹 𝗔𝗡𝗦𝗪𝗘𝗥 𝗦𝗪𝗜𝗙𝗧𝗟𝗬 𝗧𝗢 𝗧𝗛𝗘 𝗛𝗘𝗟𝗠.

𝗜𝘁 𝗵𝗮𝘀 𝗯𝗲𝗲𝗻 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗽𝗵𝗲𝘀𝗶𝗲𝗱 𝗶𝗻 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘆 𝗳𝗮𝗯𝗿𝗶𝗰 𝗼𝗳 𝗺𝗮𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗺𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗰𝘀. 𝟮𝟬𝟮𝟰 𝘄𝗶𝗹𝗹 𝗯𝗲 𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝘆𝗲𝗮𝗿. 𝗪𝗲 𝘄𝗶𝗹𝗹 𝗲𝗻𝗱 𝘄𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗪𝗢𝗞𝗘𝗡 𝗣𝗬𝗥𝗔𝗠𝗜𝗗𝗦 𝗵𝗮𝘃𝗲 𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗿𝘁𝗲𝗱.

[2023-12-30] (x) @CryptoKaibaCorp @NFTbark @pudgypenguins @a16zcrypto Absolutely. Indeed, one of @NFTbark's and my core theses is that brand building around NFTs benefits from having many different touch-points and repeat interactions around the same digital asset, and so I think we're going to see more assets where the experience is digi-physi, or at least intentionally cross-platform.
[2023-12-30] (x) @jesseXjesse @NFTbark @pudgypenguins @a16zcrypto This is a great question! There are several startups working at producing variations on the theme of NFTs as lower-friction and more transparent ownership records for carbon credits. In addition to potentially expanding the market of prospective purchasers, some of these companies explicitly aim to give holders reason to interact with the NFTs in a way that reinforces their personal/public identities as environmental stewards -- in effect forming a digital brand around the conservation asset. This has been done for wildlife conservation as well, and I imagine the model would extend to other ecosystem services....

[2023-12-30] (x) 25 Days to 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐄𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐲𝐭𝐡𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐓𝐨𝐤𝐞𝐧

As we get closer, @NFTbark and I are going to be sharing some of the insights from the book, and also giving out some copies 📙👀

Today, I'll be doing both! Read on 👇

---

To start with, I'm sharing what led me to become intrigued about NFTs in the first place: they're a tool for 𝐦𝐚𝐤𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐦𝐚𝐫𝐤𝐞𝐭𝐬.

NFTs provide a way of creating and verifying ownership over digital assets. This makes it possible to establish property rights over digital goods such as media files or game items in a way that was difficult or impossible to do previously. Moreover, NFTs on public blockchains achieve this in a way that's independent of any specific platform.

If you own an NFT associated to a digital trading card, for example, you can trade or gift that card to someone else without the creator of the asset having to be involved -- just like with a physical trading card. That's very different from the way digital assets have generally worked in the past (for example, if you want to give someone a Kindle e-book, you have to go through Amazon, which places all sorts of constraints on the process).

Defining property rights and various forms of exchange are fundamental to the creation of markets. By doing both, NFTs have enabled new types of markets to emerge.

I found this intrinsically confusing at the outset. I was wondering 𝘸𝘩𝘺 𝘢𝘳𝘦 𝘱𝘦𝘰𝘱𝘭𝘦 𝘱𝘢𝘺𝘪𝘯𝘨 𝘮𝘰𝘯𝘦𝘺 𝘧𝘰𝘳 𝘢 𝘥𝘪𝘨𝘪𝘵𝘢𝘭 𝘳𝘦𝘤𝘰𝘳𝘥 𝘢𝘴𝘴𝘰𝘤𝘪𝘢𝘵𝘦𝘥 𝘵𝘰 𝘢𝘯 𝘪𝘮𝘢𝘨𝘦? And then I realized that buying digital art was a type of transaction people had already wanted to engage in, and NFTs were making possible.

And to a market designer like me, that's fascinating, QED 😀

---

[2023-12-30] (x) @SHL0MS Dude this is too difficult and I have no interest in trying can I just have something for free please?
[2023-12-30] (x) "An obvious ecological parallel can be drawn between bats and humans – just as bats live in dense colonies, so now do modern humans. And we may now be at a critical point in the history of our species, in which our ever-increasing ecological footprint has brought us in close contact with bats in a way that was much rarer in the past. Our population is connected in ways that were previously unimaginable. A novel virus can make the zoonotic jump somewhere in Southeast Asia and a carrier of it can then be on the other side of the globe a mere 24-hours later, having encountered thousands of people in airports and other mass transit systems. As a result, bat pathogens are now being transferred from bat populations to the human population in what might prove to be the second major zoonotic spillover event after the one associated with domestication of livestock and pets a few thousand years ago.

Unfortunately for us, our physiology is not suited to tolerate these new viruses. Bats have adapted to live with them over many millions of years. Humans have not undergone the same kind of adaptation and cannot do so on any timescale that will be of use to those living now, nor to our immediate descendants.

Simply put, humans are not bats, and the continuous existence and improvement of what we now call
'civilization' depends on the same basic public health and infectious disease control that saw life expectancy in high-income countries more than double to 85 years."

Worth reading in its entirety: "'Endemic' SARS-CoV-2 and the death of public health" by @JohnSnowProject 👇

[2023-12-31] (x) There are 2024 𝗶𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗴𝗲𝗿-𝘀𝗶𝗱𝗲𝗱 𝗾𝘂𝗮𝗱𝗿𝗶𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗿𝗮𝗹𝘀 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝗽𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗺𝗲𝘁𝗲𝗿 𝟰𝟳 (up to rotation), and 2024 𝗶𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗴𝗲𝗿-𝘀𝗶𝗱𝗲𝗱 𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗮𝗻𝗴𝗹𝗲𝘀 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝗰𝗶𝗿𝗰𝘂𝗺𝗰𝗶𝗿𝗰𝗹𝗲 𝗱𝗶𝗮𝗺𝗲𝘁𝗲𝗿 𝗮𝘁 𝗺𝗼𝘀𝘁 𝟯𝟬. And there are 44 integer-sided triangles with perimeter 46, for a total perimeter sum of – you guessed it – 2024.
[2023-12-31] (x) Plus 2024 (= 44*46) is the first year since 888 that both has 𝗮𝗹𝗹 𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗻 𝗱𝗶𝗴𝗶𝘁𝘀 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗰𝗮𝗻 𝗯𝗲 𝗳𝗮𝗰𝘁𝗼𝗿𝗲𝗱 𝗶𝗻𝘁𝗼 𝘁𝘄𝗼 𝗻𝘂𝗺𝗯𝗲𝗿𝘀 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝗮𝗹𝗹 𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗻 𝗱𝗶𝗴𝗶𝘁𝘀.
[2023-12-31] (x) 2024 is the 𝘀𝘂𝗺 𝗼𝗳 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗳𝗶𝗿𝘀𝘁 𝟭𝟭 𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗻 𝘀𝗾𝘂𝗮𝗿𝗲𝘀. That's also the 𝗻𝘂𝗺𝗯𝗲𝗿 𝗼𝗳 𝗽𝗼𝘀𝘀𝗶𝗯𝗹𝗲 𝗯𝗶𝘀𝗵𝗼𝗽 𝗺𝗼𝘃𝗲𝘀 𝗼𝗻 𝗮 𝟭𝟮𝘅𝟭𝟮 𝗰𝗵𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗯𝗼𝗮𝗿𝗱.

And speaking of chess: 2024 is one of the 𝘀𝗾𝘂𝗮𝗿𝗲𝘀 𝘃𝗶𝘀𝗶𝘁𝗲𝗱 𝗯𝘆 𝗮 𝗰𝗵𝗲𝘀𝘀 𝗸𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗺𝗼𝘃𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗼𝗻 𝗮 𝘀𝗾𝘂𝗮𝗿𝗲-𝘀𝗽𝗶𝗿𝗮𝗹-𝗻𝘂𝗺𝗯𝗲𝗿𝗲𝗱 𝗯𝗼𝗮𝗿𝗱 with the objective of always moving to the adjacent unvisited square containing the number with the most divisors. (Believe it or not, there are only finitely many squares visited in this way – after 1113 steps, the king hits square 855481, and then stops!)
[2023-12-31] (x) There are precisely 2024 𝘄𝗮𝘆𝘀 𝗼𝗳 𝗰𝗵𝗼𝗼𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗵𝗿𝗲𝗲 𝗼𝗯𝗷𝗲𝗰𝘁𝘀 𝗼𝘂𝘁 𝗼𝗳 𝗮 𝘀𝗲𝘁 𝗼𝗳 𝟮𝟰. (This one you might've seen before!)

And there are 2024 𝘀𝗼𝗹𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀 𝘁𝗼 𝘅^𝟮 + 𝘆^𝟮 + 𝘇^𝟮 = 𝟭 𝗺𝗼𝗱𝘂𝗹𝗼 𝟰𝟲.
[2023-12-31] (x) 2024 is a 𝘁𝗲𝘁𝗿𝗮𝗵𝗲𝗱𝗿𝗮𝗹 𝗻𝘂𝗺𝗯𝗲𝗿, obtained by counting the number of balls in successively larger tetrahedral stacks. The most recent such year was 1771, and the next is 2300, followed by 2600!

2024 is also 𝗱𝗼𝗱𝗲𝗰𝗮𝗵𝗲𝗱𝗿𝗮𝗹; the next such is 2925, and then 4060.
[2023-12-31] (x) Okay so 𝟮𝟬𝟮𝟰 𝗵𝗮𝘀 𝗮 *𝗹𝗼𝘁* 𝗼𝗳 𝗶𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗿𝗲𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗻𝘂𝗺𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗰𝗮𝗹 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗿𝘁𝗶𝗲𝘀, 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗜 𝗷𝘂𝘀𝘁 𝘀𝗽𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗶𝗿𝗲 𝗱𝗮𝘆 𝗰𝗮𝘁𝗮𝗹𝗼𝗴𝘂𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗺. 

Here are a few: 👇🧵

[2024-01-01] (x) 𝗧𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗡𝗲𝘄 𝗬𝗲𝗮𝗿'𝘀 𝗗𝗮𝘆 𝗶𝘀 𝗹𝗶𝗸𝗲 𝗻𝗼𝗻𝗲 𝗼𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗿 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗰𝗲𝗻𝘁𝘂𝗿𝘆.

Why's that? 

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗱𝗮𝘁𝗲 – 𝟭/𝟭/𝟮𝟰 – 𝗹𝗶𝗻𝗲𝘀 𝘂𝗽 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗳𝗶𝗿𝘀𝘁 𝗳𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗻𝗼𝗻𝘇𝗲𝗿𝗼 𝗲𝗹𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁𝘀 𝗼𝗳 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗧𝗿𝗶𝗯𝗼𝗻𝗮𝗰𝗰𝗶 𝗦𝗲𝗾𝘂𝗲𝗻𝗰𝗲.

(As the name suggests, that's like the Fibonacci numbers, except that you add up every *three* numbers in the sequence to get the next one. So 0, 0, 1, 1, 2, 4, ....)

𝗠𝗮𝗸𝗲 𝗮 𝘄𝗶𝘀𝗵 𝗮𝘁 𝟳:𝟭𝟯:𝟮𝟰!

QED.
[2024-01-01] (x) And that's not all. 

Believe it or not, 𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲'𝘀 𝘀𝗼𝗺𝗲𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗲𝗹𝘀𝗲 𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗹𝗹𝘆 𝗰𝗼𝗼𝗹 𝗮𝗯𝗼𝘂𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗡𝗲𝘄 𝗬𝗲𝗮𝗿, 𝗯𝘂𝘁 𝗜'𝗹𝗹 𝗿𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗮𝗹 𝗶𝘁 𝘁𝗼𝗺𝗼𝗿𝗿𝗼𝘄.

[2024-01-01] (x) Moreover, 𝟮𝟬𝟮𝟯+𝟮𝟬𝟮𝟰 = 𝟰𝟬𝟰𝟳 is hexagonal pyramidal (also called a "greengrocer's number"). And 3^4047 + 2^(4047-1) is prime. 4047 is also a Lucas 9-step number, and 4047^2 = 16378209 contains exactly 8 distinct digits. There are 4047 peaks at odd level in all nondecreasing Dyck paths of semi-length 9. And continuing the dodecahedron theme, 4047 is also the rounded total surface area of a regular dodecahedron with edge length 14.

Also 𝟴*𝟰𝟬𝟰𝟳^𝟮 - 𝟮*𝟰𝟬𝟰𝟳 - 𝟭 𝗱𝗶𝘃𝗶𝗱𝗲𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝟰𝟬𝟳𝟵-𝘁𝗵 𝗙𝗶𝗯𝗼𝗻𝗮𝗰𝗰𝗶 𝗻𝘂𝗺𝗯𝗲𝗿 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝘀𝗼𝗺𝗲 𝗿𝗲𝗮𝘀𝗼𝗻.

Oh and 𝟰𝟬𝟰𝟳 𝗵𝗮𝘀 𝗮𝗻 "𝗮" 𝗶𝗻 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗺𝗶𝗱𝗱𝗹𝗲 𝗼𝗳 𝗶𝘁𝘀 𝗻𝗮𝗺𝗲 𝗶𝗻 𝗦𝗽𝗮𝗻𝗶𝘀𝗵 (cuatromilcuArentaysiete). Only three numbers in the 4000 range have this property, and they're all clustered close together (4045, 4047, and 4049).
[2024-01-01] (x) In case you're feeling nostalgic for the year we're leaving behind, don't worry: 

The average of the primes dividing 2023 and 2024 is the same. (This happens again in 5063 and then in 11111.) 

And 2023 plus the sum of its prime factors is equal to 2024 plus the sum of its prime factors (coming up again in 3478/3479!).
[2024-01-01] (x) Plus there are 2024 46-digit left- or right-truncatable primes with no consecutive zero digits. 

There exists only one such 60-digit prime, namely 202075909708030901050930450609080660821035604908735717137397. https://t.co/WvXucA38TB
[2024-01-01] (x) 𝙍𝙚𝙖𝙙𝙮 𝙛𝙤𝙧 𝙩𝙝𝙞𝙨 𝙤𝙣𝙚?

The number of integers less than 2024 that are relatively prime to 2024 is equal to the number of integers less than 2024 plus the number of integers less than 2024 that are relatively prime to 2024 that are relatively prime to 2024 plus the number of integers less than 2024 that are relatively prime to 2024. 

(This can be expressed more concisely [and slightly less syntactically ambiguously] as phi(2024) = phi(2024+phi(2024)), where phi denotes the Euler totient function 😉.)
[2024-01-01] (x) We can also express 2024 as the sum of a cube and the largest square greater than that cube:

2024 = 1000 + 1024 = 10^3 + 32^2 https://t.co/1M4ZhZjxLE
[2024-01-01] (x) Totally coincidentally, 2024 is the first year since 1927 whose largest digit raised to the power of its smallest digit adds to itself to give a square (4^0 + 2024 = 2025 = 45^2).
[2024-01-01] (x) There are 2024 compositions (i.e., ordered partitions) of 24 into squares. 

Also 2024 compositions of 15 such that no two adjacent parts are equal. 

And 2024 compositions of 38 into primes that can be written as a sum of two squares. https://t.co/GOZiq9SMjO
[2024-01-01] (x) There are 2024 rhombi on a 35x5 grid, as well as 2024 three-step self-avoiding walks on a 14x14 square. https://t.co/NrNjWTj2ZB
[2024-01-01] (x) 2024 is a squareful number with squareful neighbors.

It's the first element of a reduced infinitary amicable pair. (The smaller element of the next such pair is 62744.)

It's also the smallest base b such that there exist exactly 8 Wieferich pseudoprimes less than b. https://t.co/9RsjKCUCXd
[2024-01-01] (x) You heard that right: we have 𝗺𝗼𝗿𝗲 𝗶𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗿𝗲𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗳𝗮𝗰𝘁𝘀 𝗮𝗯𝗼𝘂𝘁 𝟮𝟬𝟮𝟰!! 👇🧵 

𝑻𝒉𝒊𝒔 𝒊𝒔 𝒕𝒉𝒆 𝒍𝒊𝒈𝒉𝒕𝒏𝒊𝒏𝒈 𝒓𝒐𝒖𝒏𝒅 ⚡️

There are 2024 distinct products a*b*c with 1 <= a <= b <= c <= 31.

And there are 2024 integral solutions to the equation a^3 + ... + k^3 = (a + ... + k)^2 with 1 <= a <= ... <= k.

And there are 2024 binary strings of length 20 that contain the 1s' complement of each of their two halves.

[2024-01-01] (x) 2024^2 = 4096576 is the 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝗰𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗻𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗼𝗳 𝘁𝘄𝗼 𝗻𝗼𝗻𝘇𝗲𝗿𝗼 𝘀𝗾𝘂𝗮𝗿𝗲𝘀 (4096 = 64^2 and 576 = 24^2). This hasn't happened since 1900, but it will happen again in 2050.

[2024-01-03] (x) Thank you, Kayvon. 

I’m hoping the book can be useful to founders like Kayvon who can share it with employees, partners, and friends to help explain why they are excited about the space.
[2024-01-03] (x) I started working on Read Write Own in October 2022, and I’m excited that it’s finally coming out in a few weeks, on January 30.

I wrote Read Write Own because I felt we needed a book that explains the full potential of blockchains and tokens, and how these technologies can support productive and not just speculative use cases. Sometimes people in the industry say things like “speculation is a bootstrapping mechanism that leads to utility.” That explanation might be sufficient for true believers, but it’s not for the rest of the world.

What I try to do is break down, in detail – based on frameworks, experience, and research – why a future internet built on these technologies would be better, and how exactly we might get there.

I like tech/science/business books that are authentic, that feel like I am overhearing conversations between colleagues, but are written in a way that non-specialists can understand. That’s what I tried to do. I wrote it as you’d hear me talk in daily conversations, except that I explain things from first principles, without assuming prior knowledge. (Early on, someone recommended that I write the book for “smart high school students,” advice that stuck with me.)

The book is simple and straightforward but in no way watered down. It is also concise: the main text is 230 pages despite covering a lot of ground (there are also 36 pages of endnotes — much of the material came from my own experience, but I also conducted new research and incorporated feedback from about twenty expert readers). I cut out around 200 pages that didn’t feel essential. I also structured the book in roughly 3-4 page chunks that can stand alone, like blog posts, so that readers can skip around. Internet veterans will be familiar with the topics covered in some sections. Crypto veterans will be familiar with others. General readers should read end to end to get the full narrative. I hope everyone will find sections that are new and interesting to them.

I sent out advance copies a few months ago and was excited by the positive feedback. I’ll be sharing more in the coming weeks, but for now here are some early blurbs that will appear on the back of the book:

“This book is a must for anyone who wants to better understand the real potential of blockchains and web3 to drive even greater innovation, inspire more imaginative thinking, and ultimately unlock new growth opportunities in the future.” —Bob Iger, CEO, Disney

“Read this book to understand a compelling vision of where the internet should go and how to get there.” —Sam Altman, CEO, OpenAI

“Unfortunately, the noise has overwhelmed the signal in the crypto industry over the last five years. Chris Dixon succeeds at explaining how crypto technology is vital to our technological future and how we all can benefit, much as we have from the internet. I would encourage all regulators and crypto naysayers around the world to read this book.” —Mark Cuban

“This book changed my mind. It’s a clear manifesto for redeeming the internet and making it the democratic platform we all want. The hero in this story is a second generation of blockchains, tamed from their earlier excesses as speculative coins and remade into a way for everyone to own what they make online. It’s the best positive case for crypto I’ve seen.” —Kevin Kelly, bestselling author of The Inevitable

“This is a pivotal moment for the internet. Chris Dixon’s fascinating book offers a refreshing and radical new take at a time when we need fresh thinking more than ever.” —Mustafa Suleyman, co-founder of Inflection AI and DeepMind, bestselling author of The Coming Wave

“Chris Dixon has always been someone who sees the future with clarity and practicality. He does this once again in Read Write Own.” —John Donahoe, CEO, Nike

Thank you to those who helped me write the book — and to everyone who plans to take the time to read it. I’m looking forward to your feedback and discussing it with you.

@NFTbark and I argue that NFTs have the potential to fundamentally transform our relationship with companies, platforms, and each other online by giving us ownership over our own digital assets...

... but to achieve that potential, NFTs have to become broadly accessible and embedded in everyday interactions, just as web pages, mobile apps, and other basic internet services are today.

This means that while NFTs' vanguard has been in many ways radical, in the future we'll see NFTs become much more a part of ordinary business and brand interactions.

[2024-01-09] (x) Stop scrolling for a moment – and like, RT, and drop your favorite pfp(s) in the comments for a chance to win a copy of 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐄𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐲𝐭𝐡𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐓𝐨𝐤𝐞𝐧 📙!

Then read on to learn why @NFTbark's and my NFT business framework helps explain the success of the pfp model. 👇👀

NFT-based brand building starts with 𝗼𝘄𝗻𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗽 of digital assets as a way to give people a stake in the brand, and supplements that ownership with 𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆 as a way of reinforcing the sense of value people get from affiliating with the brand through the asset.

A big part of the payoff of that, from the business's perspective, is that it drives holders to integrate the brand into their personal 𝗶𝗱𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗶𝘁𝘆, especially as manifested in digital space.

In the best-case scenario, individual identity extends into 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗺𝘂𝗻𝗶𝘁𝘆 identification – people who own a given NFT come to think of themselves as similar to and connected with other holders of that NFT, and potentially even work with them to drive the brand forward.

Fashion is a core part of the way people express their identities. And pfps are a form of virtual fashion particularly optimized to speedrun the identity- and community-formation steps. They give the owner a way to imagine themselves as a character reflecting their own personal aesthetics and aspirations (hacker extraordinaire? chill panda? pinball wizard?). 

Moreover, because pfp images themselves are optimized for use as social media profile pictures, they make it easy to find other people with similar affinities. (If you're the type of person who self-identifies with the the "cool @supducks aesthetic," how could you not want to be friends with other people who are drawn to the same – or at least follow them on the [former] bird app?)

And when it comes to outsiders considering joining in NFT community for the first time, the pfp provides a simple proxy that people can use to intuit which communities are likely to share their interests. (For example, @forgottenrunes -> wizard enthusiasts/classic gamers; @Azuki -> anime/streetwear; @1337skulls -> old-school Internet remix culture.)

The pfp is only the start of the story, of course – but when done right, it can provide a powerful foundation to build a brand on top of, QED.

[2024-01-11] (x) I’m attaching the table of contents of Read Write Own below. I always like to scan through the table of contents of nonfiction books to see what, if anything, interests me, so I thought people considering the book might want to do the same. The best way to read it is end-to-end, but it’s also broken into 3-4 page chunks to allow you to skip around.

A few comments:

I present a series of frameworks throughout, some of which readers might have seen me discuss before. In the book, I develop these frameworks in greater detail, and illustrate them with stories and examples. Most of the frameworks apply not just to blockchains but to software and internet technologies generally, including both classic internet services and emerging areas like AI.

Some of the frameworks in the book:

→ The platform-app feedback loop
→ Networks: the attract-extract cycle
→ Skeuomorphic vs native applications
→ Squeezing the balloon
→ Come for the tool, stay for the network
→ The next big thing starts out looking like a toy
→ Inside-out vs outside-in technologies
→ What the smartest people do on the weekend is what everyone else will do during the week in ten years
→ Internet media: the attention-monetization tradeoff
→ Computing cycles: Incubation vs growth phases

In terms of structure, the book has five main parts:

1/ Briefly covers the history of the internet, focusing on the two most recent eras from the early 1990s through today.

2/ Dives deeper into blockchains, explaining how they work and why they matter. Shows how blockchains and tokens can be used to construct blockchain networks (aka protocols), and explain the technical and economic mechanisms by which they work.

3/ Shows how blockchain networks empower users and other network participants, answering the “why blockchains?” question people often ask.

4/ Addresses controversial questions head-on, including policy and regulatory topics and the harmful casino culture that has developed around blockchains that hurts their public perception and undermines their potential.

5/ Presents a vision of promising blockchains applications in areas like social networks, video games, virtual worlds, media businesses, collaborative creation, finance, and artificial intelligence.

Whether you’re a tech veteran, a startup founder, an everyday internet user, or just someone who’s curious about the systems that govern our digital lives, I hope there’s something in the book for you.

[2024-01-11] (x) @jsheps @NFTbark @pudgypenguins @a16zcrypto 💯💯💯 @NFTbark and I try to tackle the "isn't it all a scam" question head-on, starting on page 8 – we make the point that web3 at the moment is a lot like the early Internet. The technology hasn't matured yet, and many of the business models people have been experimenting with are unsustainable at best, and outright fraudulent at worst. But just like with the Internet as it matured, that's changing – and we hope that by providing a blueprint for NFT success, we can do a little bit to help move the business models forward....

[2024-01-15] (x) @srimisra The Fibonacci numbers, which you're likely already familiar with, are a sequence obtained by starting with 1 and 1 and adding every two numbers in the sequence to get the next number. 

So 1 + 1 = 2, 1 + 2 = 3, 2 + 3 = 5, 3 + 5 = 8.

A somewhat surprising fact that comes immediately out of the additive construction of the sequence is that the sequence is periodic modulo m (that means, reducing its elements to the remainders left after dividing by m).

To see this, note that there are only m possible remainders (0, ..., m-1), and so there are only m^2 possible pairs of remainders, which means that some pair must eventually repeat. But because of the way the sequence is constructed, the second you repeat one pair, you get the same sequence of pairs over again -- and in fact, it turns out that any pair of adjacent elements determines the full sequence.

For example, modulo 3 we get:

1 + 1 = 2, 
1 + 2 = 0 (mod 3), 
2 + 0 = 2, 
0 + 2 = 2, 
2 + 2 = 1, 
2 + 1 = 0, 
1 + 0 = 1, 
0 + 1 = 1, 
1 + 1 = 2... and we're back to the start again, meaning that the sequence is periodic with period 8.

The statement I was quoting is a surprising observation about how large the period can possibly be -- apparently it's bounded above by 6m, which in general is a lot less than m^2. And the other thing that's really interesting is that even though the period can sometimes be really small relative to m, it hits the maximum 6m infinitely often. I believe the first time it's achieved with equality is m = 10, which has period 60.

https://t.co/g5NC2tdP8v
[2024-01-15] (x) @srimisra Sure! Or at least I can explain the statement quickly -- I'm not sure the explanation of why it's true has a super concise version.

👇
[2024-01-15] (x) Fun fact I just learned: the period of the Fibonacci sequence modulo m is bounded above by 6m, with equality arising infinitely often.

(I can't believe I'm procrastinating on Mystery Hunt by reading the American Mathematical Monthly, but here we are 😅)

[2024-01-17] (x) More on SEC v. Coinbase - Here's a thread from a couple years ago on why the underlying legal theories for the SEC's case are difficult to apply to secondary markets like Coinbase.
[2024-01-17] (x) The SEC v. Coinbase faceoff today was the first critical test of the SEC’s enforcement strategy against digital asset exchanges. 

That strategy is more high-risk than the SEC’s historical focus on token issuers, so heres's some important background/context...👇

Before last year, the SEC’s approach had primarily focused on token issuers, with dozens of enforcement actions between 2017 to 2020 and a flurry of investigations launched during 2021 and 2022. But after the collapse of FTX in Nov 2022 and the SEC’s struggles to bring cases against issuers, the SEC appears to have shifted more of its attention to trading platforms, targeting Coinbase, Binance, and Kraken in 2023.

What’s driving the shift? Trading platforms are a critical chokepoint for decelerating blockchain-based innovation. They act as a gateway for new users, there are only a few servicing U.S. customers and they are easily found. Through their enforcement actions the SEC likely hopes to seize control of what tokens U.S. trading platforms can list.

Meanwhile, measuring decentralization of individual issuers/protocols under Howey is a labor-intensive process and there are thousands of them, so there’s no way to scale regulation-by-enforcement sufficiently to stem the tide of issuers. It’s an endless game of whack-a-mole.

Nevertheless, the application of Howey to secondary markets (like Coinbase) is unprecedented and is even more confusing than when it’s applied to token issuers. The SEC’s attempt to do so could be construed so broadly as to apply to any asset sale, including buying Taylor Swift tickets on Stub Hub.

As a result, the Coinbase, Binance and Kraken cases aren’t slam dunks for the SEC and they’re unlikely to produce clear rules of the road or provide significant consumer protection. Instead, inconsistent court rulings and long time horizons will likely only breed more uncertainty – we’ve already seen this from the divergent Ripple and Terraform rulings.

Ultimately, regulation-by-enforcement is no substitute for formal rulemaking and public comment processes, regardless of whether the target of that enforcement is a trading platform or a token issuer.

Thus, the SEC’s newfound focus on trading platforms raises the stakes while, ironically, making actual progress just as unlikely.

[2024-01-19] (x) The point of our result above is that fancy primitives like VDFs and ephemeral keys are the *only possible way* for proof-of-stake blockchains to defend against long-range attacks without any out-of-protocol assistance; there are no other options (10/11)
[2024-01-19] (x) Ephemeral keys, as seen e.g. in the Algorand blockchain, are not time malleable because they fail property (ii). Here, the old keys in K have expired and no longer enable an attacker to create new blocks (9/11)
[2024-01-19] (x) Verifiable delay functions (VDFs), as seen e.g. in the Chia blockchain, are not time malleable because they fail property (i). They prevent the attacker from quickly creating an alternative history (as in step 4 above) (8/11)
[2024-01-19] (x) Here a "time malleable" cryptographic primitive is one that (i) can be evaluated quickly and (ii) whose output does not depend on the time of evaluation. E.g., typical signature schemes and cryptographic hash functions are time malleable in this sense (7/11)
[2024-01-19] (x) Long-range attacks are most commonly dealt with out-of-protocol (e.g. https://t.co/INsDBGms4T). But can we repel them in-protocol, without appeal to something like "social consensus"? (5/11)
[2024-01-19] (x) 4. the attacker can now fork the blockchain from block 10000 and unilaterally create as long a chain as it wants
Now if some new user shows up after the attack, how can they distinguish the attacker chain from the "true" chain? (4/11)
[2024-01-19] (x) Example:
1. let K denote the private keys controlling stake at block 10000
2. suppose by block 20000 owners of keys in K have cashed out and transferred all their stake to other users
3. an attacker buys the keys in K for a song (they're no longer valuable to their owners) (3/11)
[2024-01-19] (x) We've known about long-range attacks for a long time (e.g. https://t.co/TrRMjUe15t). So what are they? (2/11)
[2024-01-19] (x) Last thread (promise!) on results from my permissionless consensus paper with @AndrewLewisPye. The topic: are long-range attacks unavoidable for proof-of-stake blockchains? (1/11)
