[2024-01-22] (x) Two years ago, I added Web3 to my "Making Markets" class for the first time.

Today, @BernsteinShai, @WhatTheLJW, and I launched "Building Web3 Businesses" at @HarvardHBS, and tomorrow I drop my book with @NFTbark, 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐄𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐲𝐭𝐡𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐓𝐨𝐤𝐞𝐧 📙.

What a journey. 🤝

Here are a few things I've come to believe along the way:

🔸️ Web3 has the potential to disrupt the monolithic power of today's top web platforms. It's not inevitable, but it's definitely real.

🔸️ The core mechanism by which this would work is giving people direct control of their data and other digital assets, along with the ability to seamlessly transfer them across platforms. This stands to enhance competition, and hopefully improve the consumer experience (it already has done both in some instances). 

🔸️ The driving network effect in this environment is different from what we're used to: In Web3, instead of network effects accruing to a specific platform, they accrue to the assets and protocols that people use most frequently. Many standard "moats" don't work in this environment, and new approaches are required.

🔸️ Simply the possibility that Web3 *could* reshape the Internet – and the critical differences in business strategy that Web3 necessitates – means that today's business leaders need to study and think critically about it. Even if you were skeptical of the Internet back in the 1990s, it would've still been important to understand what it might mean for your business.

🔸️ We have much more to learn as this unfolds.

🙏🙏🙏 I could never have done this without my extraordinary colleagues at both @HarvardHBS and @a16zcrypto, as well as my students, collaborators, and many friends in the world of Web3. I'm looking forward to continuing to work with you and learn from you, QED.

[2024-01-23] (x) @RSSH273 Under the current administration, the SEC has consistently sought to obfuscate its intent with statements like the one you've highlighted. In my opinion, those statements should not be given equal or more weight than the SEC's actual actions.

In particular, those actions include the SEC's (1) attempt to modify the definition of exchange, (2)  failure to allege that the swap feature of Coinbase's wallet was an unregistered exchange and (3) failure to bring any enforcement actions against DEXs for operating as unregistered exchanges. Sure, nothing is stopping the SEC from calling a DEX an exchange. But, I'd love to see a judge interpret that move in light of the foregoing. 

Meanwhile, if the threshold for forecasting SEC intent is to look for a concise and clear statement of said intent, then we all might as well just be relying on a crystal ball.
[2024-01-23] (x) @SatoadshiLite No doubt. They're already trying with the rule changes they've proposed for the Exchange Act.  But we'll fight them every step of the way.
[2024-01-23] (x) 12/ The same reasoning applies to most DEXs, so SEC attacks on most DEXs using broker rules should fail.

That may not deter this SEC though, as its actions against decentralization are more about stifling innovation than delivering on its stated mission.
[2024-01-23] (x) 11/ That means the extension of broker rules to Coinbase’s wallet is unwarranted and counterproductive – the risks the rules aim to shield people from are adeptly mitigated by the transparency of blockchains and smart contracts. 

So, broker rules shouldn't apply.
[2024-01-23] (x) 10/ “No Action Letters” for platforms with similar functionalities (eg, Finders, Internet Portals, Online Bulletin Boards) are all consistent on this point – 

Where there is no potential for execution risks or conflicts of interest to arise, broker regulations should not apply.
[2024-01-23] (x) 9/ Yes, Coinbase makes more money from more sales, but without a specific solicitation for transactions, the conflict of interest is no different than what exists in ordinary business arrangements that never trigger broker regulations (e.g. sales of cars, art, homes, etc.).
[2024-01-23] (x) 8/ In particular, the swap feature of the wallet does not give rise to any conflicts of interest that might be mitigated through regulation – 

There’s no “salesmen’s stake conflict” because there is no solicitation for investment or recommendation for purchases or sales.
[2024-01-23] (x) 7/ The SEC’s claim ultimately hinges on the alleged 1% fee Coinbase charged on all wallet swaps. 

However, the SEC’s own policy rationale for applying broker regulations where there is transaction-based compensation does NOT support an action against Coinbase.
[2024-01-23] (x) 6/ Per Coinbase, the wallet doesn't custody funds, negotiate or structure transactions, or make recommendations; it merely facilitates user-directed actions.

In fact, the wallet doesn’t implicate nearly any of the 16 factors commonly used by the SEC in finding broker activity. https://t.co/8H54n9wY9N
[2024-01-23] (x) 5/ Probably not. 

Even if we assume the SEC’s theory that the digital assets available for swapping via the wallet are subject to U.S. securities laws (the highly contested issue being argued in court), the SEC’s allegation that Coinbase is a broker should fail. 

Why?
[2024-01-23] (x) 4/ The SEC’s primary allegation against DEXs will likely be that they are operating as “unregistered brokers”. 

This is similar to the allegations brought against the swap feature of Coinbase’s wallet, which functions like a DEX. 

So, is that claim against CB likely to succeed?
[2024-01-23] (x) 3/ Fortunately, the SEC already conceded that DEXs aren’t “exchanges” under securities laws (one of the reasons why they’re seeking to expand the definition of “exchange”).

So, their best weapon against CEXs is likely off the table. 

But that may not deter them.
[2024-01-23] (x) 2/ The SEC's focus on centralized exchanges (CEXs) is a shift from its historic focus on token issuers.

The rationale for that shift (exchanges are a critical chokepoint and act as a gateway for new users) also applies to DEXs.
https://t.co/iIDlsBbZRV

[2024-01-25] (x) Decoding the art of scaling can unlock massive business value, help avert painful crises and put you in the top echelon of startup operators.

Knowing when, how and in what dimension to scale your company is one of the most perplexing and often misunderstood job requirements faced by founder/CEOs.

Here’s the 4 step framework I used to master this essential company building skill:

1. Start by thinking through all of the dimensions of scale that are relevant to your company and product.

Here are a few of my favorites:

- Product traction- Growth in users/customers/developers/subscribers, transactions, TVL, etc.
- Business- Revenue, cash flow, EBITDA, Net Income, Expenses
- Organization- Employees, executives, functions
- Geographies- Markets, office locations

2. Tune your senses, instincts and data collection in order to identify when and in what dimensions to scale in order to create the most value and growth.

Here are a few clarifying questions to ask yourself:

- Are customers in love with your product but you can’t deliver it to them fast enough?
- Is the market clearly happening but you’re being out maneuvered by a competitor who is getting to the most important customers first?
- Do you know what you need to build to win the market but don’t have the people or skills to do it quickly enough?

3. Take a first principles approach in designing your process to scale.

- Define the key results you are trying to achieve
- Deconstruct the obstacles to achieving your desired results
- Determine whether you have the talent inside the company to accomplish the mission or whether you need to look externally
- Put in place a v0.1 team and process designed to capture the opportunity
- Measure your results at regular intervals
- Iterate and improve

4. Heed these hard learned words of caution to avoid common pitfalls:

- Scaling any business or administrative function ahead of achieving a tight lock on product-market-fit will only serve to burn precious capital that you can never get back
- You can almost always catch up if you scale later than is ideal but building out a team or function too early usually results in a redo at best and a further waste of time and precious capital at worst

After one round trip through this process, you will likely feel like a battle hardened scaling pro and can lather, wash, rinse and repeat to scale other parts of your company.

Onward!

[2024-01-25] (x) Token extensions are a great unlock, but developers should be mindful of hidden risks.  

For example, if NK tries to use a token with confidential transfers to launder hack proceeds, the U.S. could try to sanction the token's smart contract mid-process, making the entire supply of that token toxic and adversely impacting legitimate users.

Fortunately, token extensions also give developers several ways to guard against NK using a given token.

[2024-01-29] (x) This new paper proposes a compelling and novel solution for one of the most critical web3 regulatory challenges – illicit finance. 

Few thoughts on how this solution fits within the "Regulate Web3 Apps, Not Protocols" framework:

TL;DR: The proposal fits within RANP and is complementary to its approach, which is focused on the regulation of web3 businesses, not software.

Let's dive in. The proposed framework for tackling illicit finance is broken down into three parts:

🔹(1) Measuring Decentralization – The team proposes to first measure the decentralization of a given DeFi protocol (looking at whether any individual has independent control of the protocol) in order to determine whether traditional regulations applicable to TradFi the BSA might be applicable. This is a logical first step. 

We used the same concept to establish criteria for determining whether any regulation should be applied to on-chain activity. Ultimately, where activity is truly disintermediated, traditional intermediary-based regulation does not make sense and a different approach is warranted. Where that disintermediation is merely a facade, it may make sense to apply traditional regulation.

🔹(2) Establishing Certain Protocols as Critical Infrastructure – The team next proposes that certain well-established protocols be deemed to be "critical infrastructure" and subject to oversight by the OCCIP. The proposal for such oversight is not a proposal that would violate the autonomy of such protocols. Rather, it would seek to be used to enhance the security and resilience of such protocols and reduce their operational risk.

In our RANP series, we similarly proposed a sliding scale approach to applying regulation to applications, with more established apps having a heavier regulatory burden. Further, we considered that this framework would entail certain requirements with respect to the underlying protocols that such applications gave users access to, such as code audits and disclosures.

As a result, these concepts can collectively be used to benefit users without subjecting software to regulation, which would diminish their global utility. Further, these approaches do not unnecessarily hinder developers, as the sliding scale approach would leave developers of nascent technologies undisturbed by regulation.
 
🔹(3)   Critical Communication Transmitters (CCTs). Finally the team proposes to apply certain tailored financial integrity risk management obligations on certain service providers that facilitate communications, most notably node-as-a-service providers for RPCs.

This proposal addresses a shortfall in the RANP framework when it comes to illicit finance. 

In particular, by proposing to only apply regulation to applications, RANP would not account for illicit finance activities of users interacting with non-compliant apps or directly with smart contracts (in circumvention of regulated applications). This new proposal would capture such activity without applying compliance obligations to RPC operators that would necessitate subjective determinations they aren't capable of making. For example, as discussed in the paper, an RPC operator can easily engage in risk scoring of a wallet, but cannot easily determine whether or not an individual transaction involves a token that is a security.

While it's true that bad actors can also technically circumvent compliant RPC operators, regulation does not need to eliminate the possibility of illicit use to be effective. This is particularly the case where the elimination of illicit activity would require regulation that is so burdensome that it negates the benefits of the technology or stifles innovation of potentially beneficial technologies. The proposal strikes a nice balance in that regard.

Overall, this proposal is a noteworthy effort that demonstrates a very nuanced understanding of the risks and benefits of blockchain technology. It should therefore be a useful tool for policymakers and regulators.

[2024-01-30] (x) @llamaonthebrink Agreed that everything being on-chain means that they theoretically have all the tools they need for transaction monitoring. However, I don’t think they’re currently capable of using that information to their full advantage, which is why they currently rely on providers like Chainalysis and TRM. 

This stuff is complicated, law enforcement is under resourced and the industry moves quickly, rolling out new chains and bridges at a fast clip.  So, you’re probably more likely to have success through public-private partnerships.

For protocols, I think that at most I’d be supportive of best practices like code audits being implemented (in line with our RANP framework). Don’t think they’re proposing that protocols develop massive compliance/reporting orgs, as that would obviously negate decentralization. 

But node-as-a-service businesses are generally centralized and could engage in some reporting and adopt other compliance measures that might prove helpful law enforcement without introducing a ton of censorship risk, like onchain KYC might do.
[2024-01-30] (x) @llamaonthebrink Depends on perspective. If you think the end state of crypto regulation is no regulation, then yes, this represents a concession. If you don’t think AML is worthwhile, then yes, this doesn’t achieve much. 

But regardless of the validity of those views, they’re currently extreme and unlikely to succeed. Instead, they’re likely to lead to someone like Warren getting her way, which is the far worse result. 

I think this approach could strike a good balance at preserving censorship resistance while giving law enforcement better tools than they have currently. Obviously, a lot will depend on the details and the hooks regulators have to control node operators. Hopefully minimal.

[2024-02-09] (x) Energized by the rate at which key crypto primitives are snapping into focus!

Some compelling recent/near horizon advancements include:

- Continued drumbeat of L2 progress in terms of reducing Ethereum transaction fees and increasing throughput- @optimism, @zksync, @arbitrum, impending EIP 4844 release to mainnet
- Reduced new user onboarding friction through embedded wallets- @privy, @dynamic_xyz 
- Increasingly robust AltDA capabilites on the horizon- @eigencloud, @CelestiaOrg 
- Super simple (and fun!) app development- @farcaster_xyz Frames

Historically, the confluence of these kinds of platform elements has been a major accelerant in terms of developer experimentation in new ecosystems. e.g.

- Web1- browser+Java application server+CDN
- Web2- mobile+social+public cloud

Excited to see where this goes!

[2024-02-15] (x) "For experts striving to unravel its causes, one of the biggest shocks has been that many who have suffered from long Covid were never badly sick with the virus in the first place." https://t.co/YOx2f6DddZ via @ft (h/t @a_nichifor)
[2024-02-16] (x) Independently, @Suflaky and @yaish_aviv proved a similar impossibility result for the case of deterministic mechanisms. Their paper is well worth reading, as their proof techniques are interesting and different from ours: https://t.co/vuc1HIHPMW 7/7
[2024-02-16] (x) Caveat: this work considers the original, pre-MEV model for transaction fee mechanism design. Adding in MEV does change things: https://t.co/2P6Dd7ZKs1 6/7
[2024-02-16] (x) Answer: no, even for randomized mechanisms! In this sense, the EIP-1559 and tipless mechanisms are "optimally incentive-compatible." 5/7
[2024-02-16] (x) A variant that I called the "tipless mechanism" has the same set of properties, except that it loses OCA-proofness rather than DSIC when the base fee is too low. Obvious question: can we get all three properties, all the time, with no extra conditions? 4/7
[2024-02-16] (x) EIP-1559 satisfies all three properties as long as the base fee is high enough that all eligible transactions fit into a single double-size (30M gas) block. But if the base fee is too low, it effectively reverts to a first-price auction and loses DSIC 3/7
[2024-02-16] (x) DSIC: users have an "obvious optimal bid"
MMIC: miner/validator is instructed to maximize their fee revenue, has no incentive to insert fake transactions
OCA-proof: if miner + all users collude off-chain, they can't do better collectively than in canonical on-chain outcome 2/7
[2024-02-16] (x) Happy to report that the main open theory question from my original work on EIP-1559 has been resolved (with two brilliant collaborators, Hao Chung and @ElaineRShi)---no transaction fee mechanism can be DSIC, MMIC, and OCA-proof!  https://t.co/gUEsMBXZIg
(more context below) 1/7
[2024-02-16] (x) Some of my favorite mentions from the Farcaster community:  https://t.co/OvjYjPDL3o
[2024-02-16] (x) @JeremyDanielFox @ho_ben @johnjhorton @MatthewGNagler It's not obvious to me that it's uniformly against their interests to allow interoperability -- for example, these games spend a lot of time and money advertising; allowing item interoperability into complementary platforms and games sort of turns those items into user-driven embedded advertisements.
[2024-02-16] (x) @ho_ben @JeremyDanielFox @johnjhorton @MatthewGNagler And the "solely you control" thing might not sound like a big deal relative to the way we hand over lots of other identity control to platforms, but we're seeing increasingly many issues with account compromise in both infrastructure providers and application platforms like social media.

In the world where the verification asset lives onchain (and those accounts are secure) the threat model only applies at the time of verification asset creation -- you have to trust someone to create the asset properly, but you don't have to rely on their continued security to maintain your critical identity marker.
[2024-02-16] (x) @ho_ben @JeremyDanielFox @johnjhorton @MatthewGNagler In this case I actually think a critical role of blockchain is the interoperability it provides -- even to the extent that verification does require a secure and trusted off chain party initially, once you have that secure verification, on a blockchain, it resides in an account that solely you control, and that you can privately prove ownership of to any third party platform when you post a message.

[2024-02-22] (x) Today, we're announcing that we're leading @eigencloud's Series B.

It's exceedingly rare to come across a founder with a vision and plan that has the potential to change everything. I have come to know @sreeramkannan to be not only brilliant, but also fundamentally motivated by a vision of the future so crisp and compelling that it momentarily becomes real when you talk to him. Sreeram has what I like to call the Promethean flame; he is driven to bring a new technology to life first and foremost because of the future it will create.

I first met Sreeram in 2019. At the time, he was still a professor at the University of Washington and getting started on his journey as an entrepreneur. Sreeram made a strong impression, and since then we've had many touchpoints through the years with both him and the world-class team that he has brought together. In 2022, Sreeram gave an eye-opening talk in our summer research fellowship about @eigencloud and @eigen_da, and last year, he and @soubhik_deb, EigenLayer’s head of protocol research, published a comprehensive overview of the Cryptoeconomics of Slashing on our blog.

We're excited to partner with Sreeram and the whole team on building a platform that unlocks a new dimension of open innovation on top of Ethereum.

Background

It's difficult to appreciate the full breadth of EigenLayer without first zooming out: In the beginning, there was Bitcoin. It was the first example of an entire blockchain that was built for the purpose of a single application, which at the time was ostensibly payments, but since then has turned out to be more like digital gold—a store of value, albeit a volatile one.

The genius of Ethereum was to unbundle the monolithic, single-app Bitcoin approach by separating consensus from the application logic that is built on top. Ethereum makes it possible for developers to write their own applications—in the form of smart contracts—that leverage the blockchain's pooled security and computational resources. This change alone unlocked a huge design space for open innovation which has led to almost everything we know and love about the space today. It means that people can now build decentralized applications without having to spin up their own blockchains.

Ethereum's greater level of programmability has been a huge unlock for a whole set of applications. But, it's not without limitations. It still mandates a lot about what you can and cannot do as a developer. For example, the code you write must be compatible with the Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM), which will continue to be a great computational model for certain applications—those that are lightweight enough to fit within Ethereum's block-size and gas limits—but not for others that might be heavier-weight or need specialized features.

In an ideal world, you as a developer should have almost complete freedom. If you need to, you should be able to build a decentralized service with code for any instruction set, your own consensus mechanism, your own data propagation algorithm, your own block time and gas limits, and even your own set of hardware requirements for validators (like GPUs or trusted execution environments). You would be able to do all of that without having to spin up your own blockchain network from scratch. Instead, you would be able to build on top of the already decentralized and secure validator set of the most secure programmable blockchain: Ethereum.

Enter EigenLayer

EigenLayer is a set of smart contracts on Ethereum that creates a two-sided marketplace for raw cryptoeconomic security: On the demand side, it allows creators of custom decentralized services to write code that effectively extends the functionality of Ethereum. And, on the supply side, validators participating in Ethereum consensus—if they happen to have the capacity and required hardware—can opt into running each of those decentralized services by restaking their ETH to them. The net result is that EigenLayer is a decentralized and market-based mechanism for extending Ethereum’s capabilities.

The implications of this are profound. We believe that, by further unbundling cryptoeconomic security from the EVM, EigenLayer has the potential to unlock 100x faster innovation on technologies like consensus mechanisms, novel virtual machines, decentralized oracles, bridges, and networks with specialized hardware in the same way that Ethereum unlocked 100x faster innovation at the application logic level by introducing smart contracts.

We're very excited to partner with Sreeram and team to help them bring this vision to life.

[2024-02-28] (x) @jp_koning @chrisnovaklaw @austincampbell @ImperiumPaper Analogizing bank transfers to on-chain transactions is an inherently flawed position.

[2024-03-04] (x) “In Japanese pottery, there’s an artful form of repair called kintsugi. When a piece of ceramic pottery breaks, rather than trying to restore it to its original condition, the artisan accentuates the fault by using gold to fill the crack. This beautifully draws attention to where the work was broken, creating a golden vein. Instead of the flaw diminishing the work, it becomes a focal point, an area of both physical and aesthetic strength. The scar also tells the story of the piece, chronicling its past experience.”

—Rick Rubin, The Creative Act

[2024-03-05] (x) CSX is coming to New York City this Fall. A fantastic opportunity for crypto founders - applications are now open!
[2024-03-05] (x) Applications are now open for CSX Fall 2024 in New York City!

This is the first year that our Crypto Startup Accelerator (CSX) program – which provides expert guidance, capital, and resources for founders of early-stage crypto startups – will have two cohorts, one for Spring and one for Fall. 

So as we gear up to start the CSX Spring 2024 program in London with 24 companies later this month, we’re also kicking off our search for bold and ambitious crypto founders to join our Fall program taking place in New York City.

The ten-week, in-person CSX Fall 2024 program in New York City will start in September and end with a Demo Day in November. Applications will remain open until March 29th.

New York is now a premier global hub for innovative crypto startups and technical talent, and Fall 2024 participants will be plugged into a vibrant network of leading companies and experts. Over the course of the program, they’ll receive hands-on guidance, mentorship, and inspiration from some of the best leaders and operators in crypto today.

We’re looking for founders that are dead-set on building something transformational, regardless of where they are based, so encourage startups from across the world to apply. a16z crypto invests in each participating company, with standard terms of $500,000 for 7% equity and other standard rights.

I couldn’t be more thrilled to host CSX in New York later this year. 

The application link is in my next tweet below.

[2024-03-08] (x) 9/ The law goes into effect on July 1, 2024.

[2024-03-08] (x) 8/ The DUNA provides a much needed oasis from that risk without overly restricting how DAOs are currently launched and operated – It:
🔹safeguards decentralization;
🔹empowers DAOs to grow;
🔹enables development of economic models; and 🔹keeps blockchain networks open.
[2024-03-08] (x) 7/ This comes at a critical time.

Regulatory and legal attacks are on the rise. Many allege that without a legal entity, a DAO is just a general partnership. 

That categorization would be catastrophic for DAO members, subjecting them to untenable tax and liability risk. https://t.co/qTEK4Ko9qf
[2024-03-08] (x) 6/ Further, the DUNA could help strengthen arguments that the digital assets of a project are not investment contracts (aka securities) under the Howey test. 

From @CowrieLLC 's and my article: https://t.co/OmwsDefdJe
[2024-03-08] (x) 5/ And even though it’s called a “nonprofit association,” the DUNA is able to both engage in profit making activities and pay reasonable compensation to DUNA members for any services provided to its ecosystem. 

This unlocks a huge design space for protocol economic models.
[2024-03-08] (x) 4/ The DUNA solves these challenges without exposing consumers to additional risks. 

Anyone building a consumer-facing application on top of a blockchain network, like a social media app, would continue to use traditional entity forms. https://t.co/48yRlz6Vx4
[2024-03-08] (x) 3/ Critically, the bill solves three key challenges DAOs face:
1⃣ It gives them legal existence, enabling them to contract with third parties;
2⃣ It enables them to pay taxes; and
3⃣ It provides limited liability from the actions of other members. https://t.co/pMc8PdlFPT
[2024-03-08] (x) 2/ Wyoming’s new law will enable blockchain projects to operate within the bounds of applicable laws without compromising their decentralization. It also keeps them permissionless.

This is a major breakthrough.
[2024-03-08] (x) 🚨Big News🚨Wyoming just enacted a game-changing law for web3.

It creates a new entity for DAOs – the Decentralized Unincorporated Nonprofit Association (DUNA) – that’s been years in the making.

@CowrieLLC and I provide everything you need to know below:
https://t.co/ah7uA78mc2
[2024-03-08] (x) Strong disagree. The only appropriate way to demonstrate this is through the ritual of Kanly.
[2024-03-09] (x) @Eric_Peterson_ My colleagues are accusing me of doing all of this just for the memes, which isn’t totally untrue.
[2024-03-09] (x) @rizzn Neither UNAs or the WY DUNA are subject to the corporate transparency act, so no reporting of beneficial interest to FinCEN is required. The DUNA actually safeguards privacy. We’ve been explicit in our writing that this is a core benefit of the structure.

I ain’t eating bugs.
[2024-03-09] (x) @CowrieLLC @prestonjbyrne Yeah, this is a great analysis with thoughtful critique. Two thoughts to add:

My greatest concern about DAO liability stems from member v member suits (which we’ve already seen) and responsibility for actions of other members. The DUNA solves both of those. I’m less concerned about the risk of regulatory actions being brought against individuals as that risk can be mitigated by simply not approving illegal actions. Ultimately, the DUNA should not be used as a shield to enable oneself to engage in reckless illicit activity without a regard for consequences.

We also envision fewer problems with the agency stuff as we ultimately don’t think DUNAs should be operated like businesses. They are intended to provide legal existence to autonomous software and the members that maintain the affairs of that software, but that’s about it. That should not call for many contracts or real world business engagements. Grants for development work and ecosystem growth would still be better done out of a foundation. And websites/interfaces would still be better run by c-corps.
[2024-03-09] (x) @defi_guy_too @jay_enne_ @MakerDAO 😂. Not sure. It’d be hard to call its reliance on the DeFi Education Fund and the goodwill of industry actors to save it from patent trolls a resilient example of effective decentralization.

[2024-03-12] (x) @dotta Not quite. Legal entity structures are a matter of state law, so there's not much the federal government can do to interfere. They just tried to interfere a bit with the Corporate Transparency Act, and a court just ruled that as unconstitutional.

The avenue of attack the federal government has had on weed is that they've leveraged their authority over banking and payment systems to essentially prohibit the banking of weed companies. But the federal government still can't come to your house and arrest you for smoking weed.
[2024-03-12] (x) None of the above should be taken as investment or legal advice. See https://t.co/55lOHruWeJ for more info.
[2024-03-12] (x) 🔟 Bipartisan – The WY DUNA law was a bi-partisan effort of @rothfuss & @cyruswestern. 

Smart regulation around innovation and blockchain technology should not be a partisan issue, and this law should get broader support as a result of its bi-partisan creation.
[2024-03-12] (x) 9⃣ Preserve Flexibility – The WY DUNA is designed to be incredibly flexible. Organizations can morph between a DUNA and an UNA easily, and the DUNA can be dissolved with little effort. That reduces the  risk of adopting it.
[2024-03-12] (x) 8⃣ Prepare Tax Strategies – DUNAs enable DAOs to pay taxes to the extent any are owed and creates a legal entity capable of strategic tax planning. 

There’s still a lot of uncertainty here, but having a legal entity is an effective first step towards resolving that uncertainty.
[2024-03-12] (x) 7⃣ Promote Decentralization – Protections for DAO members means the risks of participation are reduced, which should promote decentralization. The inherent decentralization of the structure could also help mitigate risks of securities laws.
[2024-03-12] (x) 6⃣ Mitigate Consumer Risk – The foregoing protections for DAO members don’t need to put consumers at risk. 

Consumer-facing apps built on top of blockchain networks, like a social media apps, should continue to use traditional entity forms. DUNAs aren't for trad businesses!
[2024-03-12] (x) 4⃣ Limit Liability – DUNAs provide for limited liability among DAO members. This will only become more important as web3 progresses.

5⃣ Enforce Arbitration – DUNAs can require all members to consent to arbitration, further insulating members from spurious litigation.
[2024-03-12] (x) 3⃣ Preserve Privacy – DUNAs preserve DAO-member privacy. The Corporate Transparency Act isn’t applicable to DUNAs and they aren’t required to KYC users to ensure they meet the 100 member threshold requirement.
[2024-03-12] (x) 2⃣ Provide Legal Existence – DUNAs enable DAOs to contract with third parties. This is critical as it enables them to hire lawyers and defend themselves in court. DAOs that don’t show up face the most severe consequences.
[2024-03-12] (x) 1⃣ Lets DAOs be DAOs – DUNAs enable DAOs to continue to be governed by smart contracts. That means they can continue to be decentralized, autonomous and permissionless.
[2024-03-12] (x) Wyoming’s DUNA legal structure for DAOs goes live on July 1, 2024. 

Companies that (1) have a U.S. nexus and (2) are using tokens to decentralize the governance of an asset (a treasury, autonomous software, etc) should consider it.

Here are 10 benefits of the DUNA👇

[2024-03-16] (x) @CryptoTaxGuyETH @jchervinsky Never in my career have I seen a lawyer be so confident in his own broad-sweeping conclusions when he doesn't even know the underlying facts of a given problem, let alone the hundreds of different variables that could materially change the problem and the available solution.

All strategic tax planning necessitates understanding the facts from the ground up and involves an assessment and weighing of risks. Highlighting risks and mitigating factors in public forums serves no purpose other than engagement, which comes at the cost of opening oneself up to attacks by a government that has repeatedly demonstrated a willingness to act in bad faith.

I'd similarly never map out a detailed strategy for mitigating risks under securities laws while highlighting where companies are likely to have lingering issues.

We also won't respond by pointing out specific issues in many of the existing structures you support as we don't want any project to be unfairly prejudiced. You are obviously free to continue to do so.

Good luck, Jason.

[2024-03-21] (x) Today, we’re announcing that we’re leading @EspressoSys’ Series B ☕

Ethereum’s scalability end-game is all about rollups—potentially thousands or even millions of rollups, each one a mini-ecosystem or an application that is built on top of Ethereum’s bedrock of security. The ideas behind how this can work were first laid out by @VitalikButerin a few years back in his now-famous rollup-centric roadmap post. Since then, other ecosystem players like @zksync, @Optimism, and @0xPolygon have extended that vision with roadmaps for Hyperchains, the Superchain, and Polygon’s AggLayer. For the first time, there is a clear path that Ethereum can follow to reach a scale that supports millions of applications and billions of users.

This plan, however, is not without its limitations. The biggest disadvantage of a rollup-centric future is the fragmentation of the Ethereum ecosystem. Today, on the Ethereum main chain, every application (i.e. smart contract) can seamlessly interact with every other application. In a world of millions of rollups, composability with applications will become harder. This is because each individual rollup is its own separate blockchain. They all share Ethereum’s security, but computation proceeds independently on each one. Each rollup is driven by what’s known as a “sequencer,” which today is a centralized party that performs all of the computation for a rollup on a server.

Today, if an app on one rollup wants to interact with an app on another, it has to either go through the main Ethereum chain or a bridge. Both approaches are much slower and make it harder for developers to synchronize state across rollups (they are asynchronous solutions). In a better world, sequencers of different rollups would have a mechanism to cooperate with each other, giving developers and end-users a smoother, faster, and cheaper interoperability experience.

The Espresso Team

Before I tell you about @EspressoSys, I need to tell you about the people behind it. I have known @benafisch and @benediktbuenz since before I joined @a16zcrypto. It was early 2017, I was at Google Brain, and both Ben and Benedikt were Ph.D. students under @danboneh at Stanford, who I knew very well from my undergrad days. Thanks to Dan, we were all connected and were advising a new crypto company that would later go on to become @eco—which is now also an a16z crypto portfolio company. I remember being very inspired by both of their technical brilliance. For a brief moment, I was even tempted to pursue a Ph.D. in cryptography under Dan, as they were doing.

What has always been most impressive to me about Ben in particular is his ability to think clearly at every level of abstraction—from the lowest-level cryptographic primitives, through every business and product consideration, and all the way to the broadest societal effects—for a new technology that he is building. I should have known it was inevitable for him to transition from his role in academia to that of a startup founder and CEO.

As it turned out, I did not go on to get a Ph.D. Instead, I left Google and joined a16z to become a venture capitalist, which is almost the same thing, right? Shortly thereafter, I met @jillgun through crypto circles, and while she was at Slow Ventures, we became fast friends. What has always been so impressive to me about Jill is her insane ability to think strategically. She has the sharpest intuitions about how the world works and how people are likely to operate within it. So, it was impossible to not take note when she joined Ben, Benedikt, and @charleslu1 at Espresso in 2021.

Along the way, through his work at Espresso, I have also gotten to know Charles. He is technically brilliant, like everyone else at Espresso, and also worked with Dan at Stanford. But most importantly, he is the perfect complement to Ben, Benedikt, and Jill because of his excellent operational capabilities. Espresso wouldn’t be the organization that it is without Charles.

Last year, as some of the limitations of Ethereum’s rollup-based roadmap became clear, Ben and team pitched us the most compelling solution to the fragmentation problem that we had heard.

Espresso Systems

Espresso is building a decentralized network that acts as a coordination mechanism for rollup sequencers. It is a marketplace in which sequencers can both sell their blockspace and participate in a mechanism that unifies transaction sequencing across rollups. With Espresso, rollups will be able to sell the right to build their blocks to proposers bidding for them in the network. Those proposers can also simultaneously bid on multiple rollup blocks to become shared proposers for more than one chain at once. This allows rollups to continue to evolve independently while also interoperating more seamlessly with other rollups.

At its core, crypto is a technology movement that builds tools for better human cooperation at a larger scale—especially when the cooperating parties mutually distrust one another. Espresso and Ethereum are the perfect embodiment of that ideal.

We're very excited to be partnering with Ben, Benedikt, Jill, Charles and the Espresso team to help them bring to life a more seamless and interoperable future for Ethereum.

[2024-03-22] (x) The bottom line: Even though misinformation is a huge problem, it's not one that content moderation can solve. Platforms can help keep us safer by focusing on what content moderation can do, and giving up on what it can't.

You can read the full article here @TheAtlantic: https://t.co/FqTER4DiCt
[2024-03-22] (x) And, if platforms accept our reasoning, they can divert resources from the task of deciding what is true, toward the still hard, but more pragmatic task, of determining what enables harm – and screening that harmful content out.
[2024-03-22] (x) There are clear challenges and downsides to tech companies deciding what content is and is not directly harmful. But platforms already routinely make judgments about this, and often language or context makes it possible to do algorithmically.
[2024-03-22] (x) A similar logic extends to other kinds of harmful content, like doxxing or hate speech. There, it's the content itself – not the beliefs it encourages – that is the root of the harm, and platforms can indeed successfully moderate it.
[2024-03-22] (x) Unlike with misinformation, blocking information that enables harm can work even if users don't trust the platform's motives. That's because it's the information itself that enables the harm, blocking it blocks the harm as well.
[2024-03-22] (x) Blocking that information wouldn't have prevented users from believing that the election was stolen – but by making it harder to coordinate where and when to go, it still might have helped slow the momentum of the eventual insurrection, thus limiting the post's real-world harms.
[2024-03-22] (x) Going back to Trump's post, imagine that the platform had simply made it much harder for him to communicate the date (January 6) and place (Washington, D.C.) for supporters to gather. https://t.co/93WRSBUcTe
[2024-03-22] (x) Our analysis also shows that content moderation can be very effective when it blocks information that can be used to do something harmful.
[2024-03-22] (x) Does this mean that content moderation is always counterproductive?

Far from it!
[2024-03-22] (x) And when users don't trust a platform, even well-meaning attempts to make things better can make them worse. (Indeed, when platforms seem to be taking sides, that can add fuel to the very fire they are trying to put out.)
[2024-03-22] (x) But of course, users often don't trust platforms – they haven't for years! https://t.co/yP38WOwcq8
[2024-03-22] (x) The logic is simple: If users believe the platform is benevolent and all-knowing, then if something is blocked or flagged, it must be false; and conversely if information is let through, it must be true.
[2024-03-22] (x) Our analysis shows that if users trust the platform to both know and do what's right – and the platform truly does know what's true and what isn't – then it's possible to successfully eliminate misinformation.
[2024-03-22] (x) Unless users trust the platform's motivations and its process, any action by the platform can look like evidence of something it is not – and this means attempts to moderate misinformation can backfire. https://t.co/9TXxgwmKgL
[2024-03-22] (x) Jesse's and my research, which we detail further in a recent @nberpubs working paper, shows that this challenge is intrinsic:
[2024-03-22] (x) But does anyone seriously believe that the people storming the Capitol on January 6 would be persuaded that Biden won just because @BPC_Bipartisan told Facebook that everything was above-board?

If anything, they might have thought it was further evidence of a cover-up.

[2024-03-22] (x) But while platforms can block, flag, or mute content that they judge to be false, that doesn’t necessarily stop users from believing it.

In fact, because many lies are believed by those who distrust the "establishment," blocking or flagging false claims can make things worse.

[2024-03-28] (x) One additional thought on "schemes" under Howey, which I think Judge Failla missed on.

In the @a16zcrypto and @paradigm amicus brief, we argued that even if a contract wasn’t necessary, the SEC still needed to allege some type of “scheme” in order to satisfy Howey. Judge Failla almost got this...

She cited to the Telegram case in order to establish that a contract wasn't necessary under Howey -- in that case the judge ruled that secondary transactions of Grams were securities transactions despite the lack of existence of a legal contract because Telegram was issuing Grams to investors and insiders at a discount with no lockup, and those individuals were then going to sell to retail at launch. As a result, the judge in Telegram deemed the arrangement to be a "scheme" and that Howey was satisfied.

Fallia used Telegram as evidence that a contract wasn’t necessary, but what she failed to do is look at what a "scheme" actually is. The "scheme" in Telegram looks nothing like what the SEC alleged with respect to the tokens named in the case against CB.

So, yes, even if a "scheme" is sufficient to show Howey is met, the SEC failed to allege any scheme existed. Should definitely hit this in the trial.

Here's the brief:
https://t.co/2r6epmMKsZ
[2024-03-28] (x) @jordanteague Agreed. The opinion was very thoughtful on a lot of things, but totally whiffed here. 

Under the judge’s reasoning, a commercial landlord holding a tenant’s security deposit in connection with a rental agreement would make the whole thing a securities transaction.
[2024-03-28] (x) Having now read the Coinbase ruling, here are my top four takeaways:

1⃣ Decentralization – The ruling is consistent with the guidance the SEC provided in 2018 and 2019 about decentralization, so to the extent token issuers have been following that guidance, not much should change. 

2⃣ Brokers – The reasoning for the dismissal of the claim against CB’s wallet tracks the argument we laid out in October on why that claim should fail. This is a big win for the industry and decentralized technology, but I am worried it will drive the SEC to accelerate their proposed rule change regarding the definition of “exchange” to scope in DEXs. More:
https://t.co/xpm4DIU6jw

3⃣Major Questions Doctrine – I'm surprised how one-sided the judge’s opinion is on MQD. In the Uniswap class action, the same judge indicated that Congress needs to determine how digital assets are regulated. Further, she’s completely neglected to contemplate the implications of the SEC’s legal theory. If an investment contract can exist where there are no rights to ongoing efforts, it’s very difficult to see how Taylor Swift tickets and Nikes aren’t investment contracts. That's far more impactful on the economy than what the judge assessed.

4⃣Staking as a Service – The court’s discussion of staking as a service is troubling as it sets a very low threshold for what constitutes “managerial and entrepreneurial efforts.” CB’s offering was appropriately designed to not involve much discretion and be very technical-only, so it’s hard to see how it involves managerial or entrepreneurial efforts. This could have significant implications more broadly for anyone relying on decentralization under Howey.
[2024-03-28] (x) I will do a more detailed drilldown soon, but for now, check out the paper here: https://t.co/YCok7Ojh4F
[2024-03-28] (x) Excited to announce a new paper with Piotr Dworczak, @marco_reuter, and @ChanghwaLee_:

We give a framework for "𝗢𝗽𝘁𝗶𝗺𝗮𝗹 𝗠𝗲𝗺𝗯𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗽 𝗗𝗲𝘀𝗶𝗴𝗻" – with applications to everything from concert ticket and NFT sales to college admissions/affirmative action policy. 

(Did you know that Taylor Swift and the Harvard admissions office are solving versions of the same fundamental math problem? 🧑‍🎓👀)

In this case, at least, there's an argument that a @MassGenBrigham-provided masks might be higher-quality than the one the average patient brings in, although surgical masks are still not optimal for preventing transmission.
[2024-03-29] (x) This from @MassGenBrigham is absurd: masks are no longer required -- or, as far as I can tell, even strongly recommended -- but if you *do* wear one, you have to put one of their surgical masks over it.

To be clear, this is a lot better than a previous policy that required you to *replace* your mask with one of theirs even if yours was more protective.

But it still makes no sense. At least in some cases, putting a surgical mask over a respirator can create pressure that might break the seal, reducing the protective effect.

And meanwhile, they're not doing anything to stop people who come in unmasked from breathing all over the space, so why should they place extra constraints on those who are trying reduce risk?
[2024-03-30] (x) @TheStalwart I haven't read this study carefully, but they at least claim *in the case of McDonald's specifically* that "Higher minimum wages are not associated with faster adoption of touch-screen ordering, and there is near-full price pass-through of minimum wages." https://t.co/Hzp9HF2j10

[2024-04-01] (x) @DragicMarkovic @goblintown I have no idea what could possibly be amusing about this. Everyone gets haircuts
[2024-04-02] (x) Full paper is here, comments and questions are welcome!: https://t.co/MVYKyejWHf (19/19)
[2024-04-02] (x) Credible auction buffs may also enjoy Section 5.2, which shows a sense in which strong shill-proofness =&gt; credibility =&gt; weak shill-proofness (18/19)
[2024-04-02] (x) Result 4: the number of rounds cannot be compressed to 1. In general, impossible to get weakly shill-proof+optimal+strategyproof+one-shot; for optimal auctions, can pick 2 and only 2 of {weakly shill-proof,strategyproof,one-shot} (17/19) https://t.co/74iBMsz8PL
[2024-04-02] (x) Result 3: if you want an optimal auction that is also strategyproof, not only can you use an English auction (as in the example), but you can also use a "compressed" version that replaces the later rounds with a (one-shot) Vickrey auction (16/19) https://t.co/mL3CKqK7LB
[2024-04-02] (x) Result 2: if you want an auction that is robustly efficient (item goes to bidder with highest valuation, auction format not overly tailored to valuation distribution), you're still more or less forced to use a Dutch auction (15/19) https://t.co/wu02gltYQo
[2024-04-02] (x) The rest of the results are about weakly shill-proof auctions (a substantially richer design space, as the English auction example makes clear) (14/19)
[2024-04-02] (x) Result 1 (of 4): the Dutch auction (with an optimal reserve) is the unique auction that is strongly shill-proof and optimal (i.e., revenue-maximizing); thus, such auctions must be iterative (with possibly many rounds) and cannot be strategy-proof (13/19) https://t.co/FQdV8tsYi3
[2024-04-02] (x) We focus on "public" auctions in which every bidder’s action is publicly observable (motivated by typical English/Dutch auctions, and typical Web3 auctions); cf., the private channels communication model in credible auctions @akbarpour_ @ShengwuLi (12/19)
[2024-04-02] (x) The answers can be visualized as a 4-D array, projected here as four 2 by 2 tables: (11/19) https://t.co/tniFJTm1rL
[2024-04-02] (x) Given these examples, anyone who knows us could guess the questions that we inevitably got obsessed with: (10/19) https://t.co/tSIomNlOOx
[2024-04-02] (x) Could any auction be strongly shill-proof? Sure, in a Dutch (descending) auction, any shill bid by the seller terminates the auction immediately, leaving the seller holding the item and earning zero revenue (9/19)
[2024-04-02] (x) Translating this to our terminology, the English auction (with optimal reserve) is *weakly shill-proof* but is not *strongly shill-proof* (8/19)
[2024-04-02] (x) If the seller *did* know bidders' precise valuations, they would want to shill (i.e., prolong the auction) up until the price is just below the highest bidder valuation (7/19)
[2024-04-02] (x) Example: single-item auction, iid bidder valuations drawn from a regular distribution. In an English (ascending) auction with an optimal reserve price, a seller without extra knowledge of bidders' valuations has no incentive to shill (6/19)
[2024-04-02] (x) But wait: For private-value auctions, isn’t a shill bid the same thing as a reserve price? The answer depends on when the seller has an opportunity to shill and the information available to them at that time (5/19)
[2024-04-02] (x) Auction theory typically punts on dealing with seller deviations like shill bids via appeal to ummodeled concepts such as “rule of law.” To what extent can these deviations instead be disincentivized through an auction’s design? (4/19)
[2024-04-02] (x) A *shill bid* is a bid submitted by the seller in order to manipulate the selling price of an auction. Shill bids have been around forever (e.g., the major art auction houses explicitly reserve the right to use them) and appear to be particularly common in online auctions (3/19) https://t.co/SUULhZ6M5R
[2024-04-02] (x) The paper lays out a theory of *shill-proof* auctions (2/19) https://t.co/tdXGZY7dgK
[2024-04-02] (x) • Which auction formats are robust to shill bidding?

• Why are Dutch auctions so popular in Web3?

• What happens if you change the credible auctions model to have public rather than private communication?

A new paper with Andrew Komo and @skominers answers all these questions 👇 (1/19)

2024: This memecoin predicts the future.

[2024-04-02] (x) Super excited about this new paper with Andrew Komo and @Tim_Roughgarden:

We give a theory of "shill-proof" auctions, asking when an auction's design can disincentivize shill bidding.

The Dutch auction turns out to be particularly distinguished, and that's not all... 👇👀 #EconTwitter
[2024-04-03] (x) @renepickhardt @skominers @peterktodd @wiz Not 100% following your questions, but in general, in settings where a seller can easily submit shill bids, one would expect better price discovery from shill-proof auctions than from non-shill-proof ones
[2024-04-03] (x) @matty_ @skominers Depends on the model. In our model here, bidders act sequentially, and the seller/shill bidders see all previous bids that have been made. If it's a simultaneous-move sealed-bid auction (outside our model), where shill bidders know nothing about other bids other than the prior, then a shill bid can typically be simulated by a reserve price
[2024-04-03] (x) @Fair_Block Note that in our model the seller/shill bidders are assumed to see the previous bids. As you say, can potentially escape the model and its conclusions via cryptography

[2024-04-09] (x) A small team built Jolt quickly: @samrags_ @moodlezhoup and @SuccinctJT with support from @NoahCitron and @arasuarun, starting Fall last year. (And all of these people have other responsibilities!) https://t.co/IMhAEfZwyn
[2024-04-09] (x) Jolt is implemented in Rust, using sum-check techniques and other new cryptography. The goal is to be the fastest, simplest, and easiest to extend zkVM. It's a public good we hope accelerates the crypto industry, scales blockchains, and gets more kinds of computation onchain.
[2024-04-09] (x) Today we're sharing our biggest open source release yet: Jolt Alpha, our zkVM built new from the ground up. The UX is excellent and it's time to share what we've learned. In short: it's fast and concise.

Learn more below! https://t.co/OERrWcF2AE

[2024-04-18] (x) @Andrew___Baker @skominers Okay, so you think the SEC has authority to regulate. That's fine. But given that, why has the SEC engaged in regulation-by-enforcement instead of rulemaking? 

Why take a path that leads to disparate results, creates confusion and perpetuates uncertainty rather than a path that clarifies their approach? 

Is it just because the industry says the SEC doesn't have authority? That'd be a pretty childish response. 

Is it because asset classes don't get separate rules? No, we have separate rules for asset-backed securities and it's easy to demonstrate how crypto doesn't fit within existing rules.

So, what could possibly be the reason the SEC doesn't engage in a more transparent and direct approach? 

The answer is politics.

[2024-04-19] (x) @GoodPoint123 Great question. It's less about costs to operate, and more about power dynamics between the users and the network operators. It comes down to a few critical properties of the network design - which may seem like small details but actually have profound economic consequences.

1. Users can easily exit (low switching costs)
2. Code is open source (fewer barriers to entry)
3. Data is public (level information playing field)
4. Protocols are extensible (open access for builders)
5. Platforms can commit to their rules (can’t be evil)

How can blockchains give networks these special properties? It's because prior to blockchains, software had to be “run” (as was therefore controlled) by trusted hardware. Blockchains now allow software to be executed without trusted hardware - which totally inverts the power dynamics.
[2024-04-19] (x) Here are some (broadly-defined) areas where I believe crypto can make a difference in the world.

1/ Payments
Problem: Payments are highly inefficient, especially when sending them across borders. People spend $647 billion on remittances every year, with an average cost of 6.25%.

Solution: Blockchains create a new global standard that cuts out all of the middlemen involved in a transaction. Payments can be extremely cheap (<$0.01), instantly settled, and global by default - just like sending an email.

2/ Digital Identity
Problem: Internet services (e.g., Facebook, YouTube, Spotify) have full control over their users’ identities and the data (e.g., followers, usernames, digital goods) they earn, purchase, or create. Consumers have no choice but to rely on these companies to safeguard this data, since they can’t exit the network while retaining their identity and everything built around it.

Solution: Blockchains empower users to control their identity and data across all their interactions online. If one service provider acts maliciously or becomes overly extractive, users can exit the system while porting their identity and all the data they contributed.

3/ Fair Marketplaces
Problem: Profit-motivated companies are extractive. For example, Apple charges up to 30% in fees on purchases made in mobile apps by consumers. YouTube takes a 45% cut of ad revenue before paying out its creators. Spotify has a 30% take rate.

Solution: Blockchain-based network architecture leads to much lower take rates and better outcomes for consumers. This is not just true in theory but also in practice – for instance, NFT marketplace OpenSea charges 2.5%.

4/ Decentralized Social Networks
Problem: Biased operators can decide the rules and reach of social networks, or even deplatform individual users. Developers are scared to build new products and experiences on top of these networks, since they can be shut down at any time.

Solution: Decentralized social networks cannot unilaterally make decisions or change their rules for users or developers. These open protocols allow users to choose among apps while letting them easily move (and own) their data.

5/ Video Games -> Metaverse
Problem: Gaming companies are incentivized to build walled gardens that trap users inside their ecosystem. The games are created to maximize profits, at the expense of players, leading to fragmented closed economies.

Solution: Digital items and reputation earned in video games are owned by the players and can be ported outside of the game, leading to more robust economies and experiences.

6/ Decentralized Physical Infrastructure Networks (DePIN)
Problem: Physical resources – like GPUs, storage, or telecom infrastructure – are scarce and therefore end up being costly for consumers. A significant amount of idle resources are wasted each day.

Solution: Using blockchain networks to coordinate a broader set of participants, we can unlock additional resources and democratize access to them.

7/ New Governance Structures
Problem: Legacy organizations, such as corporations, nonprofits, and local governments, are often highly inefficient when it comes to their governance.

Solution: Using blockchains, large groups of people can now coordinate trustlessly. This allows us to experiment with new token and reputation-based governance designs.

8/ AI Democratization and Transparency
Problem: We must rely on Big Tech companies, who have access to the most compute resources and data, to safely develop AI technology in a black box.

Solution: We can democratize AI innovation by using blockchain networks and crypto technology to decentralize access to key resources, while also increasing transparency and auditability. Moreover, consumers can own the data they (already) contribute, and get rewarded for it.
[2024-04-19] (x) @mdudas Yes, but you need a regulatory pathway to expand it beyond a toy

[2024-04-19] (x) @Marc_Fagel @Andrew___Baker @skominers Come on, Marc. 

They just lost definitively on Coinbase wallet and couldn’t even convince the judge in Ripple that their legal theory on primary token sales was mostly right. The current administration hasn’t won a single difficult case - e.g., something other than an ICO. 

Thus far, their enforcement strategy has been completely ineffective. ICOs don’t really happen anymore and the SEC hasn’t brought or won a single case that has a fact pattern applicable to “most” projects, yet Gensler says they’re all offsides. Meanwhile, they waste countless hours investigating these good actors while allowing  scams and frauds to go unpunished. 

What effect does that have? I’ve been advising companies in this space for 7 years and the number one thing I have to explain to founders is “government inaction isn’t the same thing as consent.” If the rules were sufficiently clear, government inaction would be a lot less problematic. But as it stands, bad actors are emboldened. 

Ultimately, we’d all be a lot better off if they had engaged in constructive rule making instead of playing politics.

It’s not the market’s job to regulate itself. The market doesn’t have a mission, the SEC does and its current approach is failing to meets its mission on every account.

[2024-04-22] (x) @marginxsafety @zooko Agreed. Playing the game on the same terms as everyone else doesn't undermine a principled view. Not playing the game, but owning a stake in the casino and then expressing the virtues of gambling does.
[2024-04-22] (x) @harryhendrix27 Agreed. I think regulatory clarity would resolve a lot. A lack of regulatory clarity blurs the lines between good actors and bad actors, and acts as a significant impediment to good projects developing and market efficiency.
[2024-04-22] (x) @panekkkk Eh. If you think the whole industry is a casino, then yes. If you think it has more to offer than speculation, then no. Talking ones bags isn’t necessarily the same as promoting activity that’s seriously damaging the industry’s long term chances of success for short term profits.
[2024-04-22] (x) @CloutedMind @eawosikaa @a16zcrypto @VitalikButerin I very much agree with this as well. 

My running thesis is that capital will seek out opportunities regardless of whether or not they're productive, and that the current market trend towards unproductive is the result of a combination of there being (1) a lack of productive use cases and (2) distrust of productive use cases.

One could argue that responsibility for both of these issues rests with any number of different market participants (investors, entrepreneurs, influencers, etc.). However, it's pretty clear to me that this is driven by the fact that the market is oriented towards short-term incentives. I think that's the natural orientation for improperly regulated markets, so I think ultimate responsibility here rests with the SEC.

If there were clear rules of the road that permitted companies to build decentralized protocols and there was consistent enforcement against projects, then the orientation of the market could be turned towards the long-term as it is comparably in equities.

As it currently stands, bad actors take advantage of the lack of clarity, and the lack of swift enforcement means that outsiders have difficulty telling the difference between good actors and bad actors.  So, now we are where we are.
[2024-04-22] (x) @eawosikaa @CloutedMind @a16zcrypto Agreed. There's been no shortage of bad actors in each cycle (ICOs, DeFi, NFTs, etc). The rampant speculation fueled by those bad actors and resulting consumer harm was bad for the industry, and we're seeing the same thing on repeat now.

The new narrative being sold ("decentralization from the ground up") isn't actually a new one. The only real difference is there now isn't any pretense of actual underlying value (there's no protocol, there's no art, there's no games). That means the frauds no longer seek to entice through promises of present or future value, they seek to entice through hopes of memetic value.

That's a purely speculative game and people should be free to play it if they wish. But it's no substitute for the productive direction the industry needs to pursue to establish blockchain technology as the new infrastructure of the internet. And the scams and frauds it produces will undeniably impede the ability of the industry to make progress in that direction (both from a tech and regulation perspective), just as the frauds have done in previous cycles. No steps forward, two steps back.

Those that are amplifying the narrative, claiming it's more ideologically aligned with the ethos of crypto and ignoring the negative externalities, while they're simultaneously profiting off of the activity are disingenuous at best.

[2024-04-27] (x) @BlockEnthusiast @JWVerret @lex_node Not sure where to start. There are lots of valid views on how to regulate blockchain tech. 

Some believe no regulation should be applied anywhere. That's fine, but I think it's unlikely and also ignores the fact that the technology creates risks for consumers.

I for one think an effective regulatory regime can be built around centralized apps. 

Some protest that approach on the basis that it has loopholes. That's undoubtedly true, but it's also true that regulatory regimes typically aren't designed to eliminate the possibility of the activity it seeks to curtail, they mostly try to prevent it where it impacts the most users, and they try to make it so that such activity isn't incentivized. 

The RANP framework achieves those goals, but is no means perfect. However:

 - despite your allegations, it strongly argues against regulating the base layer:

 - despite your allegations, it strongly argues against KYC.

- despite your allegations, it strongly argues against regulating developers.

If I were to attempt it, I'd add controls to my app/client that truly blocked sanctioned countries/addresses, and I'd engage in transaction monitoring. [2024-04-29] (x) @adamscochran Agree with you. 

This risk factor is meaningless. Securities laws incentivize companies to disclose every conceivable risk, not just risks they think are likely.

As Howey is a transaction based test, every transaction of a digital asset could conceivably be a securities transaction. As a result, it would be foolish to foreclose the possibility of ETH transactions from being subject to securities laws in the future. 

The inclusion of this risk factor is more an indictment of the absurd lack of clarity than it is an acknowledgment that there was real risk the SEC would view ETH as a security.
[2024-04-29] (x) @poem_as @ckc12_rb I don't know. Speculation about FSD have been around for a while.

[2024-04-29] (x) (7/7) To sum it up, while the FOMO of token launches can be alluring, moving too fast is a mistake. 

There are no one-size-fits-all solutions, so take your time and give your token the launch it deserves.

See our post for more ways to get ready: https://t.co/V2SoOhOliu
[2024-04-29] (x) 6/🏃‍♀️Operational Readiness:

It’s easy to skip the “how” in the rush to ship a token. Get ahead of operational to-do’s, from funding and custodians to employee incentives to communications strategies.

Putting all of these mechanisms in motion can take longer than you’d expect:
[2024-04-29] (x) 5/🏛️ Organizational Structure:

Your org structure will have a significant influence over your project’s ultimate success, and define how it operates well into the future. 

There’s no one sized fits all approach, and planning can take months. Get started early.
[2024-04-29] (x) 4/💹 Token Economic Model:

Tokens aren't magic beans. Projects need a sustainable model grounded in unit economics.

While network growth should be a key focus in early innings, there’s a cost to token incentives. Eventually tokens need to accrue value.

https://t.co/CA2qANqn0I
[2024-04-29] (x) 3/🎇 Plan of Decentralization:

True decentralization is a journey, not a destination. 

Before launching, have a plan that maps out how your project will become more decentralized over time. 

Start here:
https://t.co/ULIwUHX1UC
[2024-04-29] (x) 2/🎯 Product-Market Fit:

PMF is the most important focus for any new project or product.

Tokens don’t fix product issues – they may attract users, but they won’t make them stick around. And fixing product issues post-launch is exceedingly complex.

Here are some PMF strategies:

[2024-05-05] (x) @NicolasRamsrud @a16zcrypto @samrags_ @moodlezoup Yes, you have to make sure the compiler from a high-level language down to bytecode for the VM is correct, in addition to the SNARK to prove that the bytecode was executed correctly. 

If the compiler and VM are widely used outside of the SNARK community and hence subjected to scrutiny and verification efforts, this is a much better situation than a new compiler targeting a "SNARK-friendly" VM, or developers writing optimized constraint systems for every new functionality they want to apply a SNARK to, or other things in between (e.g., developers writing in new zkDSLs that are higher-level than circuits but lower-level than something like Rust).

In general, if a programming language can be "directly SNARKed with no compiler", I'd expect it to be lower level than something like Rust. So the lack of a compiler does not mean that exposing that language to developers is a strict improvement over the Rust-->VM-bytecode approach.

[2024-05-14] (x) This is incredibly important. The crypto industry has a real chance to get the regulatory clarity we deserve.
[2024-05-14] (x) New blog post based on my keynote from the first Columbia Cryptoeconomics Workshop (original video at https://t.co/kK6kSWgkSI)
[2024-05-14] (x) In the next two weeks, the House of Representatives will vote on the most important piece of crypto legislation to date: FIT21. We’ve long called for regulatory clarity that protects consumers and innovation and the FIT21 Act will do just that.

Americans have embraced digital assets, but the current regulatory approach too often limits innovation and privacy without actually addressing the solutions needed to protect consumers or tackle illicit activity.

FIT21 will help stamp out scams, ensure oversight of crypto exchanges, and protect American consumers by implementing strict rules on crypto trading. FIT21 has bipartisan support because it tackles these issues.

I encourage everyone who believes in the power of blockchain technology to support this legislation. The best thing to do is get in touch with your local representative and tell them to support FIT21.

@standwithcrypto offers helpful resources to find and contact your local representative here: https://t.co/nNf9dlyVao

[2024-05-16] (x) @AFDudley0 You could stick with two rounds and handle that type of attack through social consensus, but with three rounds you can literally prevent it from ever happening (with a &lt;67% attacker)

[2024-05-16] (x) Link to the full paper: https://t.co/5HIx737eGm
As always, questions and comments welcome!

[2024-05-16] (x) Comparison between our protocol and the Ethereum protocol:
24/25 https://t.co/COPQBeflaH
[2024-05-16] (x) We address the third challenge by having honest players attempt to reach consensus on an updated genesis block (in which slashing has been carried out) using a variant of the Dolev-Strong protocol
23/25 https://t.co/rkWhhhVK6j
[2024-05-16] (x) We address the second challenge by adding a third stage of voting in each view (we prove that the usual two stages are inadequate)
22/25 https://t.co/6ub8g2Mfw1
[2024-05-16] (x) We address the first challenge by starting from a PBFT-style protocol that is accountable, namely Tendermint (which we extend to a quasi-permissionless PoS protocol) 
21/25 https://t.co/ufwiXODLFf
[2024-05-16] (x) How does the protocol work? Achieving EAAC requires addressing three challenges: 
20/25 https://t.co/nxkHtOsXNH
[2024-05-16] (x) More generally, our protocol operates at a speed proportional to the typical message delay D (order of seconds) when there are no consistency violations and carries out slashing at a speed proportional to worst-case (perhaps out-of-band) message delay D* (order of days)
19/25 https://t.co/7uq020Iosx
[2024-05-16] (x) Answer: yes! EAAC is achievable by a PoS protocol with slashing, provided the attacker controls less than *two-thirds* of the overall stake
18/25 https://t.co/TGqu1zcs0V
[2024-05-16] (x) Recap: to get EAAC, it's necessary to work in the quasi-permissionless setting (where honest players are assumed to be online) and have some worst-case bound on message delays. Are these assumptions also sufficient?
17/25
[2024-05-16] (x) Implications for Ethereum and other PoS protocols: 
16/25 https://t.co/tWgYSJqCFT
[2024-05-16] (x) Variation of result 2: even if there *is* a worst-case bound D* on message delay (which should apply even when the protocol is under attack), to have any hope of EAAC, withdraw delays (e.g., the cooldown period after unstaking) must be proportional to D* 
15/25 https://t.co/YRQoe8WNgl
[2024-05-16] (x) In particular, this result implies that slashing in a proof-of-stake protocol cannot achieve its intended purpose if message delays cannot be bounded a priori 
13/25 https://t.co/YJIZSuRVb7
[2024-05-16] (x) Result 2: You can't get EAAC in the partially synchronous setting (in which the communication network may be periodically unreliable, e.g due to DoS attacks): once an adversary is big enough to cause a consistency violation, it is big enough to evade targeted punishment 
12/25 https://t.co/MJ1YmeD1ao
[2024-05-16] (x) Implications for Ethereum and other PoS protocols: 
11/25 https://t.co/GYUvftoDeA
[2024-05-16] (x) This result rules out non-trivial EAAC guarantees for all typical longest-chain protocols (be they proof-of-work protocols like Bitcoin or proof-of-stake protocols such as Ouroboros)
9/25 https://t.co/ZMXjVUk6aZ
[2024-05-16] (x) Result 1: You can't get EAAC in the dynamically available setting (in which non-malicious players might or might not be online): once an adversary is big enough to cause a consistency violation, it is big enough to evade targeted punishment 
8/25 https://t.co/w1sfsdqVRs
[2024-05-16] (x) While our focus is on foundations rather than specific protocols, we highlight below several implications for practical blockchain protocol design (e.g., making precise the common belief that the merge has increased Ethereum’s economic security) 
7/25
[2024-05-16] (x) The goal of our paper is to map out fundamental possibility and impossibility results about EAAC protocols. Which types of protocols are capable of achieving the EAAC property, and under what assumptions? 
6/25
[2024-05-16] (x) We define the EAAC property (expensive to attack in the absence of collapse) to capture this idea of carrying out targeted punishment against an attacker responsible for a consistency violation (whether by PoS slashing or other means) 
5/25
[2024-05-16] (x) For example, this is one of the primary aspirations of slashing in a proof-of-stake protocol like Ethereum 
4/25
[2024-05-16] (x) Ideally, a protocol would be able to "fight back" against an attacker that uses its power to violate the protocol's consistency (e.g., rolling back transactions to double-spend), and could do so without collateral damage to honest participants 
3/25
[2024-05-16] (x) Consider the bummer scenario in which an attacker acquires enough resources to interfere with your blockchain protocol (e.g., 51% of the hashrate of a Bitcoin-like protocol or 34% of the stake of an Ethereum-like protocol). Is it game over? 
2/25
[2024-05-16] (x) To slash or not to slash?

New paper with @ebudish @AndrewLewisPye on provable slashing guarantees (both possibility and impossibility results), details below 
1/25 https://t.co/vfgAULMsmW
[2024-05-16] (x) There’s been a long running debate about the benefits of slashing and Tim, @ebudish, and @AndrewLewisPye formalize much of it.
