[2024-05-20] (x) In sum: Create something useful and form a community around it.

While competition in web3 is more intense through the five forces, the path to value creation is less zero-sum.

In web3, competitive advantage is powered by collaboration. QED.

Full article in next link 👇
[2024-05-20] (x) Moreover, digital ownership enabled by blockchains drives users to build a personal attachment with a given brand or platform; this 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗺𝘂𝗻𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗰𝗼𝗵𝗲𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻 is a powerful source of competitive advantage.
[2024-05-20] (x) BUT THAT'S NOT ALL:

Composability in web3 enables competitive advantage through 𝗲𝗺𝗯𝗲𝗱𝗱𝗲𝗱𝗻𝗲𝘀𝘀.

If you invent a “Lego block” that many people want to build on, that block’s ubiquity can serve as a competitive advantage and enable value capture.
[2024-05-20] (x) Shared incentives can lead to a bigger pie overall, such that capturing a smaller share can still lead to a bigger slice than in the traditional web platform model. 🥧
[2024-05-20] (x) As I explained in a @HarvardBiz article with @Jad_AE, shared ownership has a multiplier effect in terms of value creation because it incentivizes users to contribute. https://t.co/98IMXGJQBD
[2024-05-20] (x) The same aspects that make competing in web3 challenging are also its greatest features – they offer an opportunity to grow the pie, and introduce novel sources of competitive advantage that dovetail with the open and decentralized nature of the technology. 😎
[2024-05-20] (x) But then why would anyone want to build in Web3?? 😕

Read on! 🧵👇
[2024-05-20] (x) User ownership of data – combined with wallet portability across platforms – can greatly enhance stakeholder bargaining power (forces 4️⃣ &amp; 5️⃣).

All of this puts immense pressure on platforms to deliver value – and can make value capture more difficult.
[2024-05-20] (x) Public blockchains make it easier for new firms to enter a given market (force 2️⃣ above), and intensify within-market competition (force 1️⃣).

Composability and the possibility of protocol forks both strengthen the threat of substitutes (force 3️⃣).
[2024-05-20] (x) Traditionally, platforms captured value based on a couple sources of competitive advantage.

Web3 flips this model on its head – web3 competition can be substantially more intense along all these dimensions.

😱
[2024-05-20] (x) Tactically, the Five Forces framework helps us identify and think about 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗲𝘁𝗶𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝗮𝗱𝘃𝗮𝗻𝘁𝗮𝗴𝗲𝘀.

E.g.:
🔹️ How do we differentiate?
🔹️ Is our advantage sustainable?
🔹️ Where should we focus?
[2024-05-20] (x) The first three forces reflect competition across the industry, and the other two reflect competition up and down the value chain.

Together, they help us understand how defensible a firm's position may be – and how much value that firm can capture.
[2024-05-20] (x) 🧵👇
Back in 1979, Michael Porter identified five “forces” that drive an industry’s competitive dynamics:

1️⃣ Existing firm rivalry
2️⃣ Threat of new entrants
3️⃣ Threat of substitute products or services
4️⃣ Supplier bargaining power
5️⃣ Customer bargaining power https://t.co/NgvcLPGj4w
[2024-05-20] (x) Classic business logic presents industry competition as a tug-of-war. But web3 competition is, paradoxically, powered by collaboration.

@WhatTheLJW and I explain in a new @a16zcrypto article, building on a timeless competitive strategy framework called the Five Forces:

🧵👇 https://t.co/eLALIQUgL2

[2024-05-23] (x) @david_r_barrera It’s not a city planning department doling out permits piecemeal.
[2024-05-23] (x) @samcallah If you were to be consistent in your legal reasoning, then the answer is yes. 

Decentralization mitigates the risks that securities laws are intended to address, thereby obviating the need to apply securities laws. 

If a blockchain were to become more centralized such that those risks were reintroduced, then that would point towards reapplying securities laws. 

The fact that a project could waffle back and forth is problematic from a legal perspective though as it makes it very difficult to get certainty as to treatment of an asset under the law. That’s one of the reasons that people advocate for statutory solutions. Securities laws just don’t handle morphing very well or efficiently.
[2024-05-23] (x) @david_r_barrera It would be pretty embarrassing for the SEC to be so disjointed and disorganized that it would fumble an approval process by approving the listing of a product and then subsequently denying the effectiveness of the registration statement.

[2024-05-23] (x) @bramk @mdudas It doesn’t matter how it began, it matters where it got to, which is a decentralized blockchain that isn’t controlled by anyone and that isn’t dependent on the managerial efforts of anyone. Applying securities laws to such a system doesn’t make sense.
[2024-05-23] (x) These approvals enshrine the SEC’s position in 2018 - ETH is not a security.  

That means the SEC has formally recognized that decentralization moves tokens outside of the US securities law regime.

With that beachhead established, all projects now have a path forward.
[2024-05-23] (x) @itaisher @ben_golub Why? I think match frictions are first-order in this market -- e.g. it's whybthe signaling system seems to have been helpful (https://t.co/CLbfn0oOtt). Interviews are extremely costly, which forces programs to interview a relatively narrow number of candidates, and mismatch in the interview pool could lead to not allocating a slot that would otherwise exist.
[2024-05-23] (x) @paulnovosad For some reason Twitter wouldn't let me post this link with the analysis on the signaling mechanism in the same post, but here it is: https://t.co/ZokvhgIxza
[2024-05-23] (x) @paulnovosad Unfortunately I think there are a lot of challenges to implementing a scheme like this in practice, including the issue that determining rank preferences over the full set of departments a candidate might apply to is very difficult. (And presumably for departments they would have to implement combinatorial preference functions because the number of interviews may not be fixed and instead could depend on which set of candidates end up accepting interviews.)

But I think the signaling system the AEA implemented is a pretty good step towards addressing the interview match friction problem. Maybe it should be expanded?
[2024-05-23] (x) @paulnovosad Really excellent thread! 

One point on how awareness could be driving the effect that's less zero-sum: it seems plausible to me that the same frictions you describe in reviewing applications might cause departments to miss potentially high-quality idiosyncratic matches -- people who would be especially strong candidates but get missed at the interview selection stage. It's possible that increased awareness of candidates improves the allocation of interviews in a way that leads to better matching with more total offers down the line. https://t.co/4c9Vx8efdI

[2024-05-24] (x) @paulnovosad Oh also one final note on this: the stratification you describe with correlated preferences across tiers and idiosyncratic preferences within tiers is precisely why we think the signaling mechanism might work well in this context -- if University X is generally perceived to be in a tier above University Y, then Y expects not to be able to hire a candidate who gets an offer from X. BUT Y might still want to interview that candidate if they know the candidate has some idiosyncratic reason to prefer them if they don't happen to get a job from X. In equilibrium, signaling enables candidates to convey exactly this sort of information.

By contrast, the interview deferred acceptance mechanism you're describing might result in the candidate getting only interviews from the X tier, which actually isn't optimal in this case because Y would still like to interview them.
[2024-05-24] (x) @paulnovosad (Also note there are similar issues if schools don't do a very careful job figuring out their preferences before submitting them to the mechanism.

If a school's initial preferences are "rough," they might end up with a list of 20 candidates who on further review they decide not to interview, but now the entire market in effect has a signal suggesting that those candidates *are* going to be interviewed.

Or conversely on further review they discover those candidates seem to be less good fits than they thought ex ante, but they go ahead with interviewing them anyway because they figure that other candidates are going to be congested away from them since they did not show up on their list. This could lead to departments in the end having a worse interview set and eventually making offers.
[2024-05-24] (x) @paulnovosad Unfortunately, I don't think it solves the combinatorial problem because preferences aren't uniform rankings and might reflect complementaries. Even implementing deferred acceptance in such a situation is complicated (and stable matchings don't always exist, although I think they might in this instance leveraging a method by Yenmez: https://t.co/oAMfxWCAOn).

That said, I actually think there's a bigger problem with what you're describing, which is that providing information like that to the market could actually make coordination worse rather than better if it's not clear how it's being used by the various departments.

For example: suppose some candidate shows up on the list of their top 20 departments, and as a result does not show up on the list of any of the other schools -- but those 20 departments (or some large fraction of them) end up deciding to ignore the output of this mechanism and choose not to interview them. Now other schools *also* might intentionally choose not to interview them because they didn't show up on their lists, and the candidate ends up falling through the cracks because of an equilibrium coordination problem.

(A similar problem sometimes happens in the current market whereby schools over-index on information signals sent by departments about their expectations of candidates' outcomes. But here the idea that the information reflects candidate preferences as well is likely to make that market failure even worse.)
[2024-05-24] (x) @paulnovosad My instinct is that in practice this issue can be resolved by candidates turning down excess interviews and departments sending messages saying versions of "we would like to interview you if you think there's a realistic chance you might end up coming, but we expect you'll have better offers," which does happen in the economics job market already to some degree. (Structuring the communication around that to make it credible and uniform could be very helpful.) This would resolve the uncertainty you're talking about locally for a given candidate rather than requiring full ex ante preference rankings....
[2024-05-24] (x) @paulnovosad Also in the economics job market, even if preferences are ec ante rather flat (again which I'm not sure is true -- I think the idiosyncratic match component is pretty significant), they're ex post very heterogeneous and the stakes are very high. So in the format you describe, candidates might have to undergo massive effort to sort out the ex post heterogeneity ex ante (again, including for schools they aren't going to end up interviewed by).
[2024-05-24] (x) @paulnovosad Preference formation in the residency match is hard as well, and in practice these days very expensive for doctors (and there's been a lot of complaint about that). But even so there's a very significant difference between the residency match and what you're proposing, which is that in the residency match preference formation happens after the interview set is selected, so (generally) doctors only rank hospitals they've interviewed with and hospitals only rank doctors they've interviewed. That means you're forming strict preferences over say 15-30 institutions -- most/all of which being places at which you might seriously end up employed in equilibrium -- rather than having to ex ante rank hundreds of departments about which you have relatively little differentiating information.
[2024-05-24] (x) @paulnovosad On number of signals: given that people file hundreds of applications, it's not clear that sending (say) double the number of signals would degrade information substantially relative to providing people with an additional opportunity to truthfully indicate idiosyncratic interest. For example, if you have idiosyncratic interest in living in Maryland, there are definitely more than two schools you might want to communicate that to credibly.
[2024-05-24] (x) @paulnovosad Unfortunately deferred acceptance is not strategy proof (in general) for both sides of the market, nor for any side that has more than unit demand preferences. 

But precisely the reason you indicate is part of the reason it's not clear how much deferred acceptance would add relative to signaling here -- if everyone truly ranks MIT as one of their top choices, the impact of deferred acceptance only kicks in at the margin where preference heterogeneity shows up, which is precisely where the signaling mechanism also adds value....
[2024-05-24] (x) @alive_ I have plenty of friends who own lathes, fwiw. Coincidentally, I have even more friends who use consumer AI products 🤔🤔🤔
[2024-05-25] (x) He who controls the past controls the future. 

He who controls the present controls the past.

- George Orwell, 1984
[2024-05-25] (x) @lex_node I think all of these raise some really good questions and agree the bill could be improved. 

Few thoughts:

1. DeFi/Derivatives - On your first and last post, I agree the bill doesn't really address the CFTC's authority with respect to crypto derivatives products. I don't think it expands it, but it does mostly leave the current state of play in place. That's not ideal given the spate of enforcement. 

The expansion of the CFTC's authority appears to be mostly focused on digital commodity spot markets, and I don't read that authority to subject DEXs to the same requirements as centralized exchanges, which I think is a win. Do you agree?

Obviously, it'd be great if there was a pathway for digital commodity derivatives, particularly those offered by DeFi platforms, as the disintermediation and programmatic functioning of DeFi solves a lot of the risks the CEA is intended to address when it comes to derivatives, but isn't that kind of an entirely separate regime/focus? I'm not sure I'd condition improving our SEC situation on also getting clarity for derivatives, though I think reasonable minds can disagree and I think a bill that protects all DeFi would be great.

2. Decentralized Governance System - On your second post, I can't say I know what a "commodity pool operator" even is, but my read of this is that they're carving out DAOs of DEXs as opposed to DAOs like Ooki that were operating a frontend that was violating the CEA because it facilitated derivatives trading. This goes to the same point above. I don't think the DAO of a DEX would be scoped into the regime merely for administering and maintaining the affairs of a DEX. Right?

3. Tokens vs. Securities - On your third post, I 100% agree that the definition of digital assets is narrowly tailored, meaning that the bill really only provides a pathway out of securities laws for tokens that don't look like traditional securities, but that the SEC has tried to subject to securities laws under Howey. Think that's the majority of assets in the space though. Also agree that it'd be better to get regulatory clarity for things like stablecoins and digital assets that might be notes or profit participation interests. 

Seems like it'd be easy enough to scope those in, but maybe they'd need a different test than the "decentralized network" and "functional network" tests? 

I also think there's a major concern about not creating massive loopholes to securities laws, where any financial instrument can escape security status just because it's digitized. It's hard for me to argue with that, as otherwise the bill would be the end of securities laws (which I appreciate some people may prefer, but I don't think is realistic).

- network effects (consumer) or

Bridging, shared sequencing, proof aggregation and other ideas are essentially creating cross shard transactions after the fact imo
[2024-06-02] (x) @nassyweazy 1/ scaling, I think the appchain thesis is basically implicit sharding where you figure out how to best connect the most used shards after deploying

2/ customizability, in limited use cases it’s very helpful to customize an appchain but it’s usually for performance

[2024-06-03] (x) Finally, as a reminder, none of this is business or investment advice. For important disclosures, see https://t.co/AbrQodDuEV
[2024-06-03] (x) 6/ Check out the post for details https://t.co/a6re9kb4B2. I will address additional topics as we continue to improve Jolt.
[2024-06-03] (x) 5/ The role of EVM precompiles in zkEVMs differs from the role of precompiles in zkVMs. Benchmarking SNARKs involves complexities, but it’s crucial to understand their innate performance characteristics.
[2024-06-03] (x) 4/ What sets Jolt apart? It’s *both* its exclusive use of sum-check-based PIOPs and its maximal use of lookups. Simplicity comes from lookups; performance from both.
[2024-06-03] (x) 3/ I hope the community adopts sum-check-based PIOPs combined with the Binius commitment for performant SNARKs. However, fast curve-based SNARKs (like the initial version of Jolt) will remain vital, especially for proving statements like signature verification and Verkle trees.
[2024-06-03] (x) 2/ Clarification: Only sum-check-based PIOPs can use multilinear polynomial commitment schemes. Jolt, the only zkVM today using sum-check exclusively, is uniquely positioned to leverage the Binius multilinear commitment. More details in the post: https://t.co/a6re9kb4B2
[2024-06-03] (x) 1/ My latest blog post tackles key topics following Jolt's release: the unique benefits of the sum-check protocol, the impact of lookups on performance and simplicity, and nuances of benchmarking.
[2024-06-03] (x) @KyleSamani Agree that in protocol sharding at the L1 is a more seamless UX. Rollups give developers a lot more space to customize, or deploy at a faster pace than an L1 has been able to implement sharding. 

Rollups with a coordination layer like shared sequencing, proof aggregation, restaking, mesh security, etc could have a similarly strong UX overtime imo
[2024-06-03] (x) @Melt_Dem @witnesschain Yes, I think they've done some great work on decentralized verification 

https://t.co/S1F5CkAKZk
[2024-06-03] (x) @KyleSamani I think DePIN protocols are more than that. They are a way to build user owned and operated physical businesses. 

There aren't P2P telecom networks, electricity networks, or transportation networks yet. 

Agree you need verification to combat sybils, but centrally controlling hardware means the network isn't user owned and operated to the same extent. 

That's why I think decentralized verification is valuable
[2024-06-03] (x) What are DePINs? 

They form consensus on physical state. 

Any computational state change should be trustless, and proven with a validity or ZK proof. 

But anything touching the physical world can't be computationally proven, hence the need for DePIN verification
[2024-06-03] (x) onchain: noun 

1. refers to any activity, transaction, or operation that occurs directly on a blockchain network

eg. it's about to be an onchain summer

[2024-06-05] (x) Why do shared sequencers matter? 

1. Decentralizing the sequencer. We don't have any decentralized sequencer, and shared sequencing networks could be first 
2. Atomic composability. Transactions across protocols on different rollups that feel like the same chain

[2024-06-08] (x) I hope you've found this thread helpful.

Follow me @guywuolletjr for more.

Like/Repost the quote below if you can:
[2024-06-08] (x) Disclosure: None of the above should be taken as investment advice or an advertisement for investment services; please see https://t.co/fCuRybV0ZA for more information.
[2024-06-08] (x) Read more here from @lera_banda and @danboneh on our fantastic research team: https://t.co/NYZczXgv39
[2024-06-08] (x) Non-native DA is clearly required to scale Ethereum to its full potential in the short term. Non-native DA comes with security trade offs, but that’s a topic for another thread.
[2024-06-08] (x) Ethereum also has planned full Danksharding which could increase the total native data availability to ~30 MB/s. Danksharding is likely several years away and non-native DA layers are already here.
[2024-06-08] (x) Non-native DA layers have set 1GB per second of DA throughput as their ultimate goal. This would mean ~80M TPS, or more than enough throughput to scale blockchains for all current use cases.
[2024-06-08] (x) Enter non-native data availability like @eigen_da, @EspressoSys, @CelestiaOrg.  Celestia currently targets a 2-8MB block size, with a roughly 15 second blocktime for a potential ~10-40K TPS.
[2024-06-08] (x) ~9166 TPS is not that many transactions! I’ve often heard people talk about scaling blockchains to hundreds of thousands or millions of transactions per second. For Ethereum to reach the endgame of blockchain scalability, it must have another source of data availability.
[2024-06-08] (x) Combining ~6500 rollup TPS from calldata and ~2666 rollup TPS from blobdata, Ethereum can support an average of ~9166 total transactions per second today across all rollups.
[2024-06-08] (x) Ethereum today also has blob storage from EIP-4844 and targets 3 blobs each block at 128KB in size. That’s 384 KB per block in DA, and applying the same calculation yields an additional ~2666 transactions per block
[2024-06-08] (x) At an average block time of 12 seconds, that’s ~6500 transactions per second.
[2024-06-08] (x) Assuming a single rollup occupying all blockspace, you’d get 15M/16 = 937.5k bytes of data availability. A compressed ETH transfer would require 12 bytes of data, so we’re left with 78125 transactions per block.
[2024-06-08] (x) An Ethereum block today targets an average of 15M gas per block, and each byte of non-zero transaction data costs 16 gas.
[2024-06-08] (x) If the rollup posts the data needed to update the state (2) elsewhere, such as @EspressoSys, @eigen_da, or @CelestiaOrg then the rollup is using NON-NATIVE data availability.
[2024-06-08] (x) The rollup will always post the new state root (1) on Ethereum, but can choose to post the data needed to update the state (2) on Ethereum or elsewhere.

If the rollup posts the data needed to update the state (2) on Ethereum, the rollup is using NATIVE data availability.
[2024-06-08] (x) A new state root (1) is the output of a hash function and usually fixed in size at 256 bits.

The data needed to update the prior state to the current state (2) is variable in size and can be tens of thousands of bytes or more.
[2024-06-08] (x) For each checkpoint a rollup built on Ethereum must post two types of information; 1) a new state root and 2) all data needed to update the prior state to the current state.
[2024-06-08] (x) Data availability (DA) can be either native or non-native. In the context of rollups, native DA means that a rollup doesn’t inherit any additional security assumptions.

Non-native DA means that a rollup is making an additional security assumption.
[2024-06-08] (x) Ethereum will need non-native data availability to properly scale its rollup-centric roadmap

What is non-native data availability, why does it matter, and how does it work? Let’s find out

[2024-06-11] (x) CSX demo day is here 🔥

It's easy to take the internet for granted. It works so well that we forget that it was hardly usable in the beginning. Getting to where we are now required hard work to improve every layer of the stack over the course of many years.

The state of crypto infrastructure today is similar to where the internet was in the mid-90s. We’ve gotten a glimpse of what blockchain computers will someday make possible, but we still have a lot of work to do.

It's encouraging that some of the smartest entrepreneurs we've met are doing that work for the crypto stack, going layer by layer, to address their limitations. It won't be long before we start taking crypto for granted too.

This Spring's CSX cohort includes companies that are working on new uses of ZK proofs to further unlock scalability; better technology for cross-chain composability; novel ways of connecting blockchains to the external world via secure oracles; app-specific infrastructure for crypto-enabled games, social networks, and AI applications; and many other improvements.

This is still just the beginning. LFG 💪
[2024-06-11] (x) @jasonmorton I don’t think I had to say that once during CSX because it’s already so deeply intuitive to the wonderful founders I worked with
[2024-06-11] (x) 10 truisms I’ve repeated many times at Crypto Startup Accelerator (CSX) in London.

Internalize them or learn these lessons the hard way.

1. Build a decentralized protocol with a token. The largest opportunities in crypto involve building new crypto networks.

2. Start simple. Left curve ideas with right curve execution are often the best.

3. Get more famous. The best evangelist for a new startup is the founder themself. Even, and especially, if you're an introverted engineer you need to be proselytizing and spreading the good word of your startup. Building a successful crypto project is often akin to building a successful cult.

4. Be important. Don't let high status people push you around, or lowball you with bad offers. Prioritize working with high performance and high integrity people, and know your own value.

5. Set deadlines, and communicate them clearly. You will not ship faster than your deadlines, and speed kills. Deadlines should be aggressive and push other people on your team.

6. Attack a definite future now. Hedging into multiple potential futures means none will materialize. You can always change direction in the future, but you must be focused in the short term.

7. Don't let who you are get in the way of who you could become. Many of the best startups were born out of a pivot. Don't let emotional attachment to old ideas or approaches hold you back from an obviously better future.

8. Always be hiring. Start by trying to hire all of the best people you've ever worked with, and be relentless about pursuing them.

9. Be a happy warrior. Never put down other projects or speak negatively of competitors, but be a prolific force for positivity about your own project. You should be judo throwing every other narrative to explain why it validates your own approach.

10. Shamelessly beg for distribution. Every announcement, every product launch, every post — directly ask your community, investors, peers, and customers to evangelize.

In that spirit, RT this post so future founders will apply to CSX, and follow me for more updates on our accelerator!

[2024-06-18] (x) Libraries vs. networks 

Libraries are open source code basis that do a single player task. They’re tools, but don’t accumulate network effects 

Networks are multiplayer ecosystems, and create lock in.

If you’re building in crypto, build a network

[2024-06-19] (x) @stanveuger @nomadj1s @florianederer Note that the key difference that gets away from the "single rationing price is never optimal" result is that the weight on revenue can be below the welfare weight on the targeted group. In our original paper that can never happen because revenue is redistributed as a lump sum transfer....
[2024-06-19] (x) @stanveuger @nomadj1s @florianederer I think a version of that logic is right! In a more recent paper, we look at the one-sided allocation problem with heterogeneous welfare weights and an explicit weight on revenue, which maps even more closely to the question here. (We actually mentioned National Park permits as one potential application 😁.)

The result is that you *do* sometimes want to give away the good for free, and in contexts like this, that happens when (1) the statistical correlation of willingness to pay is inversely related to the welfare weights [i.e. low willingness to pay signals *more* social welfare weight on the margin], and (2) the weight on revenue is low relative to the weight you place on the group you're trying to help through the policy.

So if for example there were no way to leverage revenue from park permits in a way that generates social value for the targeted group (here probably lower-income individuals?), we might in fact want free allocation. But if, for example, the revenue funds public services that they benefit from, we might still want a price.

(h/t Piotr for cross-checking some of my logic here)

[2024-06-20] (x) @stanveuger @nomadj1s @florianederer Ohhh sorry, I misunderstood. Yes, that's right -- you might want to raise revenue, including potentially via tax, to redistribute to the worst-off.
[2024-06-20] (x) @stanveuger @nomadj1s @florianederer I'm not completely sure I understand the question is stated, but if by tax rate you mean price, then I think the answer is yes. You might have a positive price but with a subsidy that pushes the price below market-clearing if you still care about assortative matching within the group -- for example if higher willingness to pay on the margin indicates a significantly greater degree of need.

[2024-06-21] (x) Here's a new definition for DePIN. 

DePIN projects are crypto networks that can't be computationally verified. 

Anything that's purely computational should use consensus or validity/ZK proofs. 

Physical services like transportation or energy are verified through other means

[2024-06-29] (x) Apropos of nothing in particular: if you're going to use cryptic/puzzly messaging as part of a brand activation, it works much better if the encoding scheme is coherent in context. 

You wouldn't for example use Hittite cuneiform script in the context of an alien space opera set in some other universe (at least not without some really complex backstory). Likewise, no one in practice would ever write out Morse code on a television screen.
